title: feat: replace hip-python and AMDSymmetricMemory with new SymmetricMemory
body:
## Why custom `SymmetricMemory`  ?

### API path comparison

The torch-native [`CUDASymmetricMemory`](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/358117c166b75167a09bca81ac9925940feda339/torch/csrc/distributed/c10d/symm_mem/CUDASymmetricMemory.cu#L435) on ROCm uses the **HIP VMM** path:

```
hipMemCreate(hipMemAllocationTypePinned)
  → hipMemExportToShareableHandle (POSIX FD)
  → hipMemImportFromShareableHandle
  → hipMemAddressReserve → hipMemMap → hipMemSetAccess
```

Our custom implementation uses the **HIP IPC** path:

```
hipMalloc
  → hipIpcGetMemHandle → all_gather_object → hipIpcOpenMemHandle
```

`hipIpcOpenMemHandle` directly creates a hardware-level XGMI peer mapping via the KFD driver in a single call. The VMM path goes through the DMA-BUF subsystem and a more complex virtual address management layer, which on current ROCm may not produce equally optimized XGMI peer mappings.

### Potential bug in torch ROCm path

The [`map_block`](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/358117c166b75167a09bca81ac9925940feda339/torch/csrc/distributed/c10d/symm_mem/CUDASymmetricMemoryUtils.cpp) ROCm implementation calls `hipMemMap` **twice** on the same address — a copy-paste bug (the CUDA path has a single `cuMemMap` call):

```cpp
C10_CUDA_CHECK(hipMemAddressReserve(ptr, size, 0ULL, 0, 0ULL));
C10_CUDA_CHECK(hipMemMap(*ptr, size, 0, handle, 0ULL));
C10_CUDA_CHECK(hipMemMap(*ptr, size, 0, handle, 0ULL));  // ← duplicate
```

This may cause the driver to re-establish the mapping with degraded page-table attributes.

### Benchmark

Intranode allgather on 8× MI300X, running the **same Triton kernel** on both backends:

```
python benchmark/ops/bench_symmetric_memory.py
```

| nbytes | Backend | Latency (max rank) | BW (min rank) | Speedup |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 MB | `symm_mem.py` | 0.044 ms | 191.5 GB/s | **7.4×** |
| 1 MB | `torch._symmetric_memory` | 0.325 ms | 25.8 GB/s | — |
| 2 MB | `symm_mem.py` | 0.055 ms | 306.6 GB/s | **12.4×** |
| 2 MB | `torch._symmetric_memory` | 0.679 ms | 24.7 GB/s | — |
| 4 MB | `symm_mem.py` | 0.113 ms | 297.2 GB/s | **11.8×** |
| 4 MB | `torch._symmetric_memory` | 1.338 ms | 25.1 GB/s | — |

The custom implementation is **7–12× faster** on the same workload, with all correctness checks passing.

title: Replace SWIG XML with libclang for message payload struct parsing
body: * **Tickets addressed:** Closes #1342 * **Review:** By commit * **Merge strategy:** Merge (no squash) ## Description Replaces the SWIG `-xml` step with a `libclang`-based parser (`generatePayloadMetaJson.py`) that produces a JSON description of each `*Payload.h` struct. `generateSWIGModules.py` consumes this JSON to emit `RECORDER_PROPERTY_*` macros as before, but now with accurate C-level type metadata (exact sizes, enum values, array shapes, alignment/padding) instead of relying on SWIG XML internals. The richer metadata also enables two improvements to the generated SWIG bindings for each payload class: - A typed, keyword-only `__init__` with per-field type annotations and docstrings sourced directly from the header comments, replacing the generic `**kwargs` constructor. - Typed `@property` descriptors and a `__fields__` classmethod. These improvements allow for nicer IDE support around payloads: - When constructing a payload, we get all the fields it has, valid inputs for them, and a docstring with the description of the fields: - We get a red squiggly line (and a pop up with what's wrong) if we use the wrong type: - The IDE will show you the description of a field if you hover over it: -------- Commits are organized as: (1) new libclang parser + tests, (2) refactor `generateSWIGModules.py` and update the SWIG templates, (3) wire the new pipeline into CMake. `libclang` (via `pip install libclang`) is added as a dev dependency. The parser bundles minimal stub headers so no system Clang installation is required. ## Verification Unit tests were added in `test_generatePayloadMetaJson.py` covering primitives, multi-word C types, type aliases, 1-D/2-D arrays, enums, pointers, nested structs, incomplete types, and C vs C++ parsing modes. A full CMake build was run to confirm all existing message targets produce identical `RECORDER_PROPERTY_*` output. ## Documentation No existing documentation is invalidated. ## Future work If/when merged, @E-Krantz might consider using the newly generated `json` files in his very cool bsk-ros workflow.

title: Upgrade to latest swig 4.1.1
body:
* **Tickets addressed:** bsk-1201
* **Review:** By commit
* **Merge strategy:** Merge (no squash)

## Description

This PR updates Basilisk test and build infrastructure for cleaner Python 3.14 support.

First, the branch updates SWIG dependency handling so Basilisk supports the latest working SWIG release.  Local development requirements now allow current SWIG versions while explicitly excluding `swig==4.4.0`, which produced compile failures in testing.  SWIG 4.4.1 was verified with a clean Basilisk build and unit test run.  The canary CI requirements continue to use the latest available SWIG so future compatibility issues are detected early.

Second, this PR removes obsolete SWIG runtime warning suppression that was previously hiding Python warnings such as `swigvarlink has no __module__ attribute`.  With SWIG 4.4.1, those warnings no longer appear in the clean build/test path, so the suppression is no longer needed.

Finally, this PR cleans up Python test-suite `ResourceWarning` messages observed when running `pytest -n auto` from `src`.  The warnings were traced to pytest infrastructure rather than Basilisk simulation code.  The SPICE thread-safety test now closes multiprocessing queue and process resources explicitly.  The pytest configuration also closes the optional `reportconf.py` file handle after loading it.

The remaining socket warnings were isolated to the interaction between `pytest-xdist` and `pytest-rerunfailures` 16.1.  Since there is not yet a newer released `pytest-rerunfailures` version that fixes this cleanup path, `src/conftest.py` now performs a narrow local cleanup: it closes the plugin-owned rerun status socket during pytest shutdown and wraps the plugin’s server-side connection handler so accepted sockets are closed when each handler exits.

The commits are organized by infrastructure topic: SWIG version support, removal of obsolete SWIG warning suppression, SPICE multiprocessing cleanup, pytest file-handle cleanup, and pytest-rerunfailures socket cleanup.

## Verification

SWIG 4.4.1 was verified with a clean Basilisk build.  The previous SWIG runtime warnings were not observed in the clean build log, and the unit tests completed successfully.  SWIG 4.4.0 was tested separately and produced compile failures, so that specific version is excluded from the pinned development dependency range.

Validated the ResourceWarning cleanup with warning tracing enabled:

```bash
env PYTHONTRACEMALLOC=25 PYTHONWARNINGS=always::ResourceWarning .venv/bin/pytest -q src/simulation/environment/spiceInterface/_UnitTest/test_spiceThreadSafety.py
```

Also verified the full parallel test suite from src:

pytest -n auto
The full run completed without the previously reported unclosed socket ResourceWarning.

No new tests were added because these changes update build/test infrastructure cleanup behavior rather than Basilisk runtime behavior. Existing build and test coverage was used to confirm the changes.

## Documentation
No user-facing Basilisk documentation was invalidated. This change affects developer/test infrastructure and SWIG dependency compatibility.

Reviewers should check the requirements updates, the removed SWIG warning suppression, src/conftest.py for the local pytest-rerunfailures socket cleanup shim, and src/simulation/environment/spiceInterface/_UnitTest/test_spiceThreadSafety.py for multiprocessing queue/process cleanup behavior.

## Future work
If pytest-rerunfailures releases an upstream fix for closing its xdist coordination sockets, the local cleanup shim in src/conftest.py can be removed after updating the pinned dependency range.

title: Version 1.0.0 of the SDK
body:
# Description

This PR introduces the bsk-sdk package which is a self-contained Python wheel that gives external projects everything they need to build Basilisk-compatible SWIG plugins without vendoring a full Basilisk codebase / fork.

## Includes
- Curated headers:  messaging headers needed to implement a SysModel subclass
- Runtime stubs (bsk_arch_min, bsk_runtime_min) for a minimal compiled static libs needed for linking like atmosphereBase
- SWIG interface files and typemaps: `.i` files and architecture/ typemaps for wrapping custom modules
- SWIG ABI pinning:  bsk-sdk pins swig>=4.2.1,<=4.3.1 to match the version BSK is built with. SWIG 4.4.0 bumped SWIG_RUNTIME_VERSION from "4" to "5", which changes the shared type-table capsule name. Plugins compiled against the wrong epoch can't exchange objects with BSK across module boundaries. bsk_add_swig_module() detects this at cmake configure time and aborts with a clear error.
- Eigen3 bundled : headers are included in the wheel so plugin authors don't need a separate Eigen install

# CI
1.  Builds wheels across Linux (x86_64 + aarch64), macOS (arm64), and Windows.
2. Installs the built wheel and builds the included custom-atm-plugin example against it on all three platforms
3. Smoke tests verify the installed cmake config, include paths, and swig dir resolve correctly
4. Publish workflow pushes to TestPyPI on test* tags and PyPI on v* tags

title: fix: use pure WTS ctypes API for session detection (fixes #183)
body:
## Problem
QA verification found that the previous fix (dcf691b) still fails in `schtasks /it` context because `query session` returns exit code 1, causing `_is_current_session_interactive()` to bail out prematurely.

## Root Cause
`query session` returns non-zero exit codes in many valid scenarios (schtasks, some RDP configs). Parsing its text output is also fragile across locales.

## Fix
Replace the entire `query session` subprocess approach with pure ctypes calls to `WTSQuerySessionInformationW` (WTS_INFO_CLASS = WTSConnectState). This:

1. Eliminates subprocess calls — no exit code or encoding issues
2. Directly queries the WTS connect state enum for the current session
3. Considers `WTSActive` (0) and `WTSConnected` (1) as interactive states
4. Works reliably in schtasks /it, RDP, console, and service contexts

## Testing
- All 1441 tests pass (475 skipped for platform-specific)
- Needs QA verification on compile machine in schtasks /it context

Fixes #183

title: fix: use ctypes CGEventTap to eliminate PyObjC bridge event retention
body:
## Summary

- **Root cause**: PyObjC's `CGEventTapCreate` callback bridge internally retains `CGEventRef` wrappers — `leaks` confirms they're reachable (not leaked), held by the bridge's internal bookkeeping. ~42 MB (180K HIDEvent + 94K CGEvent) accumulates over 7 hours.
- **Fix**: New `wenzi/_cgeventtap.py` module provides pure ctypes bindings for `CGEventTapCreate`, `CFRunLoop`, and all `CGEvent*` functions. Callbacks receive raw `c_void_p` pointers — no PyObjC wrappers, no `CFRetain`, no retention.
- **Also**: Wrap `recognize_text()` in `objc.autorelease_pool()` to drain Vision framework objects on the OCR thread.

### Changed files
| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `_cgeventtap.py` | **New** — pure ctypes bindings (no PyObjC) |
| `hotkey.py` | 3 classes switched to ctypes CGEventTap |
| `snippet_expander.py` | Switched to ctypes CGEventTap |
| `ocr.py` | Added `autorelease_pool` |
| `test_hotkey.py` | Updated mocks for ctypes |
| `test_snippet_expander.py` | Updated for raw-pointer interface |

## Test plan

- [x] `uv run ruff check` — 0 errors
- [x] `uv run pytest tests/ -v` — 3759 passed
- [ ] Manual: run WenZi Lite for 1+ hours, `heap <pid> -s | grep HIDEvent` — count should stay near zero
- [ ] Manual: verify keyboard input, hotkeys, key remapping, snippet expansion all work

title: fix: join CGEventTap threads before releasing ctypes callbacks on reload
body:
## Summary
- During script reload, `stop()` methods cleared `_ctypes_cb` (allowing GC of the ctypes callback) before the run loop thread had fully exited
- A pending CGEventTap event arriving at the freed callback caused SIGSEGV (3 identical crashes observed today, all in `closure_fcn` on thread 9)
- Now all `stop()` methods in `hotkey.py` and `snippet_expander.py` join the thread (2s timeout) before releasing the callback reference

## Test plan

- [x] `uv run pytest tests/ -v --cov=wenzi` — 3759 passed

title: feat(approval-workflow/python): rewrite on Model B / ScenarioBundle
body:
## Summary
- Complete rewrite of `examples/approval-workflow/python/main.py` from Model A (manual orchestration) to **Model B (ScenarioBundle + server-driven workflow)**
- All 4 scenarios defined as `ScenarioBundle` dicts with agents, humans, workflow steps (`step_deadline_seconds`, `human_task`, `remind_after_seconds`, `max_reminders`)
- Client reduced to: **one `apply_scenario()` call + `observe()` event loop**
- Human step handled via `intent.human_task_assigned` event — client pauses, reads Enter, calls `resume_intent(task_result={"approved": true})`
- Full server audit trail printed at end via `list_intent_events()`
- `TIMED_OUT` treated as terminal status alongside `COMPLETED`/`FAILED`

## What the server now owns (no longer in client code)
- Agent provisioning (create_if_missing in bundle)
- Workflow DAG compilation and step transitions
- `pending_with` updates from DAG `assigned_to`
- Deadline enforcement (`deadline_at`)
- Reminder scheduling (`remind_after_seconds`, `max_reminders`)
- Per-step timeout (`step_deadline_seconds`)

## Test plan

- [ ] `SCENARIO=1 python main.py` runs without picker

title: refactor: consolidate duplicate Python helpers into pkg/tools/python/
body:
## What Changed

Five Python venv/pip orchestration helpers duplicated between the hook executor (python_executor.go) and the framework service ( ramework_service_python.go) are now shared through pkg/tools/python/. Both consumers already depended on this package, so no new dependency edges were introduced. This eliminates ~80-100 lines of duplication and ensures future changes only need to happen in one place.

## How It Works

Extracted shared logic into exported functions/methods on python.Cli: VenvNameForDir() for naming, EnsureVirtualEnv() for stat→create, InstallDependencies() for pip/pyproject dispatch, ResolveCommand() for py/python/python3 resolution, and VenvPythonPath()/VenvActivateCmd() for platform-aware venv paths. The executor's stricter implementation (with rrors.Is and directory type checks) was used as the canonical version. The pythonTools interface was simplified accordingly.

## Issue References

Fixes #7570
Relates to #7435

title: feat: implement C#/.NET hook executor
body:
## What Changed

azd now supports C#/.NET hook scripts (`.cs` files) alongside the existing Bash, PowerShell, Python, JavaScript, and TypeScript executors. .NET developers — especially those working with Aspire-based projects — can now write hook automation in C# using either a project-backed approach or single-file scripts.

Two execution modes are supported:
- **Project mode**: When a `.csproj` (or `.fsproj`/`.vbproj`) is discovered near the script via walk-up, azd automatically runs `dotnet restore` → `dotnet build` during the Prepare phase, then executes via `dotnet run --project`.
- **Single-file mode**: On .NET 10+, standalone `.cs` files run directly via `dotnet run script.cs` without needing a project file. On older SDKs, a clear error with upgrade guidance is shown.

Auto-detection works out of the box — a hook with `run: ./hooks/seed.cs` automatically selects the .NET executor. Explicit `kind: dotnet` override is also supported.

## How It Works

The implementation follows the same `HookExecutor` Prepare/Execute/Cleanup lifecycle used by all other language executors. A `dotnetTools` interface decouples the executor from the concrete `dotnet.Cli` for testability, matching the `pythonTools` and `nodeTools` patterns. A dedicated `DiscoverDotNetProject()` function (mirroring `DiscoverNodeProject()`) walks up from the script to find `.NET` project files, avoiding false negatives in mixed-language directories where Python or Node.js files would shadow `.csproj` in the generic discovery priority list. SDK version detection via a new `SdkVersion()` method on `dotnet.Cli` gates single-file mode to .NET 10+. Missing SDK errors use `ErrorWithSuggestion` with install links.

## Issue References

Resolves #7623

## Notes

- F#/VB.NET hook scripts are not explicitly targeted but `.fsproj`/`.vbproj` project discovery works via the existing `*.*proj` glob pattern.
- Single-file mode requires .NET SDK 10.0.0+. Users on older SDKs receive a clear error suggesting they create a `.csproj` or upgrade.

title: Add hbctool._bitcodec C++ extension and route integer encode/decode through it
body:
### Motivation

- Reduce Python-level overhead for small integer/byte conversions by moving little-endian integer encode/decode paths to native code for speed and lower CPU cost.

### Description

- Added a new C++ extension `hbctool/_bitcodec.cpp` which implements `le_to_uint` and `uint_to_le` helpers for little-endian integer decoding/encoding up to 8 bytes.
- Wired `hbctool/util.py` to discover/load the new `_bitcodec` extension when `HBCTOOL_FASTUTIL=1` and to route `readuint` and `writeuint` through `_bitcodec` for sizes up to 8 bytes (falling back to the Python code when the extension is not available).
- Updated `setup.py` to build and ship both `hbctool._fastutil` and the new `hbctool._bitcodec` extensions.

### Testing

- Ran the full test suite with `python -m pytest -q`, which passed (`19 passed`).
- Built extensions with `python setup.py build_ext --inplace`, which succeeded and produced both `_fastutil` and `_bitcodec` shared objects (note: an existing compiler warning from `_fastutil.cpp` remains). 
- Exercised the fast runtime path by running `HBCTOOL_FASTUTIL=1 python -m pytest -q tests/test_util.py`, which revealed two failing tests due to a pre-existing exception-type mismatch in `_fastutil.to_uint16` error handling (the failure is unrelated to `_bitcodec` logic).

title: Update wrappers with SWIG 4.4.1
fix(setup): add swig/liblgpio-dev deps and fix commented overlay dete…
body:
…ction on Trixie

Two fresh-install failures uncovered during Pi Zero 2W bring-up on Debian Trixie (aarch64):

1. The lgpio Python package has no prebuilt aarch64 wheel on PyPI, so pip builds it from source. On a fresh Raspberry Pi OS Lite (Trixie) image the build fails first at the swig codegen step, then at the linker step against liblgpio. Add both as apt dependencies so the wheel builds cleanly.

2. The overlay-add loop used 'grep -qF' which matches any substring, including commented lines like '#dtparam=spi=on'. A fresh Trixie config.txt ships SPI/I2C/UART commented-out by default, so the loop skipped them and left the interfaces disabled. Replace with an anchored regex that distinguishes active / commented / missing and uncomments in place via sed when a commented version exists.

Also note in README that the base Trixie Lite image does not ship with git, so it must be installed before cloning the repo.

title: Fix Windows drag-and-drop ctypes handle overflow
body:
### Motivation
- Prevent an `OverflowError: int too long to convert` that occurs when the `WM_DROPFILES` `wParam` is passed directly to shell32 APIs via ctypes. 
- Ensure the shell drag handle is used with the correct native type and always released via `DragFinish` to avoid resource or API misuse.

### Description
- Declare explicit ctypes signatures for `shell32.DragQueryFileW` and `shell32.DragFinish` by setting `restype` and `argtypes` so parameters and return values use native types. 
- Convert the incoming `drop_handle`/`wParam` to `wintypes.HANDLE` before calling `DragQueryFileW` and `DragFinish`. 
- Wrap the loop that reads dropped paths in a `try/finally` and call `DragFinish(handle)` in `finally` so the drop handle is always finished even on error. 
- Update calls to use the new `handle` variable when querying file names.

### Testing
- Ran `python -m py_compile app.py` which completed successfully.

------

title: Add cron manager + shift refresh (Phase 1, PR 2)
body:
## Summary

Port the cron manager from internal 680-line bash to 654-line Python, plus shift refresh script. Second component of Phase 1 (Core Porting).

- **manager.py** (654 lines): Python rewrite of cron-manager.sh
  - Commands: register, heartbeat, status, list, cleanup, checkout
  - Configurable checkout approver (no hardcoded names)
  - Atomic writes (flock + temp + os.replace)
  - Agent name validation (path traversal prevention)
- **shift_refresh.sh** (87 lines): Atomic flag file for sidecar restart
  - Validates handoff sentinel before allowing refresh

title: Sandbox: restrict network, filesystem, and import access in render_diagram
body:
## Summary

Harden the sandbox subprocess used by `render_diagram` with multiple layers of defense-in-depth:

- **Seccomp network blocking** (Linux): Blocks socket/connect/bind/listen/accept and related network syscalls via pyseccomp BPF filter. Fails closed on Linux if pyseccomp is missing.
- **Landlock filesystem restriction** (Linux 5.13+): Restricts filesystem access to tmpdir (rw) and system paths (ro) via direct Landlock syscalls (ctypes, zero new dependencies). Best-effort on older kernels.
- **Resource limits**: RLIMIT_CPU (30s), RLIMIT_FSIZE (50MB), RLIMIT_AS (512MB on Linux).
- **AST import validation**: Blocks imports of `os`, `subprocess`, `socket`, `http`, `ctypes`, `shutil`, and other dangerous modules. Wrapper globals cleaned before user code runs.
- **Process isolation**: `subprocess.Popen` with `start_new_session=True` and `os.killpg(SIGKILL)` on timeout to kill entire process trees. Python `-I` isolated mode. Minimal env (PATH, HOME, TMPDIR, LANG only).
- **CLI hardening**: `run_cli()` also uses Popen with session isolation and process-group killing.

## Test plan

- [x] All 225 existing tests pass (21 sandbox-specific)
- [x] New tests: `test_run_code_blocks_os_import`, `test_run_code_os_not_in_globals`
- [x] New Linux-only tests: `test_run_code_landlock_blocks_write_outside_tmpdir`, `test_run_code_landlock_allows_write_in_tmpdir`
- [x] Existing Linux-only tests: seccomp socket blocking, resource limits
- [x] `ruff check` passes cleanly
- [ ] Verify on Linux: Landlock blocks `/tmp` writes, seccomp blocks sockets, pyseccomp-missing raises RuntimeError

title: Switch from python-ssdeep to simple ctypes wrapper over libfuzzy2
body:
<!-- Thank you for contributing! -->
<!-- Please fill this template before submitting your PR (fill the boxes using "x") -->

**Your checklist for this pull request**
- [x] I've read the [contributing guideline](CONTRIBUTING.md).
- [x] I've tested my changes by building and running the project, and testing changed functionality (if applicable)
- [ ] I've added automated tests for my change (if applicable, optional)
- [x] I've updated documentation to reflect my change (if applicable)

**What is the current behaviour?**
<!-- Explain how the code works currently -->

https://pypi.org/project/ssdeep/ is unmaintained and last release was on 2019. We already had issues with setuptools that removed pkg_resources which broke the building process (https://github.com/CERT-Polska/mwdb-core/pull/1137). 

python-ssdeep requires `libfuzzy-dev` because during installation it is building a native Python extension using cffi that is linking with libfuzzy. On the other hand, libfuzzy has quite simple and stable interface which can be easily called using classic ctypes wrapper.

We don't do anything useful with these hashes as we don't support fuzzy search/comparison but this hash is there since initial release, so we maintain it for compatibility reasons.

**What is the new behaviour?**
<!-- Explain how the code works after your changes -->

This PR:
- adds `mwdb.core.ssdeep` with ctypes-based binding to `libfuzzy.so.2` and `SsdeepHash` class
- removes `ssdeep` dependency
- removes build-time dependencies from Docker files, because we no longer need to compile anything (actually most of them were stale)

libfuzzy2 is a dependency of libfuzzy-dev, but it would be good to clearly indicate in the release notes that we switch to another solution and things may break (although they shouldn't).

In case of problems on some platforms, this PR adds `enable_ssdeep` configuration option that allows to disable ssdeep evaluation.

**Test plan**

- Check if ssdeep still produces the same result
- Check if MWDB doesn't break when ssdeep is turned off and produces `null` value in the ssdeep column on file upload

title: fix(folder_tool): resolve 5 test failures — Pillow compat + ctypes.windll patching
body:
## Summary

- **Pillow backward-compat**: `Image.Resampling.LANCZOS` was introduced in Pillow 9.1; the CI environment has 9.0.1 which only has `Image.LANCZOS`. Added `getattr(Image, "Resampling", Image).LANCZOS` shim in both `_load_ico_icon` and `_load_png_fallback` so source runs on any supported Pillow version.
- **ctypes.windll cross-platform patching**: `patch("ctypes.windll.shell32.SetCurrentProcessExplicitAppUserModelID")` raises `ModuleNotFoundError` on Linux because `windll` does not exist as a real attribute. Switched to `patch("ctypes.windll", mock_windll, create=True)` which injects the mock without touching the real ctypes namespace.
- **Import sort fix**: `model_generation/__init__.py` had an unsorted import block (I001) after new lazy-import lines were added in the staging pull; auto-fixed with `ruff check --fix`.

Closes #1688

title: C extension
body:
We bump the version to 1.8 (which would also include the change to docopt-ng).

In this PR, we remove the dependency to `pynacl`, which uses `cffi` and [forces the use of `bytes`](https://github.com/pyca/pynacl/blob/main/src/nacl/bindings/crypto_aead.py#L120-L275), rather than `bytearray`. That makes it difficult to use for big files, since every segment triggers lots of memory allocations instead of re-using the same buffer (see [pynacl issue#707](https://github.com/pyca/pynacl/issues/707)). Instead, we implemented a Python C extension that binds to libsodium (and tries to retain only the used functions in the final shared object).

We update the testsuite to run on macOS and ubuntu, for python versions 3.9 to 3.14. We adapt the compilation to a bundled libsodium, and to an already installed system-wide version.

We update the docs (showing on [readthedocs.org](https://crypt4gh.readthedocs.io/c-extension/)) to reflect the installation changes.

We did not push yet to PyPI (we will do so when the PR is merged, solving #52 at the same time).

**Efficiency**:
Here is a small benchmark: we encrypt a bigfile (10 GB) using version 1.7 and this new C-extension. We then decrypt the files using the version the other encrypted. We are using a Macbook Air 2025 (M4, 32 GB, with SSD).

We encrypt using version 1.7:
```
(v1.7) $ time crypt4gh encrypt --recipient_pk pubkey < bigfile > bigfile.old.c4gh                    
real  2m36.722s
user  2m34.142s
sys   0m2.200s
```

We encrypt using the C extension:
```
(v1.8) $ time crypt4gh encrypt --recipient_pk pubkey < bigfile > bigfile.c.c4gh                    
real  0m18.415s
user  0m14.699s
sys   0m1.968s
```


We decrypt using version 1.7:
```
(v1.7) $ time crypt4gh decrypt --sk seckey < bigfile.c.c4gh > bigfile.old.decrypted
real  2m36.260s
user  2m34.043s
sys   0m1.977s
```

We decrypt using the C extension:
```
(v1.8)$ time crypt4gh decrypt --sk seckey < bigfile.old.c4gh > bigfile.c.decrypted
real  0m28.526s
user  0m26.222s
sys   0m1.927s
```

Using `diff`, we get that `bigfile == bigfile.c.decrypted == bigfile.old.decrypted`

**Conclusion**: about 6 to 8 times faster ⇒ [Not too bad](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Kr0DIzqXkOQ)

title: Prevent ctypes/X11 pointer mismatch causing Python helper segfault
body:
Le helper Python pouvait crasher par segmentation fault lors des commandes X11 (notamment `check-display`) au lieu de remonter une erreur propre. Le problème venait de signatures `ctypes` incomplètes sur des fonctions natives X11.

- **Root cause: signatures natives incomplètes**
  - `ctypes` utilisait des types par défaut pour certains symboles X11.
  - Sur plateformes 64-bit, cela peut tronquer des pointeurs/arguments et provoquer un segfault.

- **Hardening des bindings X11**
  - Ajout des `argtypes` manquants:
    - `XKeysymToKeycode(ctypes.c_void_p, ctypes.c_ulong)`
    - `XStringToKeysym(ctypes.c_char_p)`
  - Ajout des `restype` explicites:
    - `XFlush -> ctypes.c_int`
    - `XCloseDisplay -> ctypes.c_int`

- **Comportement attendu après changement**
  - Les erreurs d’accès display restent des erreurs applicatives gérées (`Cannot open display ...`), sans crash process.

```python
self.X11.XKeysymToKeycode.argtypes = [ctypes.c_void_p, ctypes.c_ulong]
self.X11.XStringToKeysym.argtypes = [ctypes.c_char_p]
self.X11.XFlush.restype = ctypes.c_int
self.X11.XCloseDisplay.restype = ctypes.c_int
```

title: C API exceptions
Story 1.1: Python IEC 61850 GOOSE publisher/subscriber with ctypes libIEC61850 wrapper
body:
Bootstraps the repo with a pure-Python IEC 61850 GOOSE library (Story 1.1) that wraps libIEC61850 via ctypes when installed and falls back to a raw-socket implementation otherwise. Also delivers the project skeleton, CI pipeline, and glossary (Story 7.4).

## Core library — `iec61850/goose/`

- **`types.py`** — `GooseConfig`, `GooseDataEntry`, `GooseDataType` (IEC 61850-8-1 Table A.2 BER tags), `GooseFrame` dataclasses
- **`codec.py`** — Pure-Python BER encoder/decoder for full GOOSE PDUs; no native dependency required
- **`publisher.py`** — `GoosePublisher`: auto-selects ctypes path (libIEC61850) or raw `AF_PACKET` socket; manages `stNum`/`sqNum` lifecycle
- **`subscriber.py`** — `GooseSubscriber`: daemon thread with APPID filter and typed callback
- **`_libiec61850.py`** — ctypes bindings; auto-discovers library via `$LIBIEC61850_PATH` → `find_library` → common paths; degrades gracefully when absent

```python
from iec61850.goose import GoosePublisher, GooseConfig, GooseDataEntry

pub = GoosePublisher(GooseConfig(interface="eth0", appid=0x0001))
pub.publish([GooseDataEntry.boolean(True), GooseDataEntry.float32(1500.0)])
pub.retransmit([GooseDataEntry.boolean(True), GooseDataEntry.float32(1500.0)])
pub.close()
```

## Tests & example

- **`tests/test_goose.py`** — 51 unit tests (BER round-trips, publisher/subscriber with mocked sockets, full frame integration); no hardware or root required
- **`examples/trip_on_overcurrent.py`** — simulated ANSI 50/51 overcurrent relay; publishes GOOSE trip + retransmissions; `--dry-run` mode needs no privileges

## Infrastructure

- **`.github/workflows/ci.yml`** — lint (flake8) + pytest matrix (Python 3.9–3.12) + Docker build; `contents: read` permissions
- **`pyproject.toml`** — installable package (`pip install -e ".[dev]"`)
- **`Dockerfile`**, **`README.md`** (quickstart, architecture diagram), **`CONTRIBUTING.md`**, **`LICENSE`**

## Docs

- **`docs/glossary.md`** — 25+ terms: IED, GOOSE, MMS, SV, SCL, PTOC/PTRC/XCBR, ADMS, DNP3, COMTRADE, PTP, merging unit, logical node, data object, etc.

# 📋 User Stories — Open Source Grid Operations & Substation Automation

> **Repo Purpose:** A community-driven open-source project providing reusable tools, libraries, and reference implementations for grid operations technology — covering substation automation, SCADA integration, IEC 61850, data pipelines, and grid modernization.

## EPIC 1 — IEC 61850 Communication Foundation

> Enable open, standards-compliant IEC 61850 communication between IEDs, merging units, and control systems.

-----

**Story 1.1** `story` `domain: IEC61850` `priority: high`
**As a** protection engineer,
**I want** a Python wrapper around libIEC61850 that publishes and subscribes to GOOSE messages,
**So that** I can prototype protection logic without writing C code.

title: Add standalone Tkinter app for Nanotec stepper control
body:
### Motivation
- Provide a small, standalone, clickable Tkinter application that only controls the two Nanotec stepper motors so operators can run motor-only workflows without the full main GUI. 
- Keep integration-safe behavior by reusing the existing Nanotec protocol driver and keeping Taster/Shutter state manual until hardware I/O is fully wired. 

### Description
- Added a new standalone app `python_rewrite/nanotec_motor_app.py` that builds a two‑motor UI, manual Taster checkboxes, a manual Shutter input, encoder rotation indicators, process buttons for reference/infeed/outfeed/transfer and a sputter-run loop. 
- The app reuses the existing driver stack (`sputtering_app.devices.nanotec`, `SerialDeviceTransport` / `SerialSettings`) to start/stop/profile/poll motors and to remain compatible with NanoPro motor configuration. 
- Implemented encoder‑lamp logic that detects encoder motion from readback deltas and shows a green/red lamp, and implemented event logging to `python_rewrite/nanotec_motor_events.log` for Start/Stop/STEP/LIMIT/MOTOR_STOP events. 
- Added clickable launchers `Start_Nanotec_Motors.bat` and `Start_Nanotec_Motors.command` and updated `python_rewrite/README.md` with usage notes for the standalone app. 

### Testing

title: Add Nanotec standalone Tkinter app, launch scripts and enhanced Nanotec GUI controls
body:
### Motivation
- Provide a lightweight standalone Tkinter host for controlling just the Nanotec stepper motors reusing the existing Nanotec protocol driver.
- Improve operator ergonomics and safety with manual jog, step-range motion, step-zeroing, clearer chamber labels and direction mapping for the inverted motor wiring.

title: fix: silence Qt DPI awareness warning on Windows
body:
Qt 6 calls `SetProcessDpiAwarenessContext(DPI_AWARENESS_CONTEXT_PER_MONITOR_AWARE_V2)` during `QApplication` init. The manual `ctypes.windll.shcore.SetProcessDpiAwareness(2)` call at module load was racing it — Windows only allows setting DPI awareness once per process, so Qt's attempt returned `E_ACCESSDENIED` and printed the warning to stderr.

## Changes

- **Removed** the `ctypes` DPI awareness block from module startup — Qt 6 sets the newer `DPI_AWARENESS_CONTEXT_PER_MONITOR_AWARE_V2` context automatically, which is the functional superset of the removed `PROCESS_PER_MONITOR_DPI_AWARE` (value 2) call
- **Updated** the module docstring to reflect that PyQt6 now owns DPI awareness setup; `mss` and `GetCursorPos` coordinates remain physical pixels since `QApplication` is constructed before any capture occurs

```python
# Before — conflicts with Qt 6's own SetProcessDpiAwarenessContext() call
if sys.platform == "win32":
    try:
        import ctypes
        ctypes.windll.shcore.SetProcessDpiAwareness(2)
    except Exception:
        pass

# After — removed; Qt 6 handles this during QApplication.__init__
```

title: Fix ICM recursion/chip transfer, add range-aware equity, hybrid GTO wiring, opponent targeting, plus WindowActivator hardening
body:
### Motivation
- Correct mathematically incorrect ICM recursion and chip-flow on call branches which produced wrong tournament EVs.
- Make equity estimates and decision logic range-aware instead of sampling uniform-random opponent hands.
- Wire hybrid/GTO second-opinion into the decision context in an advisory/traceable way so hybrid mode reflects real consults.
- Improve primary-opponent selection and relative-position handling and harden execution layer init on non-Windows CI/dev hosts.

### Description
- Fixed ICM recursion by carrying full branch probability into deeper finishing positions and updated `icm_call_ev()` to transfer chips to/from a targeted opponent instead of only mutating hero stack, and added `opponent_index` support in `TournamentLogic`.  
- Replaced uniform opponent sampling with a coverage-filtered opponent pool in `EquityEngine.estimate_equity_vs_range()` and added `_filtered_opponent_pool()` and a preflop combo-ranking helper; equity results are cached per `(hero, board, coverage)`.  
- Activated hybrid/GTO advisory wiring at context-build time: `DecisionContext.gto_opinion` is populated when hybrid consults are enabled and `consult_gto()` is invoked defensively; added `_hybrid_gto_consult_enabled()`.  
- Improved decision targeting and relative-position logic in `DecisionEngine`: ` _primary_opponent_seat()` now prefers last non-hero aggressor then last money-in actor with an ordered-seat fallback; `relative_position` is computed and used to derive `position_advantage`.  
- Introduced table-driven preflop RFI thresholds via `_preflop_rfi_threshold()` and tightened/shifted `bubble_pressure` gating and hybrid open ordering; added `_icm_win_probability()` to prefer measured `real_equity` and otherwise adjust by `opponent_range.coverage` and multiway penalty.  
- Added structured range line adjustments: `RangeNarrower._LINE_ADJUSTMENTS` plus `line_adjustments()` are applied to opponent ranges and used to modulate bluff gates via `_line_bluff_modifier()`.  
- Hardened `WindowActivator` initialization to not assume `ctypes.windll` exists by using `getattr(ctypes, "windll", None)` so non-Windows imports/tests do not crash.  
- Tests: added/updated unit tests to cover ICM behavior, equity filtering, range line adjustments, primary-opponent selection, hybrid GTO population, preflop RFI thresholds, and the WindowActivator non-Windows path; relevant test files are `tests/test_tournament_logic.py`, `tests/test_equity_engine.py`, `tests/test_range_model.py`, `tests/test_window_activator.py`, and updates in `tests/test_decision_engine.py`.

### Testing
- Ran the unit test suite with `pytest`; all new and updated tests passed.  
- Verified `tests/test_tournament_logic.py` asserts equal-stack sanity and opponent-transfer semantics and passes.  
- Verified `tests/test_equity_engine.py` and `tests/test_range_model.py` assert coverage-sensitive equity and line-adjustment behavior and pass.  
- Verified `tests/test_window_activator.py` covers missing `ctypes.windll` initialization and passes.

------

title: Add C-accelerated pixel ops for millisecond-level thumbnail strip generation
body:
The no-cache contact-sheet path had unnecessary overhead: N intermediate BGRA buffer allocations, N separate ctypes calls for BGRA→RGB conversion, and Python-level bisect loops for keyframe snapping. For 4K videos this dominated post-extraction processing time.

### New C functions in `fast_thumb.c`

- **`split_strip_bgra_to_rgb`** — Combined split + BGRA→RGB in one pass over the strip. Replaces split → N×allocate → N×convert pipeline with a single C call.
- **`bgra_to_rgb_multi`** — Batch convert N concatenated BGRA frames in one ctypes call.
- **`rotate_bgra`** — 0/90/180/270° rotation with optional vflip. Available for replacing PIL `transpose()` in PyAV path.
- **`snap_to_keyframes`** — C binary search replacing Python bisect loop.
- **`scale_bilinear_bgra`** — Bilinear downscale for direct BGRA resizing.

### Integration

- **`worker.py`**: Contact-sheet caching now calls `_split_strip_bgra_to_rgb` (one C call for the entire strip→RGB conversion). Falls back through `bgra_to_rgb_multi` → per-frame → pure Python.
- **`extraction.py`**: `_snap_to_keyframes` delegates to C when available. New `_split_strip_bgra_to_rgb` wrapper exposed.
- **`_native/__init__.py`**: ctypes wrappers + pure-Python fallbacks for all new functions. JIT compilation adds `-lm`.

### Hot path before/after

```
Before: strip → C:split(N BGRA) → emit → N × C:bgra_to_rgb → join → cache
After:  strip → C:split(N BGRA) → emit → C:split_strip_bgra_to_rgb(1 call) → cache
```

18 new tests, 124 total passing. CodeQL clean.

title: STYLE: Replace declare-then-Fill(N) with ::Filled(N) factory method
body:
## Summary

Replaces the verbose two-line FixedArray initialization pattern with the modern single-line static factory method `::Filled(v)`.

**Before:**
```cpp
ImageType::SizeType size;
size.Fill(16);
```

**After:**
```cpp
constexpr auto size = ImageType::SizeType::Filled(16);
```

## Rules applied

- **`constexpr auto`** used when the variable is never subsequently modified (test files with concrete types)
- **`auto`** (without `constexpr`) used for accumulator variables in loop bodies (`.hxx` template implementations)
- **Skipped:** cases where `Fill()` is immediately followed by element-wise overrides (`size[0] = w`), or where `typename` disambiguation in deeply nested template parameter scopes makes the replacement non-trivial

## Files changed

| File | Pattern |
|------|---------|
| `Modules/IO/NRRD/test/itkNrrdLocaleTest.cxx` | `SizeType` + `IndexType` → `constexpr auto` |
| `Modules/Video/BridgeOpenCV/test/itkOpenCVVideoIOTest.cxx` | `PointType` → `constexpr auto` |
| `Modules/Filtering/Path/include/itkChainCodeToFourierSeriesPathFilter.hxx` | `VectorType` accumulators → `auto` |
| `Modules/Filtering/QuadEdgeMeshFiltering/include/itkSmoothingQuadEdgeMeshFilter.hxx` | `VectorType` accumulator → `auto` |

Pure style change — no semantic changes.

Suggested by @N-Dekker in https://github.com/InsightSoftwareConsortium/ITK/pull/6003#discussion_r3033221346

title: ENH: Add comprehensive GIL release safety tests
body:
## Summary

Add 8 safety tests for the SWIG \`-threads\` GIL release feature
(\`ITK_PYTHON_RELEASE_GIL\`), covering known gotchas from the SWIG
issue tracker and Python C API documentation.

Companion to PR #6022. Tests pass with both \`GIL=ON\` and \`GIL=OFF\`.

## Test results

| Setting | Python 3.10 | 3.11 | 3.12 | 3.13 | 3.14 |
|---------|:-----------:|:----:|:----:|:----:|:----:|
| GIL=ON  | 15/15       | 15/15| 15/15| 15/15| 15/15|
| GIL=OFF | --          | --   | --   | 15/15| --   |

## Tests and references

| # | Test | Reference |
|---|------|-----------|
| 1 | Callback safety during filter execution | [swig/swig#3091](https://github.com/swig/swig/issues/3091), [swig/swig#2670](https://github.com/swig/swig/issues/2670) |
| 2 | Concurrent callbacks from 4 threads | [Python C API: non-Python threads](https://docs.python.org/3/c-api/init.html#non-python-created-threads) |
| 3 | Exception propagation across GIL boundary | [SWIG-devel: exception + threads segfault](https://swig-devel.narkive.com/PBt1fCLN) |
| 4 | Object destruction under concurrent access | [Python refcounting](https://docs.python.org/3/c-api/refcounting.html) |
| 5 | Concurrent image processing (data races) | ITK MultiThreaderBase thread safety |
| 6 | Signal handling while GIL released | [Python signals and threads](https://docs.python.org/3/library/signal.html#signals-and-threads) |
| 7 | GIL reacquisition deadlock detection | [PyGILState_Ensure](https://docs.python.org/3/c-api/init.html#PyGILState_Ensure) |
| 8 | Stress test (200 create/destroy, 4 threads) | [swig/swig#2396](https://github.com/swig/swig/issues/2396), [swig/swig#3121](https://github.com/swig/swig/issues/3121) |

title: feat: migrate C++ physics engine to Python
body:
### Related task
README.md: Python - "Coming soon!" 항목 구현

### What has changed
1. C++ 2D 물리 엔진을 Python(pygame)으로 완전 마이그레이션 (`Python/` 디렉토리)
2. `vec2.py`: Vec2, Mat22 벡터/행렬 수학 클래스
3. `objects.py`: Object, Manifold, Joint 및 SAT 기반 충돌 감지/응답 함수
4. `physics.py`: pygame 렌더링, 게임 루프, 데모 씬, 전체 인터랙션 (오브젝트 생성, 인공 중력, 일시정지, 속도 조절 등)
5. `requirements.txt`: pygame 의존성 추가
6. `.gitignore`: venv, __pycache__ 등 제외 규칙 추가
7. `CLAUDE.md`: Claude Code 가이드 문서 추가
8. C++ 원본 버그 수정: `CollisionType()`에서 `=` → `==` 비교 연산자
9. 정적-정적 객체 충돌 시 zero-division 방어 코드 추가

### Additional info
- Windows GDI 의존성을 제거하고 pygame을 사용하여 크로스 플랫폼(macOS/Linux/Windows) 지원
- C++ 원본의 모든 물리 로직 및 인터랙션을 1:1로 포팅:
  - 충돌: AABB↔AABB, Circle↔Circle, AABB↔Circle (SAT)
  - 임펄스 기반 충돌 응답 + 마찰
  - 경계 벽 충돌 (`wallref`)
  - 마우스 드래그로 사각형 생성 / 인공 중력 모드
  - 회전 플랫폼, 스피닝 원 등 per-object 강제 동작
- 조작법: SPACE(일시정지), G(중력 토글), I(원 생성), 마우스 드래그(사각형 생성), 1(모드 전환), UP/DOWN(속도 조절), LEFT/RIGHT(토크), R(리셋), ESC(종료)

title: Fix ARM64 ESL build: generate config.m4 before phpize
body:
The FreeSWITCH ESL `php/` directory doesn't ship a `config.m4`, which `phpize` requires before it can run — causing the ARM64 build path in `install_esl_extension.sh` to fail with "Cannot find config.m4".

## Changes

- **`install/install_esl_extension.sh`**: In `build_esl_from_source()`, insert a `config.m4` generation step between SWIG binding generation and the `phpize` call. The file is only written if it doesn't already exist.

```sh
if [ ! -f "$PHP_EXT_DIR/config.m4" ]; then
  cat > "$PHP_EXT_DIR/config.m4" <<'EOF'
PHP_ARG_ENABLE(esl, whether to enable ESL support,
[  --enable-esl            Enable ESL support])

if test "$PHP_ESL" != "no"; then
  PHP_NEW_EXTENSION(esl, ESL.cpp, $ext_shared)
fi
EOF
fi
```

The rest of the `phpize` → `configure` → `make` flow is unchanged.

<!-- START COPILOT ORIGINAL PROMPT -->



<details>

<summary>Original prompt</summary>

## Problem

On ARM64 (aarch64) devices, running `install/install_esl_extension.sh` fails with:

```
Cannot find config.m4.
Make sure that you run '/usr/bin/phpize8.4 --clean' in the top level source directory of the module

Cannot find config.m4.
Make sure that you run '/usr/bin/phpize8.4' in the top level source directory of the module
```

This happens in the `build_esl_from_source()` function. The script sets `PHP_EXT_DIR` to `/usr/src/freeswitch/libs/esl/php` and then calls `phpize` there — but the FreeSWITCH ESL `php/` directory does **not** ship a `config.m4` file. `phpize` requires `config.m4` to be present in the directory before it can run.

## Fix

In `install/install_esl_extension.sh`, inside `build_esl_from_source()`, add a step **after** the SWIG bindings are generated and **before** `phpize` is called to generate a `config.m4` file if one does not already exist.

The `config.m4` content should be:

```m4
PHP_ARG_ENABLE(esl, whether to enable ESL support,
[  --enable-esl            Enable ESL support])

if test "$PHP_ESL" != "no"; then
  PHP_NEW_EXTENSION(esl, ESL.cpp, $ext_shared)
fi
```

### Specific change

In the `build_esl_from_source()` function, insert the following block **between** the `print_success "SWIG bindings generated."` line and the `# ── Build using phpize ──` comment block (i.e., before `print_info "Running phpize in $PHP_EXT_DIR ..."` on line 156):

```sh
  # ── Generate config.m4 if missing (phpize requires it) ─────────────────
  if [ ! -f "$PHP_EXT_DIR/config.m4" ]; then
    print_info "Generating config.m4 for ESL PHP extension..."
    cat > "$PHP_EXT_DIR/config.m4" <<'EOF'
PHP_ARG_ENABLE(esl, whether to enable ESL support,
[  --enable-esl            Enable ESL support])

if test "$PHP_ESL" != "no"; then
  PHP_NEW_EXTENSION(esl, ESL.cpp, $ext_shared)
fi
EOF
    print_success "config.m4 generated."
  fi
```

The file to modify is `install/install_esl_extension.sh` (BlobSha: `c2c31f6883fba3cc1a8afe5b5cdd825988e48edd`).

No other changes are needed. The rest of the `phpize`, `configure`, and `make` flow remains the same.

The `phpize` step is run inside `$PHP_EXT_DIR` which is set to `$ESL_DIR/php` (i.e. `/usr/src/freeswitch/libs/esl/php`). However, `phpize` requires a `config.m4` file in that directory — and the FreeSWITCH ESL `php/` directory **does not ship a `config.m4`**. It must be generated by running `make phpmod` (or `make php`) from the ESL root directory first, or written manually.

The script skips the `make` step in the ESL directory that produces `config.m4` and goes straight to `phpize`.

In `build_esl_from_source()`, **before** running `phpize`, you need to either:

1. **Run `make phpmod` / `make php` from `$ESL_DIR`** to let the ESL Makefile generate `config.m4`, **or**
2. **Write a minimal `config.m4` yourself** before calling `phpize`.

Option 2 is more robust (doesn't depend on the ESL Makefile working). Here is the corrected `build_esl_from_s...

title: Proyecto TP2: puente Python, C y Assembly
body:
# Proyecto TP2: puente Python, C y Assembly

Este proyecto muestra un flujo completo de integración entre:

- Python 64-bit para consumir una API pública.
- Python 32-bit para actuar como puente con una biblioteca nativa.
- C y Assembly para procesar un arreglo de valores flotantes y devolver enteros.

La ejecución principal toma el índice de Gini de países desde la API del Banco Mundial, filtra los datos por código ISO3 y luego envía la lista resultante a la librería nativa a través de `msl.loadlib`.

## Contenido del proyecto

### `main.py`
Script principal de ejemplo. Hace tres cosas:

1. Consulta la API del Banco Mundial para el indicador `SI.POV.GINI`.
2. Filtra los registros del país elegido por código ISO3, por ejemplo `BRA`, `CHL` o `COL`.
3. Envía el arreglo de valores obtenidos al puente `ClienteBridge` y muestra el resultado devuelto por la librería nativa.

### `client.py`
Define `ClienteBridge`, una clase basada en `msl.loadlib.Client64`.

Su función es conectarse al servidor 32-bit y llamar al método remoto `bridge`, enviando una lista normal de Python y recibiendo una lista de enteros como respuesta.

### `server.py`
Define `Server`, una clase basada en `msl.loadlib.Server32`.

Este servidor carga `./libbridge.so` mediante `ctypes`, declara la firma de la función nativa `bridge` y transforma la lista recibida de Python en arreglos C antes de llamar a la librería compartida.

### `bridge.c`
Capa de puente en C entre Python y Assembly.

Recibe un tamaño, un arreglo de `float` y un arreglo de salida de `int`. Recorre el arreglo y llama a `convert(...)` para cada elemento, escribiendo el resultado en la posición correspondiente.

### `convert.asm`
Rutina en Assembly x86 de 32 bits.

La función `convert` toma un `float`, lo convierte a entero con la FPU y luego suma 1 al valor obtenido. Ese es el valor que termina regresando cada conversión.

### `asm_io.asm`
Biblioteca de utilidades de entrada y salida en Assembly.

Incluye rutinas auxiliares como lectura y escritura de enteros, caracteres, cadenas y volcados de registros, memoria, pila y coprocesador matemático. Es un archivo genérico de apoyo para prácticas de Assembly.

### `asm_io.inc`
Archivo de inclusión con macros para usar las rutinas de `asm_io.asm`.

Define macros como `dump_regs`, `dump_mem`, `dump_math` y `dump_stack` para invocar las rutinas de diagnóstico desde otros archivos Assembly.

### `cdecl.h`
Cabecera de compatibilidad para la convención de llamada C.

Define `PRE_CDECL` y `POST_CDECL` para que las declaraciones funcionen correctamente tanto con GCC como con otros compiladores C/C++.

### `build/`
Directorio con artefactos generados.

En el estado actual contiene:

- `build/bridge.o`
- `build/convert.o`
- `build/libbridge.so`

## Requisitos

- Python 3.
- Paquetes de Python: `requests` y `msl-loadlib`.
- NASM y un compilador C para Linux si quieres reconstruir la biblioteca nativa.
- Un entorno 32-bit compatible para el servidor de `msl.loadlib`, si tu instalación lo requiere.

## Forma de uso

### 1. Instalar dependencias de Python

```bash
pip install requests msl-loadlib
```

### 2. Asegurar que la biblioteca compartida esté donde la espera el servidor

`server.py` carga `./libbridge.so`, así que el archivo debe estar en la raíz del proyecto.

Si solo tienes la versión compilada dentro de `build/`, cópiala antes de ejecutar el ejemplo:

```bash
cp build/libbridge.so ./libbridge.so
```

### 3. Ejecutar el ejemplo principal

```bash
python main.py
```

### 4. Cambiar el país consultado

En `main.py`, modifica la variable `country`:

```python
country = "BRA"
```

Puedes reemplazar `BRA` por cualquier código ISO3 válido que tenga datos disponibles en la API.

## Flujo de ejecución

1. `main.py` descarga los datos del Banco Mundial.
2. `main.py` crea `ClienteBridge`.
3. `ClienteBridge` llama al método remoto `bridge` del servidor 32-bit.
4. `server.py` convierte la lista de Python en arreglos C.
5. `bridge.c` recorre el arreglo y llama a `convert` en Assembly.
6. `convert.asm` transforma cada `float` en un `int` y suma 1.
7. El servidor devuelve la lista de enteros al proceso principal.

## Resultado esperado

La consola muestra:

- El país consultado.
- La lista de valores del índice de Gini obtenidos desde la API.
- El arreglo devuelto por la librería nativa.
- Un aviso si la función C retorna un estado distinto de cero.

## Notas importantes

- El script principal usa la API pública del Banco Mundial, así que necesita conexión a Internet.
- El servidor está pensado para trabajar con `libbridge.so` en la misma carpeta donde se ejecuta `server.py`.
- Los archivos `asm_io.asm` y `asm_io.inc` son utilidades auxiliares; no forman parte directa del flujo de `main.py`, pero sí son parte del material presente en el proyecto.

This PR introduces a fast and feature-separated Python test suite for the regular expression matcher project. By compiling a lightweight C API interface (`libcsa_test.so`) and using `ctypes` in Python, tests are decoupled from the internal implementation, fast, and easily extensible. Tests are categorized into classes like basic matching, counters, unicode, etc., and utilize custom pytest markers to easily skip specific features if needed in the future (e.g. `pytest -m "not unicode"`).

- Implemented `ctypes.CDLL` manual pre-load of PyTorch's `c10.dll` explicitly for Windows platforms.
- Moved `import torch`, `ultralytics`, and `easyocr` to the absolute top of `main_ui.py`, ensuring they initialize *before* any `PyQt5` modules are imported.

LibJS: Remove C++ compiler pipeline, use Rust exclusively
body:
Now that we've been running with the Rust pipeline as the default for a couple weeks and the world hasn't exploded, let's make life easier for ourselves by removing the C++ pipeline.

- Delete Parser, Lexer, AST, ASTDump, ScopeCollector, Generator, ASTCodegen, ScopedOperand, PipelineComparison (~20,600 lines)
- Remove LIBJS_CPP, LIBJS_COMPARE_PIPELINES env vars and all fallback paths
- Port EventTarget and WebDriver to compile_dynamic_function()
- Port SyntaxHighlighter and js REPL to Rust tokenizer via new rust_tokenize() FFI
- Port test262-runner parse-only check to Rust parse_program()

45 test262 early-error tests regress. These are pre-existing differences between the Rust and C++ parsers that were previously masked by the C++ fallback. Will deal with these in a later PR.

title: synq: add dialect support to Python API
body:
## Motivation

The Python API (`format_sql`, `parse`, `validate`, `tokenize`) is hardcoded to SQLite. Users need to load external dialect extensions (like Perfetto) from Python.

Stacked on #71 (Grammar→Dialect rename).

## Changes

**C library (`dialect_load.c`):**
- `syntaqlite_dialect_load(path, name)` / `syntaqlite_loaded_dialect_destroy()` using dlopen (POSIX) / LoadLibrary (Windows)
- All loading logic lives in the library, not in Python

**C FFI:**
- `syntaqlite_formatter_create_with_dialect(dialect, config)` 
- `syntaqlite_validator_create_with_dialect(dialect)`

**Python:**
- `Dialect(path, name)` class wrapping `syntaqlite_dialect_load()`
- `dialect=` keyword parameter on `format_sql`, `parse`, `validate`, `tokenize`
- `_syntaqlite.c` is a thin wrapper — no dlopen in Python

**Usage:**
```python
from syntaqlite import Dialect, format_sql, parse

d = Dialect("libsyntaqlite_perfetto.dylib", "perfetto")
format_sql("CREATE PERFETTO TABLE foo AS SELECT 1", dialect=d)
```

title: Detect screen lock/unlock and restart hotkeys on unlock
body:
## Root cause

Every time you walk away (screen locks), hotkeys break and the icon stays green. This is **not** the WH_KEYBOARD_LL hook dying — it's Windows corrupting the `keyboard` library's modifier state during lock. Ctrl/Shift can appear stuck-pressed, so combinations like `Ctrl+Space` never match even though the hook thread is still alive and delivering raw key events.

The existing sleep/wake detection only fires when the watchdog has a **gap > 30s** — screen lock without system sleep doesn't cause a gap, so it was invisible to the watchdog.

## Fix

Add `_is_screen_locked()` using `OpenInputDesktop`: when the session is locked, the input desktop is either inaccessible (returns 0) or named `"Winlogon"` instead of `"Default"`. Checked every 15s in the watchdog slow path. On the `locked → unlocked` transition, immediately restart the hotkey subprocess — fresh process = clean modifier state.

**No new dependencies.** Uses `ctypes.windll.user32` which is always available on Windows.

## What changes

- `import ctypes` added to `voice.py`
- `_is_screen_locked()` function added near the watchdog section
- `_screen_was_locked` bool tracked across watchdog ticks
- On unlock: `setup_hotkeys()` + `update_tray("#2ecc71", "Koda: Ready")`

## Test plan

- [x] 197 tests passing
- [ ] Lock screen (Win+L), wait a few seconds, unlock — hotkeys should work immediately
- [ ] Check `debug.log` for `"Screen unlock detected — restarting hotkeys to restore hook state"`
- [ ] Verify tray icon stays green (no false yellow) during normal use

title: Add minimal Python sidecar and C++ localhost health bridge
body:
### Motivation
- Provide the smallest possible Python sidecar to establish the local HTTP boundary for future STT/vision/planner adapters.  
- Give the C++ shell a simple way to detect and log whether the sidecar is available so the app can degrade gracefully.  
- Keep the repo surface minimal (one `python/service.py` file) and avoid adding model or heavy infra yet.

### Description
- Add `python/service.py`, a single-file sidecar using the standard library `http.server` that exposes `GET /health` and `POST /plan` (mock planner response) and logs incoming requests.  
- Update `src/main.cpp` to perform a socket-based startup health check against `127.0.0.1:8080/health`, emit cross-language boundary logs, and continue running if the sidecar is unreachable.  
- Update `README.md` with concise build/run steps and manual commands to test the health and mock planner endpoints.

### Testing

title: Declare free-threaded Python support on the python_function plugin.
body:
<!---
Thank you for contributing to NVIDIA DALI! If you haven't yet,
please read the contributing guidelines in the CONTRIBUTING.md file.

We need a few more information from you to proceed.
Please fill the relevant sections in this PR template.

Fields in the Checklist section can be marked after you create and save the Pull Request.
--->


## Category:

**Other** (*e.g. Documentation, Tests, Configuration*)


## Description:

The DALI backend Pybind11 extension already declares support for free-threaded Python. This PR does the same for `python_function_plugin`. This is the missing piece for full free-threaded Python support.

Since `python_function_plugin` is always transitively imported, not declaring free-threaded support results in the GIL always being re-enabled for DALI:
```
frozen importlib._bootstrap>:491: RuntimeWarning: The global interpreter lock (GIL) has been enabled to load module 'nvidia.dali.python_function_plugin', which has not declared that it can run safely without the GIL. To override this behavior and keep the GIL disabled (at your own risk), run with PYTHON_GIL=0 or -Xgil=0.
```

title: Improvements to SWIG typemaps for Python interface
body:
Various improvements to the SWIG typemaps for the Python interface, organized by priority.

### Critical — Crash/Segfault Bugs

**1. Missing NULL checks in callback wrappers (`meep.i`)**

`py_callback_wrap` (line 142), `py_amp_func_wrap` (line 152), and `custom_py_src_time::dipole` (`meep-python.hpp:32`) do not check the return value of `PyObject_CallFunctionObjArgs`. If the Python callback raises an exception, these dereference NULL →segfault. Same issue in `mpb.i` callback wrappers.

**2. Use-after-free in `_get_dft_array` (`meep.i:456`)**

The `rank == 0` zero-data path passes a stack-allocated `d[1]` to `PyArray_SimpleNewFromData`, which creates a NumPy array pointing to stack memory that goes out of scope. The singleton path also leaks `dft_arr`.

### High Priority — Correctness

**3. `meep::abort()` instead of Python exceptions (`typemap_utils.cpp`)**

Many conversion functions (e.g., `pyabsorber_to_absorber`, `pymaterial_grid_to_material_grid`, `py_list_to_gobj_list`) call `abort_with_stack_trace()` on invalid input. This kills the process instead of raising a catchable Python exception. These should set `PyErr_SetString` and return an error code.

**4. `PyFunction_Check` vs `PyCallable_Check` (`meep.i:1408`)**

The `kpoint_func` typemap rejects lambdas, bound methods, `functools.portal`, and any callable that isn't a plain function. Should use `PyCallable_Check`.

### Medium Priority — Maintenance & Compatibility

**5. Dupilcate typemaps**
- `xtics/ytics/ztics` typemaps appear twice (lines 1001-1018 and 1083-1105), first block is dead code.
- `freearg` for amplitude function appears twice (lines 748 and 1069).
- `double*` array input typemaps repeat the same `is_array` + `array_data` pattern ~8 times - could use `%apply`.

### Low Priority — Cleanup

**6. Duplicate `%ignore` directives (`meep.i:1525-1529`)**

`is_variable`, `is_file`, `is_medium` each ignored twice.

title: Merge optimize → develop
body:
## Summary

Merges the `optimize` branch (159 commits) into `develop`. Key work streams:

- **Tool routing & discovery**: `search_api`, `recommend_tools`, tags on all 142 tools, description enrichment
- **LLM test suite**: 170+ tests, progressive L1/L2/L3, benchmark tracking, cross-model sweeps
- **Geometry tools**: `create_bar_building`, `create_new_building`, `import_floorspacejs`
- **Generic object access**: `get_object_fields`, `set_object_property`, dynamic `list_model_objects`
- **Measure authoring**: full skill with ReportingMeasure support, `search_wiring_patterns`
- **Response-size guardrails**: `max_results` + filters on all list tools, brief mode
- **Agent guardrails**: anti-loop instructions, tool-bypass prevention
- **HVAC**: FourPipeBeam, CooledBeam terminals, `set_zone_equipment_priority`
- **Stdout suppression fix (issue #42)**: permanent fd redirect replaces racy global middleware
- **CI**: 5-shard test matrix, ~450+ integration tests

### Stdout fix (Fixes #42)

Commit `2395d95` replaces the global FastMCP middleware (which held `os.dup2()` on fd 1 for entire tool calls, causing concurrent response loss) with a one-time fd redirect at startup. C-level stdout (SWIG, Polyhedron, etc.) goes to stderr permanently; Python `sys.stdout` writes JSON-RPC to a private fd. No races, no missed callsites.

## Test plan
- [ ] CI shards 1-5 pass
- [ ] `test_concurrent_tools.py` passes (concurrent slow+fast and fast+fast)
- [ ] `test_complex_model_stdout_purity` passes (Polyhedron leak on 44-zone model)
- [ ] No SWIG or Polyhedron messages in stdout

title: fix: silence openstudio stdout Logger to prevent Polyhedron/Space pollution
body:
## Summary

- Adds `silence_openstudio_stdout_logger()` to `stdout_suppression.py`, called from `server.py::main()` before `redirect_c_stdout_to_stderr()`. Sets `standardOutLogger` level to `Fatal` via the intended Logger API — primary fix for Polyhedron/Space warnings reaching C stdout during `Space::volume()`.
- Keeps the existing fd-1→fd-2 redirect as a backstop for SWIG memleak warnings in edge cases and future unknown C stdout writes.
- Adds 3 regression tests (`tests/test_stdout_logger_silence.py`) including a falsification test that resets to Warn to prove the pollution is real.
- Added to CI shard 5.

Fixes #48. Upstream SWIG memleak root cause filed as NatLabRockies/OpenStudio#5608 (PyPI wheel missed #5422's `SWIG_PYTHON_SILENT_MEMLEAK` define).

title: Remove non-Python packs, focus on Python-only
body:
## Summary

- **Removed all 10 non-Python language packs** (JS, Go, C++, Ruby, Rust, Java, C#, PHP, Kotlin, Swift)
- **Updated description** from "universal architecture linter" to "architecture linter for Python projects"
- **Cleaned up entry points** to register only the Python pack
- **Added CLAUDE.md to .gitignore**

### Why

A Rust or Go developer isn't going to `pip install gaudi-linter`. The non-Python packs did shallow regex matching that native tools (golangci-lint, eslint, clippy) handle better. Gaudi's value is deep AST-based analysis of Python projects — Django models, SQLAlchemy relationships, FastAPI endpoints, etc.

The plugin architecture remains intact — external packs can register via entry points — but the shipped product focuses on what it does well.

Closes #5

## Test plan

- [x] pytest: 17/17 passed
- [x] ruff: All checks passed
- [x] gaudi check src/: 2 expected warnings (false positives in example patterns)
- [x] No stale entry point errors after clean install

title: Fix Python detection to scan all drives instead of C:\ only
body:
Python detection was failing with ENOENT errors when installations existed on non-C drives (D:, E:, etc.). The existing logic only checked system PATH and hardcoded C:\ paths.

## Changes

- **New `detectPythonExecutable()` helper**: Centralizes detection logic with all-drive scanning
  - Enumerates all available drives (A-Z) on Windows
  - Checks multiple installation patterns per drive:
    - Microsoft Store (`WindowsApps`)
    - Python.org (`AppData\Local\Programs\Python`)
    - System-wide (`Program Files\Python`)
    - Direct installs (`D:\Python312`, `E:\Python3`, etc.)
    - Developer paths (`\bin`, `\tools`, `\dev`, `\opt`)
  - Standardized version regex: `/^Python3?\d*$/i` with configurable bounds (3.8-3.13)

- **Updated 5 Python-dependent handlers** to use new detection:
  - `runPytorchAutoInstall()` (first-launch auto-setup)
  - `install-pytorch`
  - `check-pytorch`
  - `reinstall-pytorch-cpu-only`
  - `fine-tune-start`

- **Enhanced error messages**: Detailed diagnostics showing drives scanned, paths tested, and actionable installation instructions

## Example

Before: Generic ENOENT errors with no drive context
```
Debug: tried python (spawn python ENOENT), python3 (spawn python3 ENOENT)
```

After: Full scan report with troubleshooting
```
Detection Summary:
  python: ✗ spawn python ENOENT
  Available drives: C, D, E
  Tested 3 installations on D:, none valid
  
Installation options:
1. Download from python.org
2. Install via Microsoft Store
3. Install via winget
```

title: bench: ctypes-load librac_dsp_core.so — real RAC-DSP per-op timing
body:
Before: the "RAC-DSP C ref" row in bench_three_paths.py shelled out to sim/rac_systolic_ref, so the 0.71 ms/call measurement was dominated by fork/exec/mmap overhead. Totally fine as a correctness oracle (just counts wall-clock), useless as a perf data point.

After: a new ctypes path loads sim/librac_dsp_core.so directly and calls rac_dsp_eval / rac_dsp_project_batch / rac_dsp_project_sum in process. Real per-rotation timing with ~100 ns of Python↔C ffi overhead instead of ~1 ms of subprocess spawn.

New batch helpers in rac_dsp_core.{h,c}
  rac_dsp_project_batch(n, xs, zs, xout) — N project evaluations in a
    tight C loop, no ctypes boundary crossing per call. Used for the
    "CORDIC floor" measurement.
  rac_dsp_project_sum(n, xs, zs) -> sum — one column of the systolic
    array in C. Python outer loop calls this N times for an N×N GEMM.

New Makefile target
  make lib          → builds librac_dsp_core.so (-fPIC -shared)

Bench additions
  load_rac_dsp_lib()              → ctypes-load, declare prototypes,
                                    call rac_load_all_roms on the
                                    standard cordic_*.mem files
  time_glu_project_single()       → ns/rotation with one ctypes call
                                    per rotation (boundary-dominated)
  time_glu_project_batch(n=4096)  → ns/rotation inside the C loop
                                    (CORDIC floor)
  time_glu_empirical_sgemm_inproc → ms/call + GFLOPS for N×N×N matmul
                                    using rac_dsp_project_sum per col

Header now prints both numbers up-front so the user sees where the Python-to-C transition tax sits:
  GLU:  8-engine bank @ 200 MHz = 0.62 ns/op
        single rac_dsp_eval  =  1022.9 ns/rotation (ctypes round-trip)
        batch  rac_dsp_eval  =    56.5 ns/rotation (tight C, CORDIC floor)

The SGEMM row now emits:
  RAC-DSP C ref (16³):        0.02 ms/call        0.3 GFLOPS
                              (bit-exact rac_dsp.v mirror via librac_dsp_core.so)

Before commit in sandbox: 0.71 ms/call, 0.0 GFLOPS (subprocess noise)
After  commit in sandbox: 0.02 ms/call, 0.3 GFLOPS (meaningful software floor)

Order-of-magnitude faster, orders-of-magnitude more honest.

Context for the 56 ns/rotation number:
  Each rac_dsp_eval runs 10 coarse + 9 residual = 19 shift-add
  iterations on 64-bit ints. At ~3 ns per iter on a 5950X (L1
  hot, no branching), minimum floor is ~60 ns. 56 ns measured
  means the C code is near that wall.
  RTL silicon target: 1 rotation/cycle @ 200 MHz = 5 ns/rotation
  per engine, with throughput scaling by # engines. Software vs
  hardware gap ~11× per engine — which is the whole point.

https://claude.ai/code/session_016cmjHufxniVCTANADYe788

title: feat: framework-agnostic GPU detection via ctypes (replaces torch.cuda + nvidia-smi)
body:
## Summary

- **GPU detection via CUDA Driver API** — `detect_gpu()` uses `cuInit`, `cuDeviceGetCount`, `cuDeviceGetName` via ctypes. No torch import required in the core framework.
- **GPU metrics via NVML** — `get_gpu_metrics()` uses `nvmlDeviceGetUtilizationRates`, `nvmlDeviceGetMemoryInfo` via ctypes. Replaces `nvidia-smi` subprocess calls (faster, no PATH dependency).
- **GPU library preloading** — Preloads `libcuda.so.1` and `libnvidia-ml.so.1` with `RTLD_GLOBAL` via absolute paths before any framework import. Fixes CUDA init failures on GKE where `LD_LIBRARY_PATH` is not set at process startup.
- **Zero torch imports in core** — `openfilter/filter_runtime/` no longer imports torch. Individual filters still import torch in their own `setup()`.
- **Secondary torch validation** — When torch is installed, `_validate_device()` also checks `torch.cuda.is_available()` to catch CPU-only wheels or incompatible CUDA runtimes.
- **OPENFILTER_FORK=1 fix** — Now explicitly forces fork mode (previously only skipped spawn, relying on platform default).

## New file: `openfilter/filter_runtime/gpu.py`

| Function | Replaces | API |
|---|---|---|
| `preload_gpu_libs()` | `_preload_gpu_libs()` in filter.py | ctypes.CDLL with RTLD_GLOBAL |
| `detect_gpu()` → `GPUInfo` | `torch.cuda.is_available()`, `torch.cuda.device_count()` | CUDA Driver API |
| `get_gpu_metrics()` → `list[GPUMetrics]` | `subprocess.run(['nvidia-smi', ...])` | NVML |
| `nvml_shutdown()` | N/A | Clean NVML state on exit |

## Why

The openfilter framework should not depend on torch for GPU detection. Filters using TensorFlow, ONNX Runtime, or custom CUDA code shouldn't need torch installed just to detect GPUs.

## Review fixes (v0.1.28)

- `_find_gpu_lib` no longer returns a bare name fallback - returns `None`, callers use `path or "libcuda.so.1"` to try the bare name
- `set_start_method` moved from import-time side effect to `_configure_start_method()` called at top of `cmd_run()`
- `NvmlUtilization`/`NvmlMemory` ctypes structures moved to module level (avoid recreation per call)
- `preload_gpu_libs()` now populates `_libcuda`/`_libnvml` cache to avoid double-loading

## Tests

34 tests covering GPU detection, NVML metrics, preloading, and device validation (including driver-present-but-torch-unavailable scenarios).

## Test plan

- [x] 34 GPU tests pass
- [x] 379 other tests pass (9 pre-existing failures unrelated)
- [x] ruff check + format clean
- [ ] Deploy to GKE and verify torch.cuda.is_available() == True
- [ ] Verify GPU metrics in Grafana dashboard (NVML vs old nvidia-smi values)

title: perf(phlower): call gc.collect + malloc_trim after eviction
body:
**Problem**

Python (CPython) uses glibc's memory allocator on Linux. When objects like invocation records are freed, glibc doesn't automatically return that memory to the OS — it keeps the pages around in "arenas" for future allocations. With phlower churning through ~100K invocation objects continuously (evict old, create new), RSS keeps climbing even though live data stays constant. This is a well-known CPython behavior called arena fragmentation.

**Fix**

After each eviction pass, call `gc.collect()` to free unreachable Python objects, then call glibc's `malloc_trim(0)` via ctypes to ask the allocator to return unused pages to the OS. This is a standard pattern for long-running Python services.

Only activates on Linux (glibc). No-op on macOS or musl-based images.

* **重构**

- Migrate 8 MoE ASM kernel functions from pybind11 (`torch::Tensor&`) to C ABI (`AiterTensor*` + `hipStream_t`) called via ctypes
- Functions migrated: `fmoe`, `fmoe_int8_g1u0`, `fmoe_g1u1`, `fmoe_g1u1_tkw1`, `fmoe_int8_g1u0_a16`, `fmoe_g1u1_a16`, `fmoe_fp8_blockscale_g1u1`, `moe_stage1_g1u1`

title: Add ctypes C-ABI error bridging to prevent worker crashes during kern…
body:
…el tuning

AITER_CHECK and HIP_CALL now throw std::runtime_error instead of calling std::terminate()/exit(0), so exceptions can be caught at the C-ABI boundary.

New header aiter_ctypes_error.h provides:
- AITER_CTYPES_ERROR_DEF: per-TU thread-local error storage + ABI version probe
- AITER_CTYPES_DEFINE_ENTRYPOINT_VOID: macro that generates extern "C" int wrapper with automatic try/catch bridging (developer writes normal function body)
- aiter_safe_call: template that catches C++ exceptions, stores in TLS, returns -1

Python side (core.py) probes each .so for aiter_ctypes_abi_version to auto-detect the new int-returning convention and raises RuntimeError on failure.

This is mainly for tuning, not terminating the process when there is checking error.

- Split `asm_topksoftmax.cu` out of `module_moe_asm` into a new `module_moe_topksoftmax_asm` module

title: Add `__callback__` property to weakref type
body:
RustPython's `weakref` type was missing the `__callback__` read-only property, blocking PyO3 weakref compatibility (e.g. `reference.getattr("__callback__")` fails).

### Changes

- **`crates/vm/src/object/core.rs`**: Add `get_callback()` to `PyWeak` — reads the callback field under the stripe lock using the same double-check pattern as `upgrade()`
- **`crates/vm/src/builtins/weakref.rs`**: Expose `__callback__` as a read-only `#[pygetset]`
- **`Lib/test/test_weakref.py`**: Remove `expectedFailure` from `test_callback_attribute` and `test_callback_attribute_after_deletion`
- **`extra_tests/snippets/stdlib_weakref.py`**: Add regression coverage

```python
>>> import weakref
>>> class C: pass
>>> o = C()
>>> weakref.ref(o).__callback__ is None
True
>>> cb = lambda r: None
>>> weakref.ref(o, cb).__callback__ is cb
True
```

Semantics match CPython: returns the callback if alive, `None` if no callback was set or referent was collected (callback consumed during clear).

title: Update `test_bz2.py` from 3.14.4
Convert all Python solutions to C++
Python rewrite
Python rewrite: full IMRIS Coil QC pipeline (Phases 1–5 + file move)
body:
## Summary

Full Python implementation of the IMRIS Coil QC pipeline, replacing the MATLAB codebase. Covers DICOM reading, image analysis, SNR computation, drift detection, web backend, and post-processing file management.

## What's included

### Foundation (Phase 1)
- `config.py` — JSON config loader with strict type validation and MD5 provenance hash
- `storage/base.py` — `ResultStore` ABC (`save` / `load_history`)
- `storage/csv_store.py` — CSV backend with composite-key deduplication
- `dicom/parse_patient_name.py` — room + coil identity from PatientName field (e.g. `OR42Bot3Top5`)

### DICOM geometry & grouping (Phase 2)
- `dicom/coords.py` — slice normal, pixel-to-patient coords, angle between normals
- `dicom/group_planes.py` — greedy first-fit orientation clustering
- `dicom/compute_sos.py` — float64 sqrt(sum(I²)) coil combination
- `dicom/read_headers.py` — minimal header reader; coil ID from `(0051,100F)` Siemens private tag (confirmed against real Verio DICOM) with CSA `ImaCoilString` fallback

### Image analysis (Phase 3)
- `analysis/segment_phantom.py` — Otsu + largest central blob + hole fill + closing
- `analysis/estimate_noise.py` — Rayleigh MLE σ = `sqrt(mean(bg²)/2)`; NEMA `std(bg)` stored separately
- `analysis/select_noise_roi.py` — PE-direction ghost smear → 2 cm dilation → distance-transform ellipse placement
- `analysis/coil_signal_metrics.py` — max pixel inside phantom (IO1–IO8); mean inside 80%-eroded ellipse (COMBINED)
- `analysis/summarize_best_per_coil.py` — best-slice selection per coil element

### Drift detection (Phase 4)
- `analysis/compute_qc_deltas.py` — 30-day rolling median baseline, `delta_snr_pct`, `drift_flag` (threshold + absolute floor)

### Orchestration & watcher (Phase 5)
- `pipeline.py` — end-to-end orchestrator with `dry_run` support
- `watcher.py` — `watchdog`-based file watcher with 10 s settle-time heuristic; logs drift flags as warnings
- `main.py` — CLI entry point (`--config`, `--log-level`); NSSM-deployable Windows service

### Web backend & frontend (Phases 6–7)
- `web/auth.py` — Shibboleth SAML 2.0 SSO via `X-Remote-User` header; `IMRIS_DEV_USER` dev bypass
- `web/dicom_router.py` — `GET /api/dicom/{sop_uid}` → 16-bit PNG for Cornerstone.js
- `web/results_router.py` — results list/filter, single row, and `POST /manual-adjustment` (recompute SNR from user-supplied ellipses, insert `row_type=manual` row)
- `web/app.py` — FastAPI factory with CORS + static files
- `web/frontend/index.html` — single-page app: Cornerstone.js DICOM viewer (WW/WL drag), Konva.js editable ellipse ROI overlay, results table with drift flag highlighting, SNR trend chart

### Post-processing file move
- `io/move_series.py` — moves series directory to `PROCESSING_COMPLETED` or `PROCESSING_FAILED` after pipeline finishes; MD5-based collision handling; writes `manifest.json` audit record
- Config: new required fields `completed_dir` and `failed_dir`; `move_files: true` + `dry_run: false` enables moving

## Tests

227 tests, all passing. Key coverage:
- Config validation (type checking, bool/int distinction, missing fields)
- CSV store deduplication and history windowing
- PatientName regex parsing
- DICOM geometry (slice normal, pixel coords, angle, plane grouping)
- SoS computation
- DICOM header reading with mocked pydicom Dataset
- Phantom segmentation on synthetic images
- Rayleigh noise estimation (MLE accuracy, ratio to std)
- Noise ROI placement (PE exclusion, dilation, semi-axes)
- Signal metrics (max pixel, eroded mean)
- Best-slice selection with NaN SNR handling
- 30-day drift computation (median, delta %, flag logic)
- File move (simple, identical collision, different-content collision, manifest correctness)

## Config changes

```json
{
  "input_dir":     "C:\\IMRIS_Data\\FOR_PROCESSING",
  "completed_dir": "C:\\IMRIS_Data\\PROCESSING_COMPLETED",
  "failed_dir":    "C:\\IMRIS_Data\\PROCESSING_FAILED",
  "output_dir":    "C:\\IMRIS_Data\\output",
  ...
}
```

## Still needed (Phase 8)
- Integration testing with real DICOM files from the Verio
- Calibration of `snr_absolute_floor` default after first real data run
- Shibboleth SP nginx config for production deployment
