title: package/go: security bump to version 1.25.9
body:
Release notes: https://go.dev/doc/devel/release#go1.25.0

This release fixes the following vulnerabilities:
* CVE-2026-32282: os: Root.Chmod can follow symlinks out of the root on Linux
* CVE-2026-32289: html/template: JS template literal context incorrectly tracked
* CVE-2026-27144: cmd/compile: no-op interface conversion bypasses overlap checking
* CVE-2026-27143: cmd/compile: possible memory corruption after bound check elimination
* CVE-2026-32288: archive/tar: unbounded allocation when parsing old format GNU sparse map
* CVE-2026-32283: crypto/tls: multiple key update handshake messages can cause connection to deadlock
* CVE-2026-27140: cmd/go: trust layer bypass when using cgo and SWIG
* CVE-2026-32280: crypto/x509: unexpected work during chain building
* CVE-2026-32281: crypto/x509: inefficient policy validation

Go 1.25.9 also includes bug fixes to the go command, the compiler, and the runtime.

For more details, see the announcement:
https://groups.google.com/g/golang-announce/c/0uYbvbPZRWU

title: feat: Option C — replace Node server with Vite dev proxy
body:
## Summary

Implements Option C from the architecture review: delete the Node/Express backend entirely and replace it with a Vite dev proxy. Python/FastAPI (`backend/server.py`) is now the only API runtime for both dev and production.

**Dev topology:**
```
browser → Vite :5173 (HMR) → /api proxy → Python FastAPI :8000
```
**Prod topology (unchanged):**
```
browser → nginx :80 → /api proxy → Python FastAPI :8000 (internal)
```

## What changed

| | Before | After |
|---|---|---|
| Dev server | `tsx server/index.ts` (Express + Vite middleware) | `vite` (standalone) |
| Build | `tsx script/build.ts` (esbuild server + vite client) | `vite build` |
| API in dev | Node/Express (MemStorage, demo data) | Python FastAPI (SQLite) |
| API in prod | Python FastAPI | Python FastAPI (unchanged) |

**Deleted:** `server/` (7 files), `script/build.ts`, `drizzle.config.ts`

**Modified:**
- `shared/schema.ts` → plain TS interfaces (no drizzle-orm dependency); client `import type` unchanged
- `vite.config.ts` → `/api` proxy to `localhost:8000`, dropped unused `@assets` alias
- `package.json` → simplified scripts; removed ~15 server-only dependencies (`express`, `passport`, `pg`, `drizzle-*`, `@anthropic-ai/sdk`, `ws`, etc.)
- `tsconfig.json` → removed `server/**/*` from includes, dropped `"node"` type
- `docker-compose.override.yml` → new file; exposes `backend:8000` → `localhost:8000` for local dev

## Local dev workflow after this PR

```bash
# Terminal 1 — start Python backend
docker compose up backend

# Terminal 2 — start Vite with HMR
npm install   # first time after dep cleanup
npm run dev
```

## Test plan

- [ ] `npm run build` produces a clean Vite build with no TS errors
- [ ] `docker compose up --build` starts both services as before
- [ ] `npm run dev` + `docker compose up backend` — all `/api/*` calls proxy correctly to Python
- [ ] Frontend pages (Dashboard, Activities, Trends, Reports, Settings) load and fetch data

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

title: Add GnuCash Python bindings debugging guide and utilities
body:
- Add docs/DEBUGGING_GNUCASH_BINDINGS.md with practical workflow for debugging GnuCash Python SWIG vs ctypes issues across platforms
- Add infrastructure/gnucash/engine.py utilities:
  - GList structure class for safe traversal of GnuCash C API lists
  - iterate_glist() to replace raw pointer arithmetic with safe iteration
  - safe_ctypes_string() for null-safe string decoding
  - verify_ctypes_functions() for runtime validation of required C functions
- Update use_cases/export_business_objects.py to use new utilities:
  - Fix callback signature mismatch that prevented tax table export
  - Replace manual GList traversal with iterate_glist()
  - Replace manual string decoding with safe_ctypes_string()
- Update services/invoice_renderer.py similarly for consistency
- Update CLAUDE.md with hard-won platform findings:
  - Always set argtypes for 64-bit pointer safety
  - RTLD_LOCAL vs RTLD_GLOBAL differences (Debian vs Ubuntu)
  - Reading vs writing asymmetry in GnuCash bindings reliability
  - Pointer lifetime rules when mixing SWIG and ctypes

These changes address platform-specific bugs discovered during comprehensive testing across all 6 supported Linux distributions (Debian 11/12/13, Ubuntu 20/22/24). The pragmatic "try SWIG first, then ctypes" workflow is now documented and supported by reusable utilities that make ctypes usage safer and more maintainable.

title: [Feat] swiGLU
body:
>This PR is additive only: it introduces new files without changing any existing ones.

# swiGLU PTO-ISA vs torch_npu

## msprof on simulator
|PTO-ISA|torch_npu|
|---------|-----------|
|![PTO-ISA](https://github.com/Mocchibird/pto-kernels-plots/blob/main/swiglu/swiglu_pto-isa.png?raw=true)|![torch_npu](https://github.com/Mocchibird/pto-kernels-plots/blob/main/swiglu/swiglu_torch.png?raw=true)|

## msprof on device
|PTO-ISA|torch_npu|
|---------|-----------|
|![PTO-ISA](https://github.com/Mocchibird/pto-kernels-plots/blob/main/swiglu/swiglu_pto-isa_on_device.png?raw=true)|![torch_npu](https://github.com/Mocchibird/pto-kernels-plots/blob/main/swiglu/swiglu_torch_on_device.png?raw=true)|

PTO-ISA is around `24% faster` than torch_npu for simulated profiling and `13% faster`for on device profiling.

## python benchmark
|Speedup jit-compiler/ctypes vs torch_npu|Speedup cmake/pybind vs torch_npu|
|-----------|----|
|![Speedup ctypes](https://github.com/Mocchibird/pto-kernels-plots/blob/main/swiglu/swiglu_speedup_heatmap_bd24.png?raw=true)|![Speedup pybind](https://github.com/Mocchibird/pto-kernels-plots/blob/main/swiglu/swiglu_speedup_heatmap_bd24_pybind.png?raw=true)|

The benchmark with pybind has `1.5x` the speed of torch_npu, while the benchmark with jit-compiler has `0.78x` the speed of torch_npu.
Most of the slowdown for the jit-compiler version comes from repeated stream_ptr lookup. Caching the stream_ptr speeds-up the jit-compiler version significantly for smaller shapes, but not much for larger shapes.

|Speedup jit-compiler/ctypes w. cached stream_ptr vs torch_npu|
|---|
|![Speedup ctypes cached stream_ptr](https://github.com/Mocchibird/pto-kernels-plots/blob/main/swiglu/swiglu_speedup_heatmap_bd24_cached_stream_ptr.png?raw=true)|

title: feat: c like lang
body:
## Summary

Adds **C, Go, and Java** as first-class `sugar-high/presets`, introduces optional **`typeKeywords`** so built-in types use the existing `class` token (distinct from `keyword`), expands docs/tests, and trims the homepage carousel so non-web samples live next to the preset examples.

## Core

- **`HighlightOptions.typeKeywords`**: optional `Set<string>`; matched before `keywords`, emitted as the `class` token type (same slot themes already use for type-like highlighting).

## Presets (`sugar-high/presets`)

- New: **`c`**, **`go`**, **`java`** with shared **`clike-base`** comment rules (`//`, `/* */`).
- **`typeKeywords`** for built-in / primitive-style tokens; remaining language words stay in **`keywords`**.

## Docs

- **Carousel**: removed long Python / C / Java cards; keeps web-oriented stack (Rust, CSS, JS literals, TS).
- **Install banner**: preset mapping example, **C / Java / Python** sample blocks with comments and spacing; samples use **`highlight(code, preset)` + Codice `asMarkup`** so highlighting matches sugar-high (Codice’s built-in extension map only wires css/py/rs today).
- **`syntax-highlight-presets`**: adds `c`, `go`, `java` for the live editor extension picker where applicable.

## Tests

- Preset tests for **c**, **go**, **java**; **css** regression; **`typeKeywords`** unit test in `tokenize.test.ts`.

## Remark plugin

- Unchanged behavior: fence language must match an **exported preset name** exactly (e.g. ` ```c ` → `presets.c`).

title: feat: add pto.tquant op with INT8_SYM/ASYM quantization support
body:
Adds `pto.tquant`, a DPS tile op that quantizes an `f32` tile into `i8`/`ui8` using a scaling parameter tile. Implements the pto-isa `TQUANT` intrinsic across all compiler layers.

- New `QuantType` enum attr with two modes:
  - `INT8_SYM` — 2-operand form: `ins(%src, %fp)`, `i8` dst
  - `INT8_ASYM` — 3-operand form: `ins(%src, %fp, %offset)`, `ui8` dst
- Custom assembly format handles the optional `offset` operand
- Verifier enforces mode-operand consistency:
  - `INT8_SYM` rejects offset, requires `i8` dst
  - `INT8_ASYM` requires offset (`f32`), requires `ui8` dst
- A2/A3 verifier enforces row-major layout; A5 is layout-agnostic
- EmitC emits `TQUANT<pto::QuantType::INT8_SYM/ASYM, DstTile, SrcTile, FpTile>(dst, src, fp[, &offset])`

## Test plan

- [x] `ptoas --pto-arch=a3 test/basic/tquant.pto` — FileCheck passes (INT8_SYM and INT8_ASYM with offset)
- [x] `ptoas --pto-arch=a5 test/basic/tquant.pto` — FileCheck passes
- [x] `ptoas --pto-arch=(a3/a5) test/basic/tqaunt_verify_invalid.pto` outputs:
```bash
'pto.tquant' op INT8_ASYM quantization requires an offset operand
'pto.tquant' op INT8_SYM quantization must not have an offset operand
'pto.tquant' op expects dst element type i8 for INT8_SYM quantization
'pto.tquant' op expects dst element type ui8 for INT8_ASYM quantization
```
- [x] Generated C++ compiles with bisheng against pto-isa headers (`dav-2201`)
- [x] End-to-end numerical accuracy verified on A3 hardware (INT8_SYM) via `test/basic/tquant_e2e.pto`:

| src | scale | expected i8 | result |
|-----|-------|-------------|--------|
| 4.0 | 0.25 | 1 | ✅ |
| 2.0 | 0.50 | 1 | ✅ |
| 10.0 | 1.00 | 10 | ✅ |
| 127.0 | 1.00 | 127 | ✅ |
| -4.0 | 0.25 | -1 | ✅ |
| 0.0 | 1.00 | 0 | ✅ |

- [x] End-to-end numerical accuracy verified on A3 hardware (INT8_ASYM) via `test/basic/tquant_e2e.pto`:

| src | scale | offset | expected ui8 | result |
|-----|-------|--------|--------------|--------|
| 4.0 | 0.25 | 0.0 | 1 | ✅ |
| 10.0 | 1.00 | 0.0 | 10 | ✅ |
| 4.0 | 1.00 | 10.0 | 14 | ✅ |
| 200.0 | 1.00 | 0.0 | 200 | ✅ |
| 255.0 | 1.00 | 0.0 | 255 | ✅ |
| 0.0 | 1.00 | 0.0 | 0 | ✅ |

> **Note:** multi-function `.pto` modules require explicit `pto.entry` on each kernel — without it only a single-function module is auto-detected as an entry and gets `__global__ AICORE`. Both functions in `tquant_e2e.pto` are annotated accordingly.

To reproduce:
```bash
build/tools/ptoas/ptoas \
  --pto-arch=a3 \
  --enable-insert-sync \
  test/basic/tquant_e2e.pto \
  -o /mounted_home/tquant_e2e_a3.cpp

bisheng \
  -I/sources/pto-isa/include \
  -I/sources/pto-isa/include/pto \
  -fPIC -shared -O2 -std=c++17 \
  -xcce -Xhost-start -Xhost-end \
  --npu-arch=dav-2201 -DMEMORY_BASE \
  /mounted_home/tquant_caller.cpp \
  -o /mounted_home/tquant_e2e_a3.so

python test_quantop.py
```

Scripts used for testing:
- `tquant_caller.cpp`
```cpp
#include "tquant_e2e_a3.cpp"

extern "C" void call_tquant_sym(
    void *stream,
    uint8_t *src,
    uint8_t *fp,
    uint8_t *dst)
{
    tquant_sym_run<<<20, nullptr, stream>>>(
        reinterpret_cast<float *>(src),
        reinterpret_cast<float *>(fp),
        reinterpret_cast<int8_t *>(dst));
}

extern "C" void call_tquant_asym(
    void *stream,
    uint8_t *src,
    uint8_t *fp,
    uint8_t *offset,
    uint8_t *dst)
{
    tquant_asym_run<<<20, nullptr, stream>>>(
        reinterpret_cast<float *>(src),
        reinterpret_cast<float *>(fp),
        reinterpret_cast<float *>(offset),
        reinterpret_cast<uint8_t *>(dst));
}

```
- `test_quantop.py`
```python
import ctypes
import torch
import torch_npu

device = "npu:0"
torch.npu.set_device(device)

lib = ctypes.CDLL("/mounted_home/tquant_e2e_a3.so")

fn_sym = lib.call_tquant_sym
fn_sym.argtypes = [ctypes.c_void_p, ctypes.c_void_p, ctypes.c_void_p, ctypes.c_void_p]
fn_sym.restype = None

fn_asym = lib.call_tquant_asym
fn_asym.argtypes = [ctypes.c_void_p, ctypes.c_void_p, ctypes.c_void_p, ctypes.c_void_p, ctypes.c_void_p]
fn_asym.restype = None

def run_sym(src_val, scale_val):
    src = torch.full((32, 32), src_val, dtype=torch.float32, device=device)
    fp  = torch.full((32, 32), scale_val, dtype=torch.float32, device=device)
    dst = torch.full((32, 32), -1, dtype=torch.int8, device=device)
    stream_ptr = torch.npu.current_stream()._as_parameter_
    torch.npu.synchronize()
    fn_sym(stream_ptr,
           ctypes.c_void_p(src.data_ptr()),
           ctypes.c_void_p(fp.data_ptr()),
           ctypes.c_void_p(dst.data_ptr()))
    torch.npu.synchronize()
    return dst

def run_asym(src_val, scale_val, offset_val):
    src    = torch.full((32, 32), src_val,    dtype=torch.float32, device=device)
    fp     = torch.full((32, 32), scale_val,  dtype=torch.float32, device=device)
    offset = torch.full((32, 32), offset_val, dtype=torch.float32, device=device)
    dst    = torch.full((32, 32), 255,        dtype=torch.uint8,   device=device)
    stream_ptr = torch.npu.current_stream()._as_parameter_
    torch.npu.synchronize()
    fn_asym(stream_ptr,
            ctypes.c_void_p(src.data_ptr()),
            ctypes.c_void_p(fp.data_ptr()),
            ctypes.c_void_p(offset.data_ptr()),
            ctypes.c_void_p(dst.data_ptr()))
    torch.npu.synchronize()
    return dst

sym_cases = [
    # (src,    scale,  expected_i8)
    (4.0,    0.25,    1),    # 4.0 * 0.25 = 1.0
    (2.0,    0.5,     1),    # 2.0 * 0.5  = 1.0
    (10.0,   1.0,    10),    # 10.0 * 1.0 = 10.0
    (127.0,  1.0,   127),    # max i8
    (-4.0,   0.25,   -1),    # -4.0 * 0.25 = -1.0
    (0.0,    1.0,     0),    # zero
]

asym_cases = [
    # (src,    scale,  offset,  expected_ui8)   expected = round(src * scale + offset)
    (4.0,    0.25,    0.0,    1),    # 4.0 * 0.25 + 0.0 = 1.0
    (10.0,   1.0,     0.0,   10),    # 10.0 * 1.0 + 0.0 = 10.0
    (4.0,    1.0,    10.0,   14),    # 4.0 * 1.0 + 10.0 = 14.0
    (200.0,  1.0,     0.0,  200),    # large positive
    (255.0,  1.0,     0.0,  255),    # max ui8
    (0.0,    1.0,     0.0,    0),    # zero
]

all_passed = True

print("=== INT8_SYM ===")
for src_val, scale_val, expected in sym_cases:
    dst = run_sym(src_val, scale_val)
    ok = (dst == expected).all()
    all_passed = all_passed and ok
    print(f"src={src_val:6.1f} scale={scale_val:.2f} -> expected={expected:4d}  got={dst[0,0].item():4d}  {'PASS' if ok else 'FAIL'}")

print()
print("=== INT8_ASYM ===")
for src_val, scale_val, offset_val, expected in asym_cases:
    dst = run_asym(src_val, scale_val, offset_val)
    ok = (dst == expected).all()
    all_passed = all_passed and ok
    print(f"src={src_val:6.1f} scale={scale_val:.2f} offset={offset_val:.1f} -> expected={expected:4d}  got={dst[0,0].item():4d}  {'PASS' if ok else 'FAIL'}")

print()
print("ALL PASSED" if all_passed else "SOME TESTS FAILED")

```

title: feat(runtime): add type-safe uint64_t conversion utilities (to_u64/from_u64)
body:
## Summary
- Add `to_u64<T>()` / `from_u64<T>()` template functions to `data_type.h` for type-safe packing/unpacking of values into `uint64_t`, the universal scalar storage type in the orchestration framework
- Template-ize `add_scalar`, `add_output` (with initial value), `get_tensor_data`, and `set_tensor_data` with default `T=uint64_t` for full backward compatibility
- Remove duplicated hand-written `float_to_u64` / `u64_to_float` union helpers from orchestration files
- Extend Python `make_scalar_arg` to accept native `float` values
- Scope: a2a3 tensormap_and_ringbuffer runtime; other runtimes verified compatible

## Key Changes
- `data_type.h`: Add `to_u64<T>()` and `from_u64<T>()` — memcpy-based, strict-aliasing compliant, zero-overhead
- `task_arg.h`: `value_as<T>()` now delegates to `from_u64<T>()` (implementation unification)
- `pto_types.h`: `add_scalar` and `add_output` (with initial value) template-ized
- `pto_orchestration_api.h`: `get_tensor_data` / `set_tensor_data` template-ized
- `example_orchestration.cpp`, `scalar_data_orch.cpp`: Remove `float_to_u64`/`u64_to_float`, use typed API directly
- `task_interface.py`: `make_scalar_arg(1.0)` now works (IEEE 754 single-precision bit-cast)
- `test_task_interface.py`: Add `test_make_scalar_arg_native_float`

title: Feat: add ChipWorker execution interface, replace bindings.py
body:
## Summary

- Introduce `ChipWorker` (C++ via nanobind) that encapsulates the 6-step runtime C API (`dlopen`, `set_device`, `init_runtime`, `enable_profiling`, `launch_runtime`, `finalize_runtime`) into `init()`/`run()`/`reset()`
- New `CallConfig` struct for per-call parameters (`block_dim`, `aicpu_thread_num`, `orch_thread_num`, `enable_profiling`)
- `code_runner.py` migrated from `bindings.py` ctypes layer to `ChipWorker`
- `kernel_compiler.py` simplified: removed dead C++ toolchain query path, uses platform map directly
- `python/bindings.py` deleted — ctypes layer fully replaced by nanobind ChipWorker
- Fixed profiling ordering bug: `enable_profiling` now called after `init_runtime` (placement new was overwriting the flag)
- Fixed CI `detect-changes`: now catches shared code changes (`src/common/`, `python/`, `examples/scripts/`, build files) that affect A5 hardware tests

body:

## Summary

- **Clipboard paste never worked on Windows**: `_clipboard_windows` used raw ctypes calls without setting `restype`/`argtypes` — on 64-bit Windows, handle return values were truncated from 64 to 32 bits, causing silent clipboard write failure. Now sets proper types and raises `OSError` on failure.
- **Hotkey shown as "cmd" on Windows**: UI dropdowns, daemon logs, and setup wizard all displayed the raw internal key name `ctrl+cmd` instead of `Ctrl + Win`. Added `format_hotkey_display()` that maps to platform-native labels (Win on Windows, Cmd on macOS, Option for Alt on macOS).

title: Fix: Resolve Linux startup crashes by decoupling window logic
body:
This pull request addresses critical crashes that prevented the FlexiTools application from launching natively on Linux.

**Changes Made:**
1. **DPI Awareness Guard:** Added an OS check to `main.py` so that the Windows-specific `ctypes.windll.shcore.SetProcessDpiAwareness(1)` call is completely bypassed on Linux.
2. **Decoupling Window Management:** Extracted the custom borderless window styling and native window dragging code from `ui.py` into a new, dedicated `window_utils.py` file.
3. **Graceful Fallbacks:** The new `window_utils.py` securely encapsulates the `ctypes` imports using `try...except ImportError`, ensuring that `ui.py` can import these functions safely across platforms. On Linux, it now falls back to standard Tkinter behaviors (`overrideredirect(True)` and manual dragging logic) rather than raising fatal `AttributeError` exceptions.

**Testing:**
- Verified via `xvfb-run python main.py` on Linux that the application successfully initializes and loads the main UI. 
- Windows functionality remains completely unchanged and has exact feature parity.

---

title: fix(python): use native ARM64 precompiled Python on Windows ARM
body:
Hello there! 👋 

First and foremost, thank you for creating and actively developing `mise`! 😃 I discovered it through [this HackerNews post](https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=45491621) and I've used it for a personal project since then. By the way, I'm genuinely impressed by how many releases you ship every now and then 👍

Today, I wanted to run my python project on Windows ARM64, and this happened:

```
 DEBUG using lockfile URL for platform windows-arm64: https://github.com/astral-sh/python-build-standalone/releases/download/20260324/cpython-3.13.12+20260324-x86_64-pc-windows-msvc-install_only_stripped.tar.gz
  INFO  python@3.13.12       [2/4] download cpython-3.13.12+20260324-x86_64-pc-windows-msvc-install_only_stripped.tar.gz
```

I saw the resulting failure when trying to load an ARM64 DLL from the downloaded x86_64 Python:

```
  >>> import ctypes; ctypes.CDLL(r"C:\PATH\TO\ARM64\DLL\engine.dll")
  OSError: [WinError 193] %1 is not a valid Win32 application
```

This fix change the downloaded python into: 

```
  DEBUG GET https://mise-versions.jdx.dev/tools/python-precompiled-aarch64-pc-windows-msvc.gz
  INFO  python@3.13.12       [2/4] download cpython-3.13.12+20260324-aarch64-pc-windows-msvc-install_only_stripped.tar.gz
```

title: fix: correct INPUT struct size causing mouse freeze on Windows
body:
## Problem

Running the packaged app on Windows causes the mouse to freeze/become unresponsive.

## Root Cause

### Bug 1: `INPUT` struct size mismatch in `_send_ctrl_c()`

The `INPUT` union in `acquisition.py` only contained `KEYBDINPUT`, making `sizeof(INPUT)` **32 instead of the correct 40 bytes** on 64-bit Windows. When `SendInput(4, inputs, sizeof(INPUT))` read the 4-element array with the wrong stride, it interpreted garbage bytes as `MOUSEINPUT` events, injecting random mouse movements/clicks and causing the cursor to freeze.

This bug triggers whenever text acquisition falls back to the clipboard strategy (Strategy 3), which is common in modern applications where UIA and Win32Msg strategies fail.

### Bug 2: Unnecessary work in mouse hook callback

`_handle_mouse_event()` performed `ctypes.cast()` on **every** mouse event (including hundreds of `WM_MOUSEMOVE` per second), but only needed to handle `WM_LBUTTONDOWN` and `WM_LBUTTONUP`. The excess GIL contention could cause Windows to silently remove the hook due to timeout.

## Fix

1. **`acquisition.py`**: Added `MOUSEINPUT` and `HARDWAREINPUT` to the `INPUT` union so the struct size matches the Windows SDK definition.
2. **`hooks.py`**: Added early return for irrelevant mouse messages before the `ctypes.cast()` call.

title: docs(sdk): FFI performance findings — boundary-crossing overhead across SDKs
body:
## What

Adds `sdk/FFI_PERFORMANCE.md` documenting measured FFI boundary-crossing overhead across Rust, Python, and JavaScript SDKs.

## Key Findings

The pure cost of crossing from a host language into Rust and back:

| | req_transformer | res_transformer | Total per call |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| **JavaScript** (koffi) | +0.32ms | +0.69ms | **+1.01ms** |
| **Python** (ctypes) | +0.31ms | +1.47ms | **+1.78ms** |

- FFI overhead is **0.2–0.3%** of total round-trip time
- Request side is identical across languages (~0.3ms) — payloads are small
- Response side diverges — Python ctypes marshals large byte buffers ~2× slower than koffi
- Measured against live Stripe sandbox with production-shaped payloads
- Timers placed inside ConnectorClient, not the test harness

title: Benchmax: hybrid search + 8x faster numpy insert + 1.8x faster than Zvec
body:
## Summary
Vectors as a column in Collection + hybrid text+vector search + numpy zero-copy insert.

## Insert Speed (d=128, N=10K)
| Method | Speed | vs JSON |
|---|---|---|
| JSON + extract embedding | 11.6K/s | 1x |
| ctypes array FFI | 59.5K/s | 5.1x |
| **numpy zero-copy** | **91.4K/s** | **7.9x** |

## Hybrid Search
- `col.configure_vectors(dims=1536, field="embedding")`
- `col.insert_with_embedding(key, text, np_embedding)` — numpy zero-copy
- `col.search_hybrid("query", embedding, text_weight=0.3, vector_weight=0.7)`
- Text pre-filter eliminates 99% → vector re-rank on survivors only

## vs Zvec (d=3072, N=100K)
- Search: TurboDB 22.5ms vs Zvec 40.5ms (**1.8x faster**)
- Insert: TurboDB 299K/s vs Zvec 17K/s (**18x faster**)

## Changes
- `src/collection.zig`: vectors field, configureVectors, insertWithEmbedding, searchVectors, searchHybrid, extractJsonFloatArray, fastParseFloat, parallel enableInt8
- `src/ffi.zig`: turbodb_insert_with_embedding, turbodb_configure_vectors, turbodb_search_hybrid
- `python/turbodb/__init__.py`: _float_ptr (numpy zero-copy), insert_with_embedding, search_hybrid
- `python/turbodb/_ffi.py`: ctypes prototypes

title: feat: integrate unified message framing for C++ and Python
body:
## Summary
This PR completes the functional gap between C++ and Python by integrating the messaging framing system (LineFramer, PacketFramer) across all transport layers. It enables users to handle completely framed payloads instead of raw byte fragments, providing a consistent "message-based" experience in both C++ and Python.

## Changes
- **C++ Core Enhancements**:
  - Extended `ChannelInterface` and `ServerInterface` to support `set_framer()` and `on_message()`.
  - Implemented per-session framer management in `TcpServer` and `UdsServer` using a factory pattern.
  - Refactored `BuilderInterface` to store a `framer_factory_`, allowing builders to generate independent framer instances for multiple connections (critical for servers).
  - Resolved member shadowing and naming conflicts in server builders (renamed to `on_framed_message_`).
- **Python Bindings**:
  - Exposed `LineFramer` and `PacketFramer` classes to `unilink_py`.
  - Added Pythonic helper methods: `use_line_framer()` and `use_packet_framer()` to all transport classes.
  - Exposed `on_message()` callbacks which automatically handle GIL acquisition and byte conversion.
  - Updated Python documentation with comprehensive framing examples.
- **Test Stability**:
  - Improved robustness of flaky transport tests (`BackpressureReporting`, `MaxClientsLimit`, `StopSemantics`, `SendOversizedPacket`) by refining timing, polling, and data volume.
  - Fixed missing header issues in test files (`<thread>`, `test_utils.hpp`).

## Related Issues
- Fixes # (Functional gap between C++ and Python framing APIs)

## Type of Change
- [ ] Bug fix (non-breaking change which fixes an issue)
- [x] New feature (non-breaking change which adds functionality)
- [ ] Breaking change (fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected)
- [x] Documentation update
- [x] Refactoring (no functional changes)
- [x] Test coverage improvement

## Additional Notes
The framing system is now fully "Unified", allowing developers to switch between protocols (TCP, UDS, Serial, UDP) while maintaining consistent message-level processing in both supported languages.

title: feat: introduce native Python asyncio support
body:
## Summary
This PR introduces native Python `asyncio` integration for the `unilink` library. It provides a pure Python wrapper layer that bridges the C++ asynchronous core with the Python event loop, allowing developers to use modern `async/await` syntax for all core transport protocols.

## Changes
- **Python `unilink.asyncio` Package**:
  - Created a new subpackage providing `AsyncTcpClient`, `AsyncUdsClient`, `AsyncUdp`, and `AsyncSerial`.
  - Implemented a thread-safe bridge using `loop.call_soon_threadsafe` to pipe C++ callbacks into `asyncio.Queue`.
  - Added support for asynchronous message framing (Line/Packet) within the `asyncio` wrappers.
- **C++ Transport Enhancements (UDP)**:
  - Fixed a critical blocking issue in `UdpChannel::start()` where it would hang waiting for a state transition that never occurred in connectionless mode.
  - Improved `UdpChannel` to allow immediate data transmission if a remote address is configured, removing the previous restriction of needing to receive a packet first.
  - Refactored `std::promise` handling in the UDP wrapper to prevent potential `std::future_error` or memory corruption in multi-threaded Python environments.
- **Python Binding Improvements**:
  - Ensured proper object ownership in the `asyncio` layer to prevent premature garbage collection of C++ wrapper instances.
- **Documentation**:
  - Added an `asyncio` usage section to the Python Bindings guide (planned/implied by the new package structure).

## Related Issues
- Fixes # (Requirement for modern Python async/await support)

## Type of Change
- [ ] Bug fix (non-breaking change which fixes an issue)
- [x] New feature (non-breaking change which adds functionality)
- [ ] Breaking change (fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected)
- [x] Documentation update
- [x] Refactoring (no functional changes)
- [ ] Performance improvement

## Additional Notes
While TCP and UDS async clients are fully verified and performant, UDP receiving currently faces a bottleneck due to the high-frequency interaction between C++ threads and the Python GIL. This remains a known area for future performance optimization, though the reliability of UDP transmission and initialization has been significantly improved.

This PR adds a complete Python auto-rewrite pipeline that detects Python files and generates rewrites in optimal languages:

- **R Package** (_agents/): Auto-improvement infrastructure
- **C++/DLL** (cpp_linter/): Performance-critical cognitive linter
- **Rust Library** (ust_cache/): Rate limiting and caching
- **Go CLI** (go_cli/): Command-line interface

## Changes

### R Package (r_agents/)
- Agent memory system, configuration, task results
- Gemini LLM service with rate limiting
- Base agent, research agent, improvement cycle
- CLI interface

### C++/DLL (cpp_linter/)
- Cognitive linter API for markdown validation

### Rust Library (rust_cache/)
- Thread-safe rate limiter and cache implementation

### Go CLI (go_cli/)
- Cobra-based CLI for managing improvement cycles

### CI/CD Pipeline
- .github/workflows/python-rewrite-pipeline.yml
- Auto-detects Python files on push/PR
- Weekly scheduled rewrites
- Analyzes code to determine optimal rewrite target

### Scripts
- scripts/detect_python_files.py - Python file detection and analysis

### Config
- config/autolearn-python-rewrite.json - AutoLearn integration

## Language Assignment
- **R**: Auto-improvement infrastructure, data analysis, agents
- **C++/DLL**: Performance-critical parsing, regex, computation
- **Rust**: Systems programming, concurrency, caching
- **Go**: CLI, HTTP services, network programming

title: Add Python conversion of Qt C++ TagLister
body:
Converts `TagLister.h` / `TagLister.cpp` (Qt C++) to idiomatic Python as `convert/TagLister.py`. TagLister scans raw XHTML source and produces an ordered flat list of `TagInfo` records — one per tag in document order — used for structural navigation of XHTML content.

## Key translation decisions

- **`TagInfo` / `AttInfo`** — C++ nested structs become `@dataclass` types
- **Qt string types** — `QString`, `QStringView`, `QStringList` all collapse to `str` / `list[str]`; `QStringView::isNull()` → `is None`
- **`QList` ops** — `<<` / `takeLast()` / `removeLast()` / `last()` → `append()` / `pop()` / `[-1]`
- **`parseAttribute`** — C++ took `AttInfo&` by reference; Python version returns a new `AttInfo` instead
- **Private methods** — C++ `private:` block becomes `_`-prefixed methods
- **Error output** — `qDebug()` → `print(..., file=sys.stderr)`

## Usage

```python
from convert.TagLister import TagLister

lister = TagLister(xhtml_source)
for i in range(lister.size()):
    ti = lister.at(i)
    print(ti.tname, ti.ttype, ti.pos, ti.tpath)

# Attribute extraction
ainfo = TagLister.parseAttribute('<div class="foo">', "class")
# ainfo.avalue == "foo"
```

title: Add PrettyPrinter.py: Python port of Qt C++ PrettyPrinter
body:
Converts `PrettyPrinter.h` / `PrettyPrinter.cpp` to Python, completing the prettyprinter toolchain alongside the existing `TagLister.py` and `XhtmlFormatParser.py`.

## Changes

- **`convert/PrettyPrinter.py`** — new module exposing four static methods mirroring the C++ API:
  - `PrettifyXhtml(source, xfparser)` — main entry point; walks the tag list produced by `TagLister`, resolves per-path formatting rules from `XhtmlFormatParser`, and rebuilds the XHTML string with correct indentation, newlines, and condensed text
  - `trimmed(text, chars)` — strip arbitrary leading/trailing characters (not just whitespace)
  - `condenseText(text)` — collapse newline variants and whitespace runs to a single space
  - `RegexSub(regexp, alt, text, max_count)` — regex substitution with `\N` back-reference expansion, matching the C++ single-digit group semantics

## Notable translation decisions

| C++ | Python |
|---|---|
| `XhtmlFormatParser::ASCEND` | `SortMode.ASCEND` |
| `xfparser.m_gobal_props` | `xfparser.global_props` |
| `xfparser.m_pathPropsCache` | `xfparser.path_props_cache` |
| Qt lambdas (`isSelectorMatchNode`, `calcFinalProps`, `cleanOpenTagText`) | inner closures |
| `CSSInfo` (Sigil-internal CSS reformatter) | optional import with graceful fallback — CSS inside `<style>` is left as-is when unavailable |

The Emmet `\v` cursor-position marker inside `<style>` content is explicitly preserved, matching the C++ behaviour.

```python
from XhtmlFormatParser import XhtmlFormatParser
from PrettyPrinter import PrettyPrinter

xfparser = XhtmlFormatParser(open("prettyprinter.pcss").read())
pretty = PrettyPrinter.PrettifyXhtml(raw_xhtml, xfparser)
```

title: Update Python versions and modernize C++ standards
body:
## Summary
This PR updates the development environment configuration to support modern Python versions and upgrades C/C++ language standards to more recent versions.

## Key Changes

### Python Version Updates
- Updated Python include paths from 3.6m-3.9m to 3.10-3.13 across build tasks and IntelliSense configurations
- Removed deprecated Python 2.7m support
- Updated global Python version from 3.9.6 to 3.12.9 in installation memo
- Removed hardcoded libpython3.6m.so linker references

### C/C++ Standard Upgrades
- Upgraded C standard from c11 to c17
- Upgraded C++ standard from c++14 to c++17
- Applied to both Windows and Linux configurations

### Python Tooling Modernization
- Migrated from deprecated `python.pythonPath` to `python.defaultInterpreterPath`
- Updated linting configuration from legacy `python.linting.*` settings to modern `flake8.enabled` and `flake8.args`
- Updated formatting configuration from `python.formatting.*` to `autopep8.args`
- Added explicit Python formatter configuration with `ms-python.autopep8`
- Added new VS Code extensions: `ms-python.autopep8` and `ms-python.flake8`

### Build Configuration Fixes
- Fixed typos: "Sucess" → "Success" in compiler and linker output messages
- Simplified task naming in launch configurations (removed "linux :" prefix from preLaunchTask references)
- Minor formatting cleanup (trailing whitespace removal)

### Removed Configuration
- Removed obsolete `python.experiments.optOutFrom` setting

title: ci: extract Python smoke test to scripts/smoke-test-python.py
body:
## What

Extract the Python smoke test script from the inline `python -c "..."` block in `python.yml` to `scripts/smoke-test-python.py`, and replace all three per-OS test jobs with `python scripts/smoke-test-python.py`.

## Why

The smoke test body was duplicated verbatim in `test-ubuntu`, `test-macos`, and `test-windows` (introduced in #1481). Any future assertion change required keeping three copies in sync manually. A shared script eliminates the maintenance burden.

## Test results

The script is called via `python scripts/smoke-test-python.py` from each job after `actions/checkout` runs, so the file is present in every runner environment. CI will exercise this on all three platforms.

## Review summary

Low-severity finding from the auto-review of #1481, filed as #1482.

Closes #1482

title: Replace DATA_PTR and Data_Get_Struct by SWIG_ConvertPtr
body:
Mixing ruby functions and SWIG functions to access Ruby/C++ objects is bad practice. It relies on a particular expectation of how SWIG maps C++ objects to Ruby objects.

This commit changes the code to use SWIG functions only and to not use raw Ruby functions to access SWIG mapped objects.

Also change the flag of ConvertPtr to the SWIG provided constant SWIG_POINTER_DISOWN, to make it more clear, that the object will be deleted by the object it was assigned to.

The compiler deprecation warning is re-enabled, since they do no longer appear when built with SWIG with the following patch:
  https://github.com/swig/swig/pull/3326

Fix type conversions of Array\<FXPoint>, Array\<FXRectangle>, Array\<FXSegment> and Array\<FXArc> to raise an error on type mismatch. This wasn't checked before and could lead to invalid memory access.

title: Add f32 namespace to navAggregate
body:
* **Tickets addressed:** ema-gnc-2130
* **Review:** By commit  <!-- Choose from: "by commit", "by file" -->
* **Merge strategy:** Merge (no squash)  <!-- Choose from: "merge (no squash)", "squash and merge" -->

## Description
F32 (single-precision) modules under src/fp32-modules/algorithms/ define C++ types with identical names to their non-F32 counterparts in src/fswAlgorithms/. When both are loaded in the same Python process (e.g., pytest from root), SWIG's global type table — keyed by C++ type name — collides. The last module to swigregister wins the clientdata (Python class), causing property getters on shared types like AggregateAttInput to wrap members with the wrong ReadFunctor type.

The solution is to namespace modules (namespaces throughout xmera is also part of the solution but not something we can do immediately). 

The following are the changes made to accommodate module namespacing
  - Headers (.h): wrapped types in namespace f32 {}
  - Implementations (.cpp): added using namespace f32;
  - C wrappers (_c.cpp): explicit ::f32:: qualification in casts
  - SWIG (.i): using namespace f32; in %{}, %rename directives, qualified %attribute/STRUCTASLIST
  - C++ tests: using namespace f32; in helpers/test files
  - Python tests: no changes needed


## Verification
All test runs as expected.

## Documentation
NA

## Future work
None

title: Add explicit CodeQL workflow to prevent erroneous C/C++ scanning
body:
GitHub's automatic default CodeQL setup incorrectly detected C/C++ in this repo, ran a scan, failed, and left a persistent stuck alert in the Security tab that cannot be dismissed.

## Changes

- **`.github/workflows/codeql.yml`** — New custom CodeQL workflow that explicitly scopes scanning to `javascript-typescript` and `python` only. Runs on push/PR to `main` and weekly (Mondays 03:00 UTC). Uses a matrix strategy so each language is analyzed independently.

This custom workflow overrides GitHub's default setup, preventing any future auto-detection of irrelevant languages.

## Remaining manual step

In **Settings → Code security → Code scanning**, disable "Default setup" to fully deactivate it. Then dismiss the stuck C/C++ alert under **Security → Code scanning alerts → Dismiss → Won't fix**.

title: fix: U-Boot 2024.01 build failures on modern hosts
body:
U-Boot 2024.01 fails to build on modern hosts due to two independent issues in `scripts/build-uboot.sh`.

## `ARCH=arm64` → `ARCH=arm`

U-Boot has no `arch/arm64/` — all ARM targets use `ARCH=arm`. `arm64` is a Linux kernel convention. Passing it caused `asm/config.h` (and everything depending on it) to be unresolvable at compile time.

## `libfdt_wrap.c` incompatible with SWIG ≥ 4.2.0

U-Boot ships a pre-generated `scripts/dtc/pylibfdt/libfdt_wrap.c` produced by an older SWIG. SWIG 4.2.0 added a third `int is_void` argument to `SWIG_Python_AppendOutput()`. On modern hosts the SWIG runtime header declares the 3-argument form, but the 6 call sites in `libfdt_wrap.c` use the old 2-argument form:

```c
// SWIG < 4.2.0 (what libfdt_wrap.c was generated with)
resultobj = SWIG_Python_AppendOutput(resultobj, val);

// SWIG ≥ 4.2.0 (what the host runtime header declares)
resultobj = SWIG_Python_AppendOutput(resultobj, val, 0);
```

A `sed` fixup is added inside the existing patch-stamp guard in `build-uboot.sh` to append `, 0` to each affected call site. The fix is idempotent and runs once per extracted source tree.

title: Create complete MusicManager project (Python backend + C# WPF GUI)
body:
Bootstraps the MusicManager project from an empty repo — a full-stack music library manager with a Python backend exposing a JSON CLI and a WPF frontend consuming it via MVVM.

## Backend (`Backend/`)
- **`main.py`** — entry point; parses args, inits DB/config, routes to modules, all output as JSON to stdout
- **`modules/config.py`** — YAML config singleton; generates `config.yaml` with sane defaults on first run
- **`modules/database.py`** — peewee ORM over SQLite; tables: `tracks`, `artists`, `albums`, `playlists`, `playlist_tracks`, `queue`, `sync_log`
- **`modules/downloader.py`** — spotDL wrapper; downloads to `_Temp/` first; `ThreadPoolExecutor` (max 2); retry ×3 with 300 s backoff
- **`modules/tagger.py`** — enriches tags via MusicBrainz / Last.fm / Genius; embeds lyrics; writes ReplayGain via ffmpeg; backs up original tags before mutation
- **`modules/organizer.py`** — builds library folder structure from config templates; Windows filename sanitization (`<>:"/\|?*`); MD5-based dedup; 180-char max path segment
- **`modules/folder_art.py`** — cover art pipeline (MusicBrainz min 1000 px → iTunes → Spotify); writes `folder.jpg` + `desktop.ini` with correct Hidden/System/ReadOnly attributes
- **`modules/instrumental.py`** — searches Spotify/YouTube for instrumental versions; ±10 s duration tolerance; organizes under `Strumentali/{Album} [Instrumental]/`
- **`modules/playlist.py`** — full playlist CRUD; copies files with `{NN}. {Artist} - {Title}.flac` numbering; maintains `playlist.json` manifest; syncs tags between `Artisti/` and `Playlist/` copies
- **`modules/inbox_watcher.py`** — watchdog on `_Inbox/`; 3 s debounce; subfolder name → playlist destination; clears subfolder after processing
- **`modules/queue_watcher.py`** — parses `queue.txt` (URLs or plain names); writes back `[✅ date]` / `[❌]` status markers
- **`modules/device.py`** — detects TS1802 SD card; FLAC → MP3 320 kbps via ffmpeg; incremental sync (new / updated / removed); reports all four categories
- **`modules/sync.py`** — reads `.spotdl` files from `data/sync/`; diffs against current Spotify playlist state; downloads additions, removes deletions, renumbers on reorder
- **`modules/foobar.py`** — triggers foobar2000 library refresh via its CLI
- **`modules/cli.py`** — `--download`, `--queue`, `--sync`, `--check`, `--fix`, `--stats`, `--search`, `--export-device`, `--retag`, `--playlist-{list,add,remove,reorder}`, `--status`
- **`sync-all.bat` / `sync-playlist.bat` / `export-device.bat`** — convenience wrappers

## GUI (`GUI/`) — C# WPF, net8.0-windows, MVVM
- **Dark theme** defined in `App.xaml` (`#1E1E1E` bg, `#0078D4` accent); applied globally to Button, TextBox, ComboBox, ListView, TabItem
- **`MainWindow`** — 3-tab shell (Download / Playlist / Dispositivo) with bottom status bar
- **`DownloadTab`** — URL/name input; queue `ListView` with status emoji (✅ ⏳ 🕐 ❌), per-item progress bar, Retry button
- **`PlaylistTab`** — playlist selector; drag-drop zone (`AllowDrop=True`, mixed file+folder drops); track list with position, reorder arrows, remove button
- **`DeviceTab`** — device label (`💾 D:\ (TS1802) — 4.2 GB liberi`); playlist checklist with track count + estimated MB; per-playlist export progress
- **`PythonBridge`** — launches `python main.py <args>` as async `Process`; streams JSON stdout line-by-line; exposes `OutputReceived` event
- **`DeviceMonitor`** — WMI `ManagementEventWatcher` for USB insertion/removal; filters by "TS1802" volume label; raises typed `DeviceConnected` / `DeviceDisconnected` events
- **`FileDropHandler`** — static `HandleDrop(DragEventArgs)`; top-level audio file extraction from dropped files/folders; filters `.flac .mp3 .m4a .opus .ogg .wav`

## Config & housekeeping
- `config.yaml` (gitignored) generated on first run with full path/audio/metadata/naming/device/API-key template
- `.gitignore` excludes `config.yaml`, `data/music.db`, `data/music.log`, `data/sync/`, `__pycache__/`, `*.pyc`, `bin/`, `obj/`

title: org-babel python, arm_c
body:
### gitignore: add dot-emacs.el

Should have done it in PR 12.

### execute python in org code blocks

https://orgmode.org/worg/org-contrib/babel/languages/ob-doc-python.html

This requires a virtual environment.

### python3-dev

Some python packages must invoke a C compiler during installation. For example: evdev.

### start.sh: timestamp

With a timestamp in the container name it is possible to run more than one.

### arm_c

Add the ARM GCC toolchain, CMake, Ninja and OpenOCD to develop embedded software in C and C++.

### gitignore: arm-gnu-toolchain

The toolchain archive downloaded from developer.arm.com is not version-controlled.

### cmake-mode

Install the Emacs mode for CMake files.

### render.py: --var arm_gnu_toolchain required for -l arm_c

Add the `--var` argument to `render.py` to pass key-value pairs.

Language `arm_c` requires `arm_gnu_toolchain`.

### bashrc.template: arm_c

Add environment variables used by CMake to build ARM projects.

### start.sh: DOCKER_RUN_OPTIONS

Use getopts to receive the image name and options for `docker run`.

### update readme

Add the `arm_c` language option and instructions to build an image.

title: Feature/camera metadata storage global ctypes
body:
feat(nvdsinfer): improve TensorRT engine file naming and auto-discovery

Problem:
- Engine filenames only included batch size, ignoring input dimensions,
  GPU model, and TensorRT version
- System only looked for engines via model-engine-file config parameter,
  making it effectively obsolete
- Valid existing engines were ignored, causing unnecessary rebuilds
- Changing input size caused infinite rebuild cycles (files overwritten)

Solution:
- New naming format: {model}_b{batch}_i{WxH}_{sm}__{gpu}_{trt}_{precision}.engine
  Example: model_b8_i640x640_sm86_rtx3090_trt10.7_fp16.engine
- Implement 3-step engine discovery: config path → standardized path → rebuild
- Auto-discover existing engines without requiring model-engine-file param
- Include GPU compute capability AND model name for hardware-specific optimization
- Add TensorRT version to prevent cross-version incompatibility
- Automatic fallback to rebuild on any failure (corrupted file, param mismatch)

Benefits:
- Reduces pipeline startup from minutes to seconds when engine exists
- Each configuration gets its own engine file (no overwrites)
- Works across different GPUs with proper identification
- Self-healing: auto-rebuilds on any failure
- Backward compatible: model-engine-file still works if specified

Files modified:
- nvdsinfer_model_builder.h: Add helper function declarations
- nvdsinfer_model_builder.cpp: Implement naming logic and helpers
- nvdsinfer_context_impl.cpp: Implement 3-step engine discovery
- ENGINE_FILE_NAMING_IMPROVEMENT.md: Documentation

title: OPE-3175 Fix workflow to use unity or dotnet exclusively
body:
Ticket: https://magnopus.atlassian.net/browse/OPE-3175

This PR updates the workflows to:

1. Properly filter artifacts between Unity and dotnet, making the workflows exclusive and independent.

2. Fix linking using a variable instead of hard coding the dllname used by dllimport (needed as __Internal for iOS and visionOS due to the static linking, but as ConnectedSpacesPlatformDotNet for platforms that need dynamic linking).

3. Produce a tarball for Unity (.tgz instead of .zip), which is more standard for Unity import flow.

4. Update release pipeline to use release branch (where source codes generated by SWIG are pushed to and versioned) + Github release page (where the versioned libraries and binaries produced by SWIG are published to, versioned).

5. Generate the .meta files for the source code pushed to the release branch of Unity, because once Unity pulls the source codes it stores them into PackageCache, which is immutable. This means that Unity won't be able to generate the .meta required to compile the scripts, so we produce them via CI.


**Testing**
I tested the whole workflow by using a temporary push trigger for this branch, which triggered the make-release.yml flow. As a result, this flow:

1. Produced the libraries (.tgz for Unity and .zip for DotNet, publishing them onto [this Github release page](https://github.com/magnopus-opensource/connected-spaces-platform-unity/releases/tag/test-0.0.1-23)).
2. Pushed the related versioned code generated by SWIG into the [release/unity](https://github.com/magnopus-opensource/connected-spaces-platform-unity/tree/release/unity) and [release/dotnet](https://github.com/magnopus-opensource/connected-spaces-platform-unity/tree/release/dotnet) branches.

I then tested the import into Unity, which I detailed into our internal documenation.

Updated docs: https://magnopus.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/OL/pages/4191060014/CSP+Unity+interop+Release+process

title: refactor: Python API internals
body:
## What changed
- fixed verified Python API bugs around `run(initial_partition={})` and NetworkX multilayer/state imports with non-integer labels
- introduced a reusable `InfomapOptions` model plus `Infomap.from_options()` and `run_with_options()`
- split the Python facade into internal modules for bindings, options, NetworkX import, results/iterators, and writers while preserving the public `from infomap import ...` API
- renamed internal modules to clearer names and ignored local Python build artifacts in the package tree
- updated Python docs, README examples, generated docs output, and regression coverage

## Why
The Python interface had a few correctness issues, a very large monolithic implementation file, and duplicated option/documentation logic that was drifting. This change fixes the verified bugs first and then separates the internal responsibilities without changing the public API surface.

## Impact
- multilayer and state NetworkX graphs with string IDs now import deterministically and correctly
- reusable options can be shared between construction and runs
- internal Python package layout is easier to navigate and maintain
- generated docs and examples describe the new options workflow and NetworkX/state usage more clearly

## Validation
- `ruff check interfaces/python/src/infomap/__init__.py interfaces/python/src/infomap/_bindings.py interfaces/python/src/infomap/_facade.py interfaces/python/src/infomap/_networkx.py interfaces/python/src/infomap/_optional.py interfaces/python/src/infomap/_options.py interfaces/python/src/infomap/_results.py interfaces/python/src/infomap/_version.py interfaces/python/src/infomap/_writers.py test/python/test_api.py test/python/test_iterators.py test/python/test_wrapper_args.py`

The Dockerfiles were already updated to 1.26.2 in a prior dependabot PR. This commit updates the remaining pinned versions in go.mod and CI workflow files.

title: Rewrite Python bindings from ctypes to nanobind-based HGS-CVRP integration (v0.1.0)
body:
## Summary
This PR rewrites the Python/C++ binding layer from `ctypes` to a compiled `nanobind` extension, while preserving the existing `Solver`/`AlgorithmParameters` interface for backward compatibility.

## What Changed
- Replaced dynamic library loading and `ctypes` structs/calls with a native `nanobind` module: `hybgensea/_core.cpp`.
- Added direct Python APIs:
  - `solve_cvrp(...)`
  - `solve_cvrp_dist_mtx(...)`
  - `solve_tsp(...)`
- Kept legacy compatibility:
  - `AlgorithmParameters` dataclass still available.
  - `Solver.solve_cvrp()` and `Solver.solve_tsp()` now delegate to the new nanobind-backed functions.
- Added log capture/callback support (`log_callback`) and solver log return in `RoutingSolution.log`.
- Released the Python GIL during solver execution to improve concurrency behavior.
- Updated build system:
  - Added `nanobind` to build requirements.
  - CMake now builds and installs `_core` extension.
  - Switched embedded HGS dependency to `mdealencar/HGS-CVRP` commit `0b448216...`.
- Bumped package version from `0.0.1` to `0.1.0`.
- Updated README with fork motivation and migration note to nanobind-based implementation (0.1.0+).

## Why
- Improve performance and reduce Python/C interop overhead.
- Provide a cleaner, typed native extension path.
- Enable better runtime behavior (GIL release, log streaming callback).
- Keep existing user-facing API largely stable during the migration.

## Notes / Compatibility
- Core solver algorithm/default parameters remain aligned with previous behavior.
- Existing code using `Solver` and `AlgorithmParameters` should continue to work.
- Internal implementation is now extension-module based, so local builds/tests require `_core` to be built.

title: Fix 33 bugs from thorough audit: VT compliance, event loop, rendering, backends
body:
## **User description** ## Summary Thorough bug audit of the entire zt codebase (~7000 LOC) using 6 specialized review agents + manual analysis. Found 40 issues, fixed 33. ### Critical (2) - **SIGCHLD handler reaping PTY child** → `deinit()` hang on already-reaped PID (use WNOHANG) - **Wide char rendering row-level bounds** → buffer overrun at last column (added stride check) ### High (11) - EPOLLHUP/EPOLLERR not handled → PTY EOF spin - XTVERSION hardcoded "0.5.3" → dynamic `config.version` - DECSC/DECRC missing G0-G3 charsets save/restore - VPA (CSI d) ignoring DECOM origin mode - DECSTBM accepting top==bottom (1-line scroll region trap) - DCS handler missing BEL terminator - XTSAVE/XTRESTORE wiping all saved state → per-mode update-or-insert - Sync update timeout skipping render frame - Wayland clipboard object ID double-free - fbdev input_event hardcoded 24-byte buffer → proper extern struct - macOS ztKeyDown emitting duplicate KeyEvent+TextEvent ### Medium (14) - BEL/title/IME updates dropped when screen clean (moved before `hasDirty()`) - DECSTR incomplete reset (ul_color, hyperlink_id, vt52_mode) - Colon colorspace detection ambiguous → count-based - REP clamp too conservative (cols → cols*rows) - Wide char boundary cleanup leaking ul_color_rgb/hyperlink_ids - Double underline overflow past cell bottom - `last_render_ns` using stale loop-start timestamp - Selective erase destroying DECSCA-protected wide chars - Wayland recv_buf 4KB → 16KB (prevent RecvBufferFull) - Wayland ObjectIdAllocator free_list 128 → 256 slots - FontBlob bitmap bounds check for malformed blobs - macOS ztWindowShouldClose returning YES prematurely - Cursor blink reset on input activity (all backends) - PTY drain cap comment mismatch ### Low (6) - env_arr bounds safety comment - demo.sh version update ### Not fixed (intentional) - C1 controls (0x80-0x9F) ignored — deliberate for UTF-8 compat - OSC 8 URI validation — click handler not yet implemented - OSC/DCS 8192-byte truncation — acceptable robustness behavior ## Test plan - [x] `zig build test -Dbackend=x11` — all tests pass - [x] `zig build -Dbackend=x11` — clean build - [ ] Manual: verify VT compliance with vttest - [ ] Manual: verify macOS input handling (dead keys, IME, window close) - [ ] Manual: verify Wayland clipboard paste cycle 🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code) ___ ## **CodeAnt-AI Description** Fix terminal input, rendering, and cleanup issues while adding a demo script ### What Changed - Terminal cleanup no longer hangs when the child process was already reaped, and Wayland clipboard IDs are no longer released twice. - The terminal now stops when a PTY hangs up or errors, and cursor blink resets after typing or pasting so the cursor stays visible during input. - Rendering no longer writes past wide-character boundaries, and underline rendering stays within the cell. - Several terminal escape sequences now behave as expected: BEL/title/IME updates still run when the screen is unchanged, scroll regions reject a 1-line trap, vertical cursor positioning respects origin mode, DCS accepts BEL as an end marker, and cursor save/restore keeps character sets and reset state. - Backend and font handling now tolerate larger Wayland message bursts, parse fbdev input safely, and reject malformed font data. - Added a demo script that shows the terminal’s styled text, colors, hyperlinks, and box drawing output. ### Impact `✅ Fewer terminal freezes on exit` `✅ Fewer clipboard crashes on Wayland` `✅ Safer rendering for wide text and styled underlines`
💡 Usage Guide ### Checking Your Pull Request Every time you make a pull request, our system automatically looks through it. We check for security issues, mistakes in how you're setting up your infrastructure, and common code problems. We do this to make sure your changes are solid and won't cause any trouble later. ### Talking to CodeAnt AI Got a question or need a hand with something in your pull request? You can easily get in touch with CodeAnt AI right here. Just type the following in a comment on your pull request, and replace "Your question here" with whatever you want to ask: 
@codeant-ai ask: Your question here
This lets you have a chat with CodeAnt AI about your pull request, making it easier to understand and improve your code. #### Example

### Preserve Org Learnings with CodeAnt You can record team preferences so CodeAnt AI applies them in future reviews. Reply directly to the specific CodeAnt AI suggestion (in the same thread) and replace "Your feedback here" with your input:

This helps CodeAnt AI learn and adapt to your team's coding style and standards. #### Example

title: refactor(build): remove C++ taglib adapter
body:
### Description

This PR removes the CGO-based C++ TagLib adapter (`adapters/taglib/`) and all the cross-taglib build infrastructure that came with it. The WASM-based `adapters/gotaglib/` adapter (already present) becomes the sole metadata extractor. Because `gotaglib` registers itself under the name `"taglib"`, existing installations with `ND_SCANNER_EXTRACTOR=taglib` (the default) keep working transparently — no configuration change is required.

**What's removed:**
- `adapters/taglib/` — the entire CPP/CGO wrapper (~1400 lines)
- `.github/actions/download-taglib/` — the download composite action
- All `CROSS_TAGLIB_VERSION` / `CGO_CFLAGS_ALLOW` / `pkg-config --define-prefix` references from `Dockerfile`, `.devcontainer/Dockerfile`, `.devcontainer/devcontainer.json`, `.github/workflows/pipeline.yml`, and `Makefile`
- The `taglib-build` stage and its mount/env exports in `Dockerfile` (the existing glibc `build` stage is kept intact)
- The `_ "github.com/navidrome/navidrome/adapters/taglib"` import from `cmd/root.go` and `cmd/wire_gen.go`

**Defensive fix included:** `core/storage/local/local.go` no longer calls `log.Fatal` when the configured extractor is not registered — it now warns and falls back to `consts.DefaultScannerExtractor` ("taglib", which resolves to gotaglib after this PR). This protects users whose config still references a removed/unknown extractor name (e.g. a stale `ffmpeg` value) from a hard startup crash.

**Why:** The CPP TagLib adapter required a pre-built cross-compiled taglib distribution, a dedicated GitHub action to download it, `--define-prefix` CGO flag allowlisting, and a separate `taglib-build` Docker stage. With gotaglib (WASM) mature and already the default extractor, the CPP adapter is redundant build complexity.

### Related Issues

None.

### Type of Change
- [ ] Bug fix
- [ ] New feature
- [ ] Documentation update
- [x] Refactor
- [ ] Other (please describe):

### Checklist
- [x] My code follows the project's coding style
- [x] I have tested the changes locally
- [ ] I have added or updated documentation as needed
- [x] I have added tests that prove my fix/feature works (or explain why not)
- [x] All existing and new tests pass

_No new tests: this PR is a pure deletion of a redundant adapter; the existing `core/storage/local` and `adapters/gotaglib` test suites cover the remaining extractor path, and they pass._

### How to Test

**1. Default install (existing taglib config keeps working):**
1. Build and run: `make build && ./navidrome`
2. Scan a music library with a variety of formats (mp3, flac, ogg, m4a, etc.)
3. Verify metadata (title, artist, album, track/disc, replay gain, cover art) is extracted correctly — it should be identical to master, since gotaglib was already the default.

**2. Stale extractor config no longer crashes:**
1. Set `ND_SCANNER_EXTRACTOR=ffmpeg` (or any unknown value) in env or `navidrome.toml`
2. Start navidrome
3. Confirm it no longer exits with `FATAL` — instead it logs a WARN `"Extractor not found, using default"` and falls back to `taglib` (gotaglib), and scanning works.

**3. Docker image build:**
1. `make IMAGE_PLATFORMS=linux/amd64 DOCKER_TAG=navidrome:test docker-image`
2. Verify build completes without referencing `taglib-build`, `CROSS_TAGLIB_VERSION`, or `cross-taglib` downloads.
3. Run the container, scan a small library, verify metadata extraction works.

**4. Standalone binary via docker buildx:**
1. `docker buildx build --platform linux/amd64 --output ./binaries --target binary .`
2. Run the produced binary outside Docker and scan a library.

### Screenshots / Demos

Not applicable — no UI changes.

### Additional Notes

- **No behavior change for the default config.** `gotaglib` registers under the name `"taglib"`, so the default `ND_SCANNER_EXTRACTOR=taglib` keeps resolving to a valid extractor. No migration needed.
- **Net diff:** 17 files changed, ~3 insertions / ~1440 deletions across both commits.
- **Docker build simplification:** the `Dockerfile` loses the `taglib-build` stage entirely and the glibc `build` stage no longer needs `PKG_CONFIG_PATH`, `CGO_CFLAGS_ALLOW`, or the taglib mount.
- **CI simplification:** `.github/workflows/pipeline.yml` no longer needs the `Download TagLib` step in lint or test jobs, nor the `pkg-config --define-prefix --cflags --libs taglib` debug line before tests.

title: Port OCHO C game to Python with Tkinter GUI
body:
### Motivation
- Port the original OCHO C game to Python and provide a simple interactive GUI so the game can be played with buttons representing holes and a roll command.

### Description
- Added `ocho_gui.py` which implements the game as `OchoGame` and preserves original mechanics such as randomized rolling, initial-roll match requirement, scoring, match return + reroll behavior, and a 10-turn limit.
- Implemented `OchoApp` using Tkinter that displays 8 hole buttons with match highlighting, a "Roll Again (Give Back Selected Match)" button, and an "End Turn" button, plus an entrypoint `main()` to launch the GUI.
- Exposed internal methods including `roll_balls()`, `current_score()`, `reload_non_matches()`, `return_match_and_roll()`, and `end_turn()` to mirror the original game flow and facilitate testing.

### Testing
- Compiled the module with `python -m py_compile ocho_gui.py`, which succeeded.
- Performed a smoke test by instantiating `OchoGame` in Python and asserting the initial turn contains at least one match, which passed.

------

title: Improve performance in jitcdde_template.c
body:
This has two performance improvements:
1. Uses Horner to evaluate some of the cubic polynomials. As far as I can tell, this has a very negligible effect (likely because `-O3` does it anyway).
2. Passes anchors around by pointer instead of by value. This seems to have a noticeable effect for larger systems, because it avoids a bunch of copies.

I've tested the changes on the `examples/kuramoto_network.py` example (using hyperfine to do full timing) and using [this benchmark](https://gist.github.com/alexfikl/c9864709490653b6f3096731cdf16e80) (to time separate parts). The results can be seen below.

This was run on my machine (AMD Ryzen AI 9 HX 370) and Python 3.14 using GCC and SymEngine 0.14.0. The huge amount of time is spent in the compilation step, but not sure we can do much there (between symengine and gcc doing work). The improvement I was hoping for is in the `run` part, which seems pretty nice :grin:

What do you think? Also checked that there are no new leaks reported by valgrind.

#### Before
```
> hyperfine 'python -O examples/kuramoto_network.py' 
Benchmark 1: python -O examples/kuramoto_network.py
  Time (mean ± σ):      2.774 s ±  0.036 s    [User: 2.765 s, System: 0.121 s]
  Range (min … max):    2.725 s …  2.827 s    10 runs
```
```
> python -O benchmark_kuramoto.py --reps 10 --n 200    
Results (omp=off)  (n=200, 10 reps, mean ± stdev)
  setup        1.1 ms  ±  1.3 ms
  compile      4.806 s  ±  228.4 ms
  transient    2.5 ms  ±  1.8 ms
  run          201.5 ms  ±  84.6 ms
  total        5.011 s  ±  184.9 ms
```

#### After
```
> hyperfine 'python -O examples/kuramoto_network.py'
Benchmark 1: python examples/kuramoto_network.py
  Time (mean ± σ):      1.679 s ±  0.067 s    [User: 1.568 s, System: 0.109 s]
  Range (min … max):    1.626 s …  1.858 s    10 runs
```
```
> python -O benchmark_kuramoto.py --reps 10 --n 200    
Results (n=200, 10 reps, mean ± stdev)
  setup        1.1 ms  ±  1.3 ms
  compile      4.679 s  ±  178.8 ms
  transient    1.8 ms  ±  1.4 ms
  run          123.2 ms  ±  52.1 ms
  total        4.805 s  ±  150.8 ms
```

I'm not sure why the `hyperfine` run was so much faster after, since it really should be dominated by the compilation part.

#### Wilson-Cowan

I've also tested this on my own code (a similarly sized Wilson-Cowan system with fewer delays) and it seems to improve the situation quite a lot. When running a Lyapunov exponent calculation, I'm seeing
```
# before 
eps = 0.000 tau = 1.610 behavior 'Periodic' (wall 50.14251s)
# after
eps = 0.000 tau = 1.610 behavior 'Periodic' (wall 5.90889s)
```

This is a bit dramatic and I'm not quite sure why it improves it so much, but it seems repeatable with the changes on this branch. I can make an smaller script for it, if you think it's useful.

title: アクセシビリティチェックの PyInstaller bundle 内 segfault を修正
body:
## 概要
- `AXIsProcessTrustedWithOptions` を ctypes 直呼び出しから pyobjc の `ApplicationServices` バインディングに置き換え
- 旧 ctypes コードは PyInstaller bundle 内で `CFGetTypeID`（faulting address 0x8）で確実に segfault していた。`CFDictionaryCreate` が NULL を返しており、原因は手動 lookup した `kCFBooleanTrue` のポインタがフレームワークインスタンス越しで解決されないこと
- PyInstaller の hidden imports に `ApplicationServices` / `HIServices` を追加して bundle に含める
- テストは新しい実装に合わせて書き直し

## レビュー反映
- `sys.platform != "darwin"` を早期 return に追加（ImportError ≠ 非 macOS の誤判定を排除）
- macOS 上で pyobjc の ImportError が起きた場合は warning を出して False を返す（install/bundling バグを黙らせない）
- 対応するテストを追加

## 再現・検証
- Before: `open -n dist/VoiceInput.app` で即座に `EXC_BAD_ACCESS` クラッシュ（`~/Library/Logs/DiagnosticReports/` に crash report）
- After: アクセシビリティ設定に応じて True/False を正しく返す

## テスト方針
- [x] `uv run pytest tests/` (全件 pass)
- [ ] 再ビルドした bundle を `open dist/VoiceInput.app` で起動、segfault しないことを確認
- [ ] メニューバーアイコンが表示されることを確認

title: compile: godebug default ordering, cgo constraint OR, surface ObjC/SWIG
body:
## Summary
- `DefaultGODEBUG` resolves `godebug default=goX.Y` first so explicit `godebug k=v` directives survive regardless of position.
- `matchesCgoConstraint` rewritten to match `go/build`: comma=AND, space=OR within a group; `unix`/`cgo`/`gc` and custom build tags now consulted.
- `MFiles`/`SwigFiles`/`SwigCXXFiles` are plumbed through `gofiles`/`compile`/`golist` and the Rust plugin, with a clear "not yet supported" error at compile time instead of being silently dropped.

## Test plan
- [x] `(cd go/go2nix && go test ./... && go vet ./...)`
- [x] `cargo test` (in `packages/go2nix-nix-plugin/rust`)
- [x] `nix flake check` — formatting + check-godebug-table pass

title: plugin: include MFiles/SwigFiles in go list -json= filter
body:
## Summary

The `PkgFiles` struct (added in #61) deserialises `MFiles`/`SwigFiles`/`SwigCXXFiles` and `CompileGoPackage` fails fast when they're non-empty, but the `-json=` field filter passed to `go list` at resolve.rs:312/356 omitted them — so `go list` never emitted them, the structs always defaulted to empty, and the "Objective-C/SWIG not yet supported" error never fired in default/dag mode. Packages with `.m`/`.swig` sources were silently compiled without those files.

Appends `MFiles,SwigFiles,SwigCXXFiles` to both `-json=` strings and adds a regression test that runs the real `go list` against a temp module with `.m`/`.swig`/`.swigcxx` files. The test is skipped when `GO2NIX_DEFAULT_GO` is unset at compile time (bare `cargo test`) and exercised in the nix build where it's baked in.

## Test plan

- [x] `cargo test` — 45/45 (test skips gracefully without baked-in go)
- [x] `GO2NIX_DEFAULT_GO=$(which go) cargo test run_go_list_surfaces` — passes; verified it would have failed before the fix (`go list` with the old field set returns `"MFiles":null`)
- [x] `nix build .#go2nix-nix-plugin`
- [x] `nix flake check` (formatting)

title: Remove Qt from ModelEditor Ruby bindings
Move Gaussian splatting autograd and pipeline logic from C++ to Python
body:
## Summary

- Port all C++ autograd functions (projection, rasterization, SH evaluation) to pure Python `torch.autograd.Function` implementations in a new `_gaussian_autograd.py` module, making the differentiable pipeline easier to extend and debug.
- Eliminate the C++ `GaussianSplat3d` class entirely: the Python `GaussianSplat3d` class now directly manages `torch.Tensor` attributes instead of delegating to a C++ `_impl` object.
- Rewrite `deduplicatePixels` as a pure Python function using PyTorch ops, removing its C++ binding and implementation.
- Refactor PLY I/O to return a flat tuple of tensors (no intermediate struct), and update the viewer to accept individual tensors directly instead of a C++ object.
- Remove ~7,600 lines of dead C++ code (`GaussianSplat3d.{h,cpp}`, `detail/autograd/*`, `DeduplicatePixelsTest.cpp`, `GaussianSplat3dCameraApiTest.cpp`).
- Simplify pybind bindings to only expose low-level dispatch functions; all autograd wiring now lives in Python.

## Test plan

- [x] All 135 Python unit tests pass (`cd tests && pytest unit/test_gaussian_splat_3d.py -v`)
- [x] All 21 C++ gtests pass (`ctest --test-dir build/.../src/tests`)
- [x] CI passes (codestyle, DCO, full test matrix)

title: [24.10]: u-boot: backport swig bugfix (tegra, sunxi)
body:
This PR is the same as #21415, but fixes more impacted targets (`tegra` & `sunxi`) which were missed during that PR.

---

When building on current Debian stable (13), which has a newer swig version, the build process fails. This was previously described in #20620, #21415, and in this PR's commit messages.

I was not able to find backportable commits for these targets (`tegra` & `sunxi`), as most simply updated to/past 25.01 on `main`, before this problem showed up.

For additional context, u-boot 25.01 added the SWIG fix: u-boot/u-boot@a63456b9191fae2fe49f4b121e025792022e3950, which was part of v2025.01-rc2.

As such, targets either updated to/past u-boot 25.01 (e.g. tegra 92cd360aacd697f81f649ef78a7a5bb0d007f84a and sunxi 2aba2b32ce69556be76e428499abb39293f0a94b), or removed the patch when bumping the version (e.g. rockchip 92814fec77fe24a68ab4a2acbf3ac245163c6090).

title: Display macOS Finder color tags
body:
## Summary
Read the \`com.apple.metadata:_kMDItemUserTags\` xattr via \`ctypes\`-bound \`libc.getxattr\` (macOS-specific signature) and parse the binary plist with stdlib \`plistlib\`. Each tag string is \`"Name\nIndex"\` where the index maps to a Finder color (1-7); colorless tags (index 0) are skipped.

Listing entries now carry an optional \`tags\` array of color indices, and the list/column views render small colored dots after the file name (matching Finder's palette: gray, green, purple, blue, yellow, red, orange).

Zero new dependencies — pure stdlib (\`ctypes\`, \`plistlib\`).

Closes #48

## Test plan

title: Preparations for symmetrizing the two-atom basis
YAML 101: Show JSON, TOML, Python, C++ & Julia
body:
## Motivation

From feedback from early PALS users and contributors that have not used YAML yet much, the YAML 101 can be slightly improved with better examples: By showing equivalent examples of the two fundamental container types (list and dict) in JSON, TOML, Python, C++ and Julia we can make it easier for people new to YAML to read the rest of the document.

## Compact Implementation

By using a "tab-set" (see [preview here](https://pals-project--209.org.readthedocs.build/en/209/conventions.html#syntax-used-in-this-document-is-yaml)) this adds no scroll bloat to someone reading the standard that already knows YAML.

## Alternative Placements

I also considered moving this in the end to _file formats_, but it breaks **linear reading** of the standard early on, if one has to jump there to learn the conventions used in the document, jump back to continue reading, etc. As a long-term documentation writer, I would avoid such literal and mental jumps.

One could also move [file formats](https://pals-project--209.org.readthedocs.build/en/209/fileformats.html) as a whole forward to _conventions_. Open to ideas.

title: Add PJSUA2 AudioMediaAiPort wrapper for AI media port
body:
## Summary

Wraps the experimental `pjmedia_ai_port` (from PR #4866) as a PJSUA2 `AudioMedia` subclass, enabling C++/Java/Python/C# applications to use the AI media port through the high-level API.

Ref: #4523

## Usage

```cpp
AudioMediaAiPort ai;
ai.createPort();
ai.connect("wss://api.openai.com/v1/realtime?model=...", apiKey);

// Route call audio to/from AI
callMedia.startTransmit(ai);
ai.startTransmit(callMedia);

// Later
ai.disconnect();
```

Events (connected, transcript, etc.) are delivered via the virtual `onEvent()` callback, which can be overridden in C++ or target languages via SWIG directors.

## Design

- Uses **pjsua's SIP endpoint ioqueue and timer heap** for WebSocket I/O — no separate worker thread, pjsua's existing worker threads handle polling
- Uses `pjmedia_ai_event_type` enum directly (no wrapper enum)
- Destructor nulls the `user_data` back-pointer under grp_lock to prevent use-after-free from in-flight callbacks
- Wrapper pool lifetime tied to grp_lock destroy handler to prevent premature release

## Limitations

These are known limitations for reference in future improvements:
- No SSL/TLS parameter configuration (e.g. certificate verification, client certs, cipher suites). The C API supports `ssl_param` but it is not yet exposed in the PJSUA2 wrapper.
- No way to customize the OpenAI session configuration (voice, instructions, modalities) — currently hardcoded in the backend
- No `isConnected()` / `getState()` query method — connection state can only be inferred from `onEvent()` callbacks
- No JSON persistence (`readObject`/`writeObject`) for `AiMediaPortParam`

## Changes

| File | Description |
|------|-------------|
| `pjsip/include/pjsua2/media.hpp` | `AiMediaEvent`, `AiMediaPortParam`, `AudioMediaAiPort` class |
| `pjsip/src/pjsua2/media.cpp` | Implementation (~120 lines) |
| `pjmedia/include/pjmedia/ai_port.h` | Add `pjmedia_ai_port_set_user_data()` |
| `pjmedia/src/pjmedia/ai_port.c` | Implement `set_user_data()` |
| `pjmedia/include/pjmedia.h` | Add `ai_port.h` to umbrella header |
| `pjsip-apps/src/swig/pjsua2.i` | Add director feature for `AudioMediaAiPort` |
| `pjsip-apps/src/swig/symbols.lst` | Add `pjmedia_ai_event_type` |
| `pjsip-apps/src/swig/symbols.i` | Regenerated with `importsym2.py` |
| `pjsip-apps/src/swig/python/test.py` | Python SWIG test for AI port lifecycle + optional OpenAI connection |

## Test plan
- [x] Build verified on Linux (zero warnings)
- [x] Build on Windows/MSVC (Release-Static, C++ lib clean)
- [x] Python SWIG test: create, conf bridge register, destroy (WSL)
- [x] Python SWIG test with OpenAI connection on MSYS2/MinGW (real audio, confirmed working)

Co-Authored-By: Claude Code

title: Add Python 3 importsym script and regenerate symbols.i
body:
The existing `importsym.py` has several issues:
- Requires Python 2 (uses `print` statements, `dict.has_key()`, etc.)
- Depends on `pycparser` external package with hardcoded install paths
- Requires pycparser's `fake_libc_include` headers to be present
- Has a bug: references undefined variable `k` instead of `name` in warning message

This PR adds `importsym2.py`, a Python 3 rewrite that uses `cpp` (C preprocessor) + regex instead of `pycparser`, eliminating all external dependencies. The original `importsym.py` is kept for reference.

Also:
- Remove `pjmedia/tonegen.h` from `symbols.lst` — these are struct types (not enums) that were never successfully extracted and are not referenced in the SWIG bindings.
- Regenerate `symbols.i` with current header definitions (picks up new enum values added since last generation).

Usage: `python3 importsym2.py` (from `pjsip-apps/src/swig/`)

### Test plan

- [ ] Run `python3 importsym2.py` and verify `symbols.i` is generated with all symbols
- [ ] SWIG bindings build successfully with the regenerated `symbols.i`

Co-Authored-By: Claude Code

title: Add C++ extension modules for CPU-intensive routines (pybind11)
body:
Implement compiled C++ replacements for the highest-impact performance bottlenecks in freqtrade, organized into 6 submodules:

S-tier (10-50x speedup potential):
- backtesting: Fast trade simulation engine (simulate_trades) replacing the inner backtest_loop with compiled OHLCV+signal array processing, ROI/stoploss/trailing-stop evaluation

A-tier (5-20x speedup potential):
- trade_math: Native double-precision profit calculations replacing FtPrecise string-based math (calculate_profit, calc_profit_ratio, calc_close_rate_for_roi, recalc_trade_from_orders, batch operations)
- data_processing: Fast NaN filtering (combined_nan_mask), feature shifting (shift_features), fillna+predict mask for FreqAI pipelines

B-tier (3-10x speedup potential):
- report_gen: Binary-search signal candle extraction replacing O(n^2) iterrows+concat loops, max drawdown calculation, pair metrics aggregation, expectancy calculation
- orderflow: Volume profile aggregation, imbalance detection, stacked imbalance finding, per-candle bid/ask/delta aggregation
- indicators: Heikin Ashi (eliminates row-by-row .at[] loop), rolling mean/std/sum/min/max, EMA

Architecture:
- freqtrade/cpp/src/*.cpp — C++ source with pybind11 bindings
- freqtrade/ft_cpp/*.py — Python wrappers with graceful fallback
- setup_cpp.py — Build script (python setup_cpp.py build_ext --inplace)
- All existing code paths fall back to pure Python if C++ not compiled

Integration points:
- qtpylib/indicators.py: heikinashi() uses C++ when available
- optimize_reports.py: signal candle extraction uses binary search
- freqai/data_kitchen.py: filter_features() and populate_features() use C++ NaN filtering and feature shifting

https://claude.ai/code/session_01235AT46ZzRkMthznH2BTMc

| `src/engine/tq_model.c` | Fused `attn_qkv` detection, LongRoPE factor loading, fused `gate\|\|up` FFN |

| `src/engine/tq_generate.c` | Phi-3 BOS token handling |

title: Switch to offline-first Jarvis architecture (Android + Python + C++)
body:
### Motivation
- Replace the prior web-based frontend/backend with an offline-first, multi-language architecture focused on an on-device APK entrypoint and local AI runtime named Jarvis.
- Keep offline operation as the default while allowing explicit, opt-in connectors to public REST APIs for live data when configured.
- Provide minimal scaffolds across mobile, orchestration, and core runtime layers to bootstrap development in Kotlin/Java, Python, and C++ respectively.

### Description
- Removed the previous JavaScript `frontend/` and `backend/` web app implementation and related Node artifacts to simplify repo scope. 
- Added an Android APK scaffold with `android-app/app/src/main/java/com/jarvis/offline/MainActivity.kt` as a Kotlin entrypoint for launching Jarvis on-device. 
- Added a Python orchestration brain at `ai-brain-python/jarvis_brain.py` implementing `QueryContext` and a `JarvisBrain` class that is offline-first and supports optional web API routing via `allow_web_api`.
