title: Python rewrite
Port EMU7800 Atari 7800/2600 emulator from C# to Python
body:
Complete port of the EMU7800 C# emulator to Python, including:

- MOS 6502 CPU emulation (all 256 opcodes, addressing modes, BCD, interrupts)
- TIA graphics chip (2600 video rendering, collision detection, WSYNC)
- MARIA graphics chip (7800 video with DMA, 6 render modes, DLL/DL)
- PIA/RIOT 6532 (timer, I/O ports, 128B RAM, controller/console switch reading)
- AddressSpace with page-granularity memory mapping and snooper device
- InputState with double-buffered controller/console-switch state
- TIASound (2-channel) and PokeySound (4-channel) audio emulation
- 27+ cartridge mappers for both 2600 and 7800
- Machine hierarchy: MachineBase -> Machine2600/Machine7800 -> NTSC/PAL variants
- Platform layer: pygame window, audio output, keyboard/gamepad input handling
- Shell services: ROM loading with .a78 header parsing, machine factory
- CLI entry point with argparse for loading and running ROMs

title: optimize C extension
[Python] Start switching ROOT pythonizations to Python Limited C API
body:
This patch replaces usage of CPython macros and non-stable APIs with functions compatible with the Python Limited C API (stable ABI).

Replace direct macros with API functions:

  * `PyBytes_AS_STRING`   : `PyBytes_AsString`
  * `PyBytes_GET_SIZE`    : `PyBytes_Size`
  * `PyTuple_GET_SIZE`    : `PyTuple_Size`
  * `PyTuple_SET_ITEM`    : `PyTuple_SetItem`
  * `PyList_GET_ITEM`     : `PyList_GetItem`
  * `PyList_GET_SIZE`     : `PyList_Size`
  * `PyInt_AS_LONG`       : `PyInt_AsLong`
  * `Py_DECREF`           : `Py_DecRef`
  * `Py_XDECREF`          : `Py_DecRef`

Furthermore, replace `PyUnicode_AsUTF8` with a helper based on the stable `PyUnicode_AsUTF8AndSize`. Also, replace `<Python.h>` with `"PythonLimitedAPI.h"`, where the `Py_LIMITED_API` macro is set.

This change is part of enabling PyROOT to build against the Python stable ABI.

title: [Python] Only use Python Limited C API in ROOT Pythonizations library
body:
This follows up on 4e0c7bf0dda25984c, swapping out the remaining use of CPython API functions that were not inside the limited API.

In the cases where we're sill using private headers from cppyy, the parts of these headers that don't compile with the limited API are excluded via the `Py_LIMITED_API` preprocessor macro.

title: fix(python_plugins_loader): suppress -Wconversion for SWIG-generated code
body:
The SWIG-generated swigpyrun.h contains an implicit size_t to int narrowing in SWIG_Hash():
```c
  rem -= sizeof(unsigned int);
```
This triggers -Werror=conversion with GCC 16 (not Clang). The bug is fixed in SWIG master (https://github.com/swig/swig/pull/3287), but unfortunately, not in any released SWIG version at the time of writing (see
https://github.com/swig/swig/pull/3287/changes/296fe5e4325795e8fea933341c20b73566cffc87, 4.4.1 narrowly missed it).

This warning was previously masked by a leaked -Wconversion diagnostic suppression in fmt < 12 (https://github.com/fmtlib/fmt/pull/4588). fmt 12 properly scoped the suppression, exposing this pre-existing SWIG issue.

Add -Wno-conversion to the existing warning suppression list for the python_plugins_loader target, consistent with the existing approach of suppressing warnings in SWIG-generated code that we don't control.

We can revert this change once the downstream packaging picks up the SWIG fix.

title: SHARED-12177 patch: toMonomeric: improve monomer recognition
body:
* Linked Case: SHARED-12177

### Description

This is just a cherry pick of the mmshare commit below (excluding SWIG changes and Python unit tests)

### Testing done

Build with unit tests.

### Original description

The key change here is the enumerate_tautomers() function, which is used both when generating the core SMILES lookup table for all monomers, and when generating the query molecules for the complex monomers. It is a rudimentary implementation that only handles some common imine-enamine and imidazole tautomerisms, but it is good enough to bring our success rate at generating SMILES-free HELM strings from ChEMBL from 66.7% to 96.4%, while being much faster than the RDKit tautomer canonicalizer with default settings, which was our previous, unsuccessful attempt. Still, this is a work in progress.

There were a few other changes which may seem random, but all are related with improving monomer recognition.

- neutralizeAtoms() now also standardizes the representation of sulfoxides as neutral S=O.
- changed the definition of "complex monomer" to include monomers that match the generic residue pattern only once but have the two attachment points unusually apart (!= 4 bonds)
    - also, allow the pattern to match alpha-disubstituted amino acids
- removed the urea exception from the generic amino acide SMARTS, because those are now handled by the more general complex monomer mechanism
- added a fallback to findHelmSymbol(): if there is no match, try again without removing the terminal atom and/or the R1 attachment point. The reason is that ChEMBL has many custom monomers that lack one or the other even though the SMARTS pattern would match them (typically, N- or O-substituted amino acids that are meant to be terminal)

Incidental changes:
- added a couple of missing SWIG wrappers
- moved a couple of "private" functions that were needed from one than one translation unit to the new monomer_utils.cpp

title: Add ctypes Airspy wrapper module for Windows
body:
### Motivation
- Provide a small, pure-Python `ctypes` wrapper around `airspy.dll` so the Airspy device can be opened, configured, and used from Python on Windows.
- Expose the core control functions and an RX callback that collects interleaved int16 I/Q samples into NumPy for downstream processing.

### Description
- Added `airspy_wrapper.py` which loads `airspy.dll` from the module directory via `ctypes.CDLL` and resolves the DLL path using `Path(__file__).resolve().parent / "airspy.dll"`.
- Declared an `AirspyTransfer` `ctypes.Structure` and `AIRSPY_RX_CALLBACK` `CFUNCTYPE`, and configured `argtypes`/`restype` for `airspy_open`, `airspy_close`, `airspy_set_sample_rate`, `airspy_set_freq`, `airspy_set_lna_gain`, `airspy_set_vga_gain`, `airspy_start_rx`, and `airspy_stop_rx`.
- Implemented an `AirSpy` class with `open()`, `close()`, `set_frequency(hz)`, `set_sample_rate(rate)`, `start()`, and `stop()` methods and added `set_lna_gain()`/`set_vga_gain()` helpers; all DLL calls check return codes and raise `AirSpyError` on non-zero responses.
- Implemented the RX callback to copy incoming interleaved `int16` samples, convert them to `numpy` complex64 (I + 1j*Q), buffer chunks during `start()`, and return a concatenated NumPy array from `stop()`; included a runnable usage example under `if __name__ == "__main__":`.

### Testing
- Ran `python -m py_compile airspy_wrapper.py` which completed without errors.

title: feat: add session payments with on-chain Swig enforcement
body:
## Summary

This PR implements session payment method support following https://mpp.dev/faq#what-are-sessions for the Solana SDK with on-chain Swig smart swallets, and adds full integration coverage for the session lifecycle.

## What changed

- Added session method support in the SDK client/server surface and exports.
- Implemented `SwigSessionAuthorizer` with on-chain policy validation against Swig role state.
    - Validates delegated session signer and role binding.
    - Validates role program permissions and spend limits from chain data.
    - Supports session metadata returned by wallet adapters (`openTx`, `swigRoleId`, `createdAt`) and enforces TTL safely.
- Updated `BudgetAuthorizer` to be Swig-role-backed and fail closed.
    - `swig` config is now required (`swigAddress`, `swigRoleId`, optional `rpcUrl`).
    - Resolves on-chain Swig role constraints on open and clamps effective limits to on-chain caps.
    - Rejects unknown channels for update/topup/close, so non-open actions cannot bypass open-time role validation.
    - Rejects roles that cannot prove program authorization checks.
- Updated `makeSessionAuthorizer` for `wallet-budget` profile.
    - Requires `swigWallet.swigAddress` and `swigWallet.swigRoleId`.
    - Passes Swig config through to `BudgetAuthorizer`.
- Added a Surfpool mitigation in client charge flow.
    - Appends a memo with challenge reference so rapid identical transfers are unique.
    - Prevents `This transaction has already been processed` failures in local integration runs.

## Tests added and updated

- Added full session integration coverage:
    - auto-open then update flow
    - auto-topup management flow
    - close then reopen flow
    - `swig_session` positive on-chain flow with enforced Swig spend limit
    - `regular_budget` positive on-chain Swig role-limited flow
- Added negative integration coverage:
    - `swig_session` rejects delegated signer not present on-chain
    - `regular_budget` rejects unknown configured Swig role

## Validation

- `npm run typecheck` passed
- `npm test` passed
- `npm run test:integration` passed with Surfpool running

title: feat: harden swig session settlement lifecycle + add swig session demo
body:
## Summary
- Hardened session settlement semantics across the SDK by carrying `closeTx` through close authorization and server verification, and by adding optional `settleOnLimitHit` behavior when channel limits are reached.
- Created a Swig demo which uses USDC base units end-to-end (pricing, deposits, settlement transfers, and verifier checks) and aligned all Swig references to the real Swig program id.
- Delegated-role lifecycle in the demo creates a fresh delegated Swig role per session, removing that role on successful close settlement, and reinitializing session state so reopened channels do not reuse stale role limits.

## Implementation Details
- Added `closeTx` support in session method schema/types/client/server and added `verifyClose(...)` handling on the server path.
- Added `settleOnLimitHit?: boolean` to the session client so over-limit requests can auto-close/settle instead of throwing when `autoTopup` is disabled.
- Swig demo server enforces USDC SPL asset semantics and validates open/topup/close transactions against expected token transfer behavior.
- Swig demo client now:
  - builds open/topup/close USDC token-transfer flows,
  - uses delegated-role-scoped signing for settlement,
  - recycles delegated roles by removing them on successful close,
  - detects and recreates legacy Swigs with session-based manager role config.
- Updated docs and demo UX text to reflect Swig session + USDC behavior, plus delegated role visibility in the demo UI.
## Validation
- `npm run typecheck`
- `npx tsc --noEmit` (demo app)
- `npm run build` (demo app)
- manually tested demo end 2 end

## **User description** ## Summary by Sourcery Simplify the Qt application UI to a basic tabbed main window, remove advanced scheduling and TensorBoard features from the C++ core and GUI, and introduce Supabase-based persistent storage with tests, updating the controller and entrypoint to use it alongside Redis and GitHub LFS. Documentation: - Document Supabase as the primary persistent storage backend, adjusting controller and main entrypoint documentation and env var descriptions accordingly. Tests: - Add a Supabase storage test suite that mocks HTTP calls and verifies controller storage-key precedence and fallback order. Chores: - Remove unused scheduling, TensorBoard, and related Qt visualization components and tests from the C++ codebase and build configuration. ___ ## **CodeAnt-AI Description** **Simplify the desktop app and make model storage use Supabase first** ### What Changed - The Qt app now opens as a standard tabbed window with the main workflow tabs only, and the schedule, TensorBoard, and 3D inspection views were removed - Training settings now only use the built-in learning-rate options, and the removed schedule controls no longer appear in the UI - Model storage now uses Supabase as the first persistent location, then GitHub LFS, then Redis if needed - Startup text and environment variable guidance now describe Supabase as the primary persistent store - Added storage tests that check Supabase upload/download behavior and confirm the fallback order for saved model keys ### Impact `✅ Simpler training app layout` `✅ Clearer model storage priority` `✅ Fewer failed saves when one storage backend is unavailable`

title: Bitwuzla: Simplify updating the SWIG patch
body:
Hello,

this PR is in response to a comment when we last updated the Bitwuzla bindings:
> We should document the steps for updating the swig-patchfile. :-) Quite some manual work to get right paths.
[link](https://github.com/sosy-lab/java-smt/pull/598#issuecomment-3962927859)

To simplify updating the patch the build process for Bitwuzla has now been split into two separate steps:
* We first generate the new SWIG bindings with `ant bitwuzla-generate-jni`
* We then have a chance to fix any issues with the generated code
* Finally, we run `ant publish-bitwuzla` to automatically generate the updated patch file before then compiling the solver binaries

I've documented the process in `Developers-How-to-Release-into-Ivy.md`, including some hints on how to fix common issues with the generated code

Note that I also switched to using the preinstalled `jdks` from the container: We were already using the preinstalled versions of gmp/mpfr, and this removes two more arguments from the command line

title: Harden engine.py, scope Flawfinder to C/C++ only, fix ADMIN_TOKEN workflow guidance
body:
## 📋 Description

12 open code scanning alerts on `engine.py` are **false positives** — Flawfinder (a C/C++ analyzer) was scanning a Python file, matching C function names like `getenv()` and `open()`. The `branch-protection.yml` workflow fails because `ADMIN_TOKEN` is configured as a profile PAT but not stored as a repository/environment secret.

**Changes:**

- **`flawfinder.yml`**: Replace static `FLAWFINDER_TARGETS` with dynamic `find`-based C/C++ file discovery. Python files are no longer in scope. Empty SARIF fallback when no C/C++ files exist.
- **`engine.py`**: Defense-in-depth hardening — `_env_securise()` validates env vars with regex + length limits (CWE-807/CWE-20), `_chemin_reel()` resolves symlinks via `os.path.realpath()` (CWE-362).
- **`branch-protection.yml`**: Replace generic error with step-by-step setup instructions including direct URLs to PAT creation, repo secrets, and environment configuration pages.
- **`tests/test_engine.py`**: 8 new tests covering validation acceptance, rejection (invalid patterns, oversized values), defaults, and symlink resolution.

```python
# Before — raw os.getenv(), no validation
token = os.getenv("GITHUB_TOKEN")

# After — validated with regex pattern + length cap
token = _env_securise("GITHUB_TOKEN")  # ^[a-zA-Z0-9_.\-]{1,255}$
```

## 🔗 Issue liée


## 🧪 Type de changement
- [ ] 🐛 Correction de bug
- [ ] ✨ Nouvelle fonctionnalité
- [ ] 🔧 Refactoring (pas de changement fonctionnel)
- [ ] 📚 Documentation
- [x] 🔒 Sécurité / Protection
- [x] ⚙️ CI/CD / Outillage

## ✅ Checklist qualité
- [x] Mon code suit le style du projet
- [x] J'ai ajouté ou mis à jour les tests correspondants
- [x] Les tests locaux passent (`pytest tests/`)
- [ ] La vérification de type est propre (`mypy phi_complexity/`)
- [ ] L'audit phi passe (`python -m phi_complexity.cli check . --min-radiance 50`)
- [x] La documentation a été mise à jour si nécessaire
- [x] Aucun secret ni donnée sensible n'est inclus dans le diff

## 🖼️ Captures d'écran (si applicable)

Before — 12 false-positive alerts from Flawfinder scanning Python:

![Code scanning alerts](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/11a69298-1f17-424f-9eef-23374c0fe415)

ADMIN_TOKEN failure in branch-protection workflow:

![ADMIN_TOKEN error](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/9d537fd2-263f-4cb4-b219-7934cfa0349a)

## 💬 Contexte supplémentaire

- Coverage at 85.58% (pre-existing, unrelated to this PR — threshold is 89%)
- The 12 alerts will auto-close once Flawfinder runs with the corrected scope on `main`
- `ADMIN_TOKEN` still requires manual configuration: create fine-grained PAT → store as repo secret → ensure `branch-protection` environment exists

title: Add single-instance enforcement
body:
## Summary

- Prevents multiple app instances from running simultaneously
- Windows: named mutex via `ctypes.windll`
- macOS/Linux: exclusive `flock` on a temp file
- Shows a warning dialog if a second instance is launched
- Refactored into `SingleInstanceLock` class for testability

## Test plan

- [ ] Run `pytest tests/unit/test_single_instance.py -v` — all platform tests pass (opposite platform skipped)
- [ ] Launch the app, then try launching a second instance — warning dialog appears and second instance exits

- Strip `.kd` suffix, remove return type and parameter list, keep template args

| `_Z23ncclDevKernel_Generic_1...` | `ncclDevKernel_Generic_1` |
| `_ZN5aiter24add_rmsnorm_quant_kernel...` | `aiter::add_rmsnorm_quant_kernel<std::bfloat16_t, ...>` |

title: [bugfix] Rewrite transfer metrics from C++ to Python side
body:
Previously, transfer metrics (ops_total, bytes_total) were recorded in the C++ layer (transfer.cu, transfer_ssd.cpp, gds_manager.cpp) via MetricsManager macros. Although C++ has its own Prometheus exporter (port 8081), the actual data transfer code runs in forked worker subprocesses, which do not initialize the C++ Prometheus metrics server. As a result, these transfer metrics were never collected at all.

Key changes:
- Rewrite transfer metrics collection entirely on the Python side with a post-completion approach, recording metrics in the main process after transfer operations complete
- Add `flexkv_py_transfer_bytes_total` counter to Python collector for tracking data transfer throughput
- Remove `flexkv_cpp_transfer_ops_total` and `flexkv_cpp_transfer_bytes_total` counters from C++ MetricsManager

Changes include:
- Extended `CompletedOp` in `flexkv/common/transfer.py` with `transfer_type`, `num_blocks`, and `num_bytes` fields
- Compute transfer bytes in `TransferEngine` (`transfer_engine.py`) when an op finishes
- Record all three counters (ops_total, blocks_total, bytes_total) via `record_transfer_completed()` in `KVTaskManager._update_tasks()` (`kvtask.py`), ensuring metrics are collected in the main process
- Added `flexkv_py_transfer_bytes_total` counter to `flexkv/metrics/collector.py`
- Removed old `_record_transfer_ops()` from `cache_engine.py`
- Removed C++ transfer metric recording from `transfer.cu`, `transfer_ssd.cpp`, and `gds_manager.cpp`
- Updated Grafana dashboard (`flexkv-demo.json`): added Transfer Throughput panel, replaced C++ Transfer Throughput with Cache Blocks Rate panel
- Updated monitoring documentation at `docs/monitoring/README_en.md` (English) and `docs/monitoring/README_zh.md` (Chinese)

title: feat: Remove the MS C++ unit test project
body: The unit test project bases on the Microsoft Visual Studio C++ unit test framework has been replaced with a new project based on the Google unit test framework. This project is thus no longer needed and can be removed.

title: Simplify Python calls from C++
body:
Creating `decimal.py` with the below code causes the standard library module `decimal` to be passed over for this file.

```python
import math


def is_nan(self):
    return math.isnan(self)


Decimal = type("Decimal", (float,), {"is_nan": is_nan})
```

When the `is_nan` method is removed or if it is updated to raise an exception, `SortedDict` rejects the key.

```python
In [1]: from pysorteddict import SortedDict

In [2]: d = SortedDict()

In [3]: d[Decimal(0)] = 0
───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ValueError                                Traceback (most recent call last)
Cell In[3], line 1
────▶ 1 d[Decimal(0)] = 0

ValueError: got bad key 0.0 of type <class 'decimal.Decimal'>
```

Otherwise, everything works, as it should.

title: Python bus rewrite: agent lifecycle + request/reply + sessions
body:
## Summary

Full Python rewrite of khonliang-bus. Implements three bundled Step 1 FRs from the agent platform migration roadmap (see `khonliang-developer/specs/bus-mcp/architecture.md`):

- **Python rewrite** — FastAPI + WebSocket + SQLite replaces the Go v0.1 server. Same language as the rest of the ecosystem so the flow engine (Step 6) can use khonliang primitives (AgentTeam, ConsensusEngine) directly.
- **Request/reply + queue semantics** — `POST /v1/request` routes to agent callback URLs with configurable retry (exponential backoff), dead letter on max failures, NACK support. Pub/sub retained.
- **Agent lifecycle** — install/uninstall (persistent), register/deregister/heartbeat (runtime), start/stop/restart, PID crash detection, health tracking.

Also includes sessions (create/suspend/resume/archive with public/private context), execution traces with correlation IDs, and platform status.

## Endpoints

| Group | Endpoints |
|---|---|
| Lifecycle | POST install, DELETE install/{id}, GET install, start/stop/restart |
| Registration | POST register, deregister, heartbeat, GET services |
| Request/reply | POST request (with retry + dead letter) |
| Pub/sub | POST publish, ack, nack, WS subscribe |
| Sessions | POST session, GET session/{id}, message, suspend, resume, DELETE |
| Observability | GET health, status, trace/{id} |

## Test plan

- [x] 49/49 tests passing across 6 test files
- [x] Zero deprecation warnings
- [x] Manual smoke test: boot server, install agent, register with skills, request/reply (fails gracefully when no real agent), publish, create session, suspend/resume, deregister
- [ ] Copilot review

title: Python rewrite: complete port of transcriber pipeline
body:
## Summary

- **Complete Python rewrite** of the DJI audio transcription pipeline, replacing the original Bash script
- **10 source modules** with clean library + CLI architecture: audio import, STT (Parakeet), LLM (Ollama), dictionary term correction, Jinja2 templates, auto-classification, pipeline orchestration, config (Pydantic/TOML), and CLI (Typer/Rich)
- **66 passing tests** covering all modules including end-to-end pipeline tests with mocked externals
- Original Bash implementation preserved in `legacy/transcriber-bash.sh`

### New capabilities beyond the Bash version:
- YAML-based term correction dictionaries for STT errors (e.g., "Long Chain" → "LangChain")
- Jinja2 output templates (default, blog, trip-report, summary) with custom template support
- Auto-classification of transcripts into project directories by keyword matching
- TOML configuration with defaults → user config → CLI flag hierarchy
- Rich CLI with subcommands: `process`, `classify`, `reformat`, `config`, `templates`

## Test plan

- [x] All 66 unit and integration tests pass (`uv run pytest tests/ -v`)
- [ ] Manual test: run `transcriber process --skip-import` with audio files pre-staged
- [ ] Manual test: verify `transcriber templates` lists all 4 built-in templates
- [ ] Manual test: verify `transcriber config` shows default configuration

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

title: Python packaging
body:
## What this PR does

This PR adds a complete Python packaging story to the project:

1. **A self-contained installable wheel** — `pip install` / `uv sync` builds and
   installs the `mlir_ktdp` Python bindings + all native libraries. No separate
   MLIR installation required at runtime.

2. **A pure-Python utilities package** (`tools_ktdp`) — ships alongside
   `mlir_ktdp` in the wheel. Fully live-editable: changes to Python files take
   effect immediately without rebuilding.

3. **A `ktdp-walk` CLI tool** — inspect any `.mlir` file's operation tree from
   the command line.

4. **A pytest suite** — three parametrized fixtures covering dialect
   registration, flat KTDP ops, and nested control flow.

---

## How it installs

The build backend is [scikit-build-core](https://scikit-build-core.readthedocs.io/),
which drives cmake under the hood. `uv` manages the virtual environment and
locks all dependencies.

```
uv sync --extra mlir
```

That single command:
- Creates/updates the venv
- Installs `mlir_wheel` (provides MLIR cmake config + Python support libs)
- Runs cmake → builds the C++ extension + CAPI library
- Installs the project in **editable** mode

---

## Frozen dependency versions (`uv.lock`)

All versions are pinned in `uv.lock` for reproducible installs:

| Package | Pinned version | Role |
|---|---|---|
| `mlir-wheel` | `20260409+3b3ac5a11` | MLIR cmake config + bundled Python support libs |
| `nanobind` | `2.12.0` | Python ↔ C++ binding library (runtime dep) |
| `pytest` | `9.0.3` | Test runner (`--extra test`) |
| `lit` | `18.1.8` | LIT test runner (`--extra test`) |

`mlir_wheel` is an **optional** dep — users with a custom MLIR build can pass
`CMAKE_ARGS="-DMLIR_DIR=..."` and skip it entirely.

---

## Wheel contents

The wheel is self-contained (~19 MB compressed, ~70 MB uncompressed). No
`mlir_wheel` needed at runtime.

```
ktir_mlir_frontend-0.1.0-cp312-cp312-linux_aarch64.whl
│
├── mlir_ktdp/                         ← cmake-managed
│   ├── __init__.py
│   ├── ir.py, passmanager.py, rewrite.py
│   ├── dialects/
│   │   ├── ktdp_nanobind.py           ← KTDP dialect Python bindings
│   │   ├── builtin.py, ext.py
│   │   └── _ktdp_ops_gen.py, _ods_common.py, ...
│   ├── extras/
│   │   └── meta.py, types.py
│   └── _mlir_libs/
│       ├── libKtdpPythonCAPI.so       34 MB  ← statically links all MLIR C API
│       ├── libMLIRPythonSupport.so     1.5 MB
│       ├── _mlir.cpython-312-*.so      0.3 MB ← core MLIR Python extension
│       ├── _ktdpDialectsNanobind-*.so  0.1 MB ← KTDP nanobind extension
│       └── libnanobind-mlir_ktdp.so   0.2 MB
│
└── tools_ktdp/                        ← scikit-build-core managed (pure Python)
    ├── __init__.py
    ├── __main__.py                    ← entry point for ktdp-walk
    └── ir_utils.py                   ← ktdp_context(), walk_module()
```

`libKtdpPythonCAPI.so` dominates size — it statically links all MLIR dialect
C API libraries so no other MLIR installation is needed at runtime.

---

## ktdp-walk demo

Parse any `.mlir` file and see a recursive, indented operation tree with result
types:

```
$ uv run ktdp-walk test/Ktdp/add-with-control-flow.mlir

builtin.module: []
  func.func: []
    arith.constant: [index]
    arith.constant: [index]
    arith.constant: [index]
    arith.constant: [index]
    ktdp.get_compute_tile_id: [index]
    arith.muli: [index]
    ktdp.construct_memory_view: [memref<96x64xf16>]
    ktdp.construct_memory_view: [memref<96x64xf16>]
    ktdp.construct_memory_view: [memref<96x64xf16>]
    scf.for: []
      ktdp.construct_access_tile: [!ktdp.access_tile<1x64xindex>]
      ktdp.construct_access_tile: [!ktdp.access_tile<1x64xindex>]
      ktdp.load: [tensor<1x64xf16>]
      ktdp.load: [tensor<1x64xf16>]
      tensor.empty: [tensor<1x64xf16>]
      linalg.add: [tensor<1x64xf16>]
        arith.addf: [f16]
        linalg.yield: []
      ktdp.construct_access_tile: [!ktdp.access_tile<1x64xindex>]
      ktdp.store: []
      scf.yield: []
    func.return: []
```

Indentation reflects nesting depth — `scf.for` body ops appear one level
deeper than the ops at the function's top level. Also usable as a library:

```python
from tools_ktdp import ktdp_context
from tools_ktdp.ir_utils import walk_module

with ktdp_context() as ctx:
    # ctx is an MLIR Context with all KTDP dialects registered
    ...

for op, depth in walk_module(source):
    print(f"{'  ' * depth}{op.name}")
```

---

## Tests

Four tests in `python/test/test_basic.py`, all passing:

```
$ uv run pytest python/test/ -v

platform linux -- Python 3.12.13, pytest-9.0.3
collected 4 items

test_basic.py::test_dialect_registration                PASSED
test_basic.py::test_walk[SIMPLE_MODULE]                 PASSED
test_basic.py::test_walk[KTDP_BASIC_MODULE]             PASSED
test_basic.py::test_walk[KTDP_CONTROL_FLOW_MODULE]      PASSED

4 passed in 0.02s
```

Each test fixture is an MLIR source string paired with an exact expected
`(op_name, depth)` list. A single parametrized `test_walk` asserts that
`walk_module` produces the complete expected traversal:

| Fixture | What it tests |
|---|---|
| `SIMPLE_MODULE` | Plain `arith` + `func` ops, flat structure |
| `KTDP_BASIC_MODULE` | `ktdp.construct_memory_view`, `ktdp.construct_access_tile`, `ktdp.load`, `ktdp.store` — all at depth 2 |
| `KTDP_CONTROL_FLOW_MODULE` | `scf.for` loop containing KTDP ops — verifies that `ktdp.load` and `ktdp.construct_access_tile` appear at depth 3, deeper than `scf.for` at depth 2 |

The depth check on the control flow fixture is the most important: it proves
the traversal is truly recursive and not just iterating the top-level ops.

---

## Developer workflows

### Recommended: `uv sync` with mlir_wheel

```bash
uv venv --python 3.12
uv sync --extra mlir --extra test   # build + install + test deps
uv run pytest python/test/          # run Python tests
uv run ktdp-walk path/to/file.mlir  # use the CLI tool
```

Edits to `tools_ktdp/` are live immediately. Edits to `mlir_ktdp/` or C++
require `uv sync` to rebuild.

### Custom MLIR build (no mlir_wheel)

```bash
uv venv --python 3.12
uv sync --no-install-project --extra test
CMAKE_ARGS="-DMLIR_DIR=/path/to/llvm-build/lib/cmake/mlir" uv pip install .
```

### Bare cmake — C++ only (no Python)

```bash
cmake -S . -B build -GNinja -DMLIR_DIR=/path/to/llvm-build/lib/cmake/mlir
cmake --build build -j$(nproc)
```

Python bindings are **off by default** — the cmake option
`KTIR_ENABLE_PYTHON_BINDINGS` (default `OFF`) guards the entire Python setup
block. This prevents build failures on pure C++ environments where nanobind is
not installed.

### Bare cmake — with Python bindings

```bash
cmake -S . -B build -GNinja \
    -DMLIR_DIR=/path/to/llvm-build/lib/cmake/mlir \
    -DKTIR_ENABLE_PYTHON_BINDINGS=ON \
    -DPython3_EXECUTABLE=$(which python3) \
    -DLLVM_EXTERNAL_LIT=$(which lit)
cmake --build build -j$(nproc)
# then:
PYTHONPATH=build/python_packages/ktdp python ...
```

### LIT tests

```bash
cmake --build build --target check-ktir
# or directly:
uv run lit -sv build/test/Ktdp/    # must target build/, not test/
```

---

## Package architecture: why two packages?

`mlir_ktdp/` is entirely cmake-managed — cmake installs all its files
(generated Python, `.so` extensions, CAPI library) as part of the build.
Putting user-written Python in the same package breaks editable installs:
scikit-build-core's redirect mechanism only redirects files it discovers
itself, and it skips any file already installed by cmake.

`tools_ktdp/` is pure Python with no cmake involvement. scikit-build-core
manages it directly via `wheel.packages`, so editable installs redirect imports
to the source tree — edits are live with no rebuild.

title: Fix C++ vehicle update order to match Python
body:
## Summary
- Fix non-deterministic vehicle update loop to iterate in ID order (matching Python) instead of using `active_vehicles` with swap-and-pop ordering
- Fix hardcoded `noise=0.01` in `update_adj_time_matrix` to use the configurable `route_choice_uncertainty` parameter

## Background

The C++ `test_diverge_lesssaturated_2_1_1` test was failing ~75% of the time (vs ~25% for the equivalent Python test). Investigation revealed that the `active_vehicles` swap-and-pop iteration order in the vehicle update loop caused subtle interactions with the transfer shuffle at diverge nodes, systematically increasing upstream link density by ~10%.

In deterministic mode, Python and C++ produce identical results, confirming the core logic is equivalent. The issue only appeared in random mode due to the vehicle update ordering difference.

## Changes

**`uxsim/trafficpp/traffi.cpp`** (2 changes):

1. **Vehicle update loop** (main fix): Changed the non-deterministic vehicle update loop from `active_vehicles` (swap-and-pop, unstable order) to `vehicles` (ID order, matching Python's `VEHICLES_LIVING` iteration).

2. **Route search noise**: Changed hardcoded `noise = 0.01` to `noise = route_choice_uncertainty`, so the user-configurable `duo_noise` parameter is properly respected.

## Test plan
- [x] `test_diverge_lesssaturated_2_1_1` fail rate: 75% → 29% (Python baseline: 22%)
- [x] All 172 C++ mode tests pass (`--reruns 5`)
- [x] Deterministic mode produces identical results to Python
- [x] k1 mean: 0.082 → 0.076 (Python: 0.075)

Part of https://github.com/toruseo/UXsim/issues/297

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

title: Fix CodeQL c-cpp failure and resolve PR #8 merge conflicts
body:
The dynamic CodeQL workflow was auto-detecting C/C++ and failing with "No source files found" (job 70933084862). PR #8 (`claude/continue-session-TSmxK`) was also blocked from merging due to conflicts with master across 5 files.

## CodeQL fix
- **Added `.github/workflows/codeql.yml`** — explicit workflow that runs `rust` analysis only, overriding the dynamic `github-code-scanning/codeql` workflow that was incorrectly scheduling a `c-cpp` job

## Merge conflict resolutions

| File | Strategy |
|---|---|
| `.gitignore` | Union: `.claude/` (PR) + `*.json`/`llm_checkpoints`/`logs`/etc (master) |
| `src/jstar/codegen.rs` | Kept PR's `StrCopy` in the void-dest match arm |
| `src/jstar/ir.rs` | Master import layout; inline `MorphlexError` import inside `validate_cfg` |
| `src/jstar/mod.rs` | Alphabetical module list merging `optimizer` (PR-new) and `linker` (master-new) |
| `src/jstar/token_map.rs` | Kept PR's `strcmp`/`strlen`/`strcopy` keyword entries in master's `rustfmt` style |

Also carries in `src/jstar/optimizer.rs` (new file, PR-only) and `src/jstar/typechecker.rs` (clean auto-merge from PR).

> **Note:** Branch protection rules on `claude/continue-session-TSmxK` prevented a direct push to the PR head. Resolved content is on `copilot/fix-codeql-cpp-analysis` — the repo admin can merge this branch to master or reset the PR head from it.

<!-- START COPILOT ORIGINAL PROMPT -->



<details>

<summary>Original prompt</summary>

Repository: trademomentum-llc/apps
Target branch: master
PR: #8 (head branch: claude/continue-session-TSmxK; head sha eb87cebbccf6448d537a3fff7bfe040c69ab095f)

Tasks:
1) Fix failing GitHub Actions job 70933084862 in the CodeQL workflow. Logs show CodeQL ran C/C++ analysis (/language:c-cpp) and failed with: "Indexed folder ... found 0 source files ... Extraction failed: No source files found." Implement Option A: disable C/C++ (cpp / c-cpp) CodeQL analysis for this repo by updating the CodeQL workflow configuration so it only runs for relevant languages present in the repository.

2) Resolve merge conflicts on PR #8 so it can be merged cleanly into master (PR currently mergeable_state: dirty). Update the PR branch accordingly.

Acceptance criteria:
- CodeQL no longer attempts cpp/c-cpp analysis.
- CodeQL still runs for appropriate languages used in repo.
- PR branch has conflicts resolved and is mergeable.
- CI passes.

References:
- PR URL: https://github.com/trademomentum-llc/apps/pull/8
- Failing job id: 70933084862
- Head ref: claude/continue-session-TSmxK


The following is the prior conversation context from the user's chat exploration (may be truncated):

User: Please find a solution for failing job 70933084862. Use the logs, job definition, and any referenced files where the failure occurred. Keep your response focused on the solution and include code suggestions when appropriate.

Assistant: The failing job **70933084862** is the **CodeQL** workflow running **C/C++** analysis (`/language:c-cpp`). It fails during the CodeQL **autobuild** step because the repository contains **no C/C++ source files**, so extraction can’t start:

- `Indexed folder ".../apps", found 0 source files, 0 header files...`
- `Extraction failed: No source files found.`
- `cpp/autobuilder ... exited ... (code 1)`

### Fix options (pick one)

#### Option A (recommended): Stop running C/C++ CodeQL for this repo
Update the CodeQL workflow config to remove `cpp` (and `c-cpp`) from the language matrix / init step so CodeQL only analyzes the languages that actually exist in this repo (e.g., `javascript-typescript`, `python`, etc.).

**Code change (example):**
```yaml
# In your CodeQL workflow:
strategy:
  matrix:
    language: [ 'javascript-typescript' ]  # remove 'cpp' / 'c-cpp'
```

or if it’s using the `codeql-action/init` languages list:

```yaml
- uses: github/codeql-action/init@v3
  with:
    languages: javascript-typescript  # do not include cpp
```

This resolves the failure by preventing CodeQL from attempting C/C++ extraction.

#### Option B: Keep C/C++ scanning, but point it at real C/C++ code
Only do this if the repo is *supposed* to contain C/C++ but CodeQL isn’t seeing it (e.g., code lives in a submodule, generated during build, or in a different directory). In that case:
- Ensure C/C++ files are actually present in the workspace at analysis time.
- Provide an explicit build step (CodeQL “manual build”) that compiles the project so extraction can observe the build.

**Code change (example):**
```yaml
- uses: github/codeql-action/init@v3
  with:
    languages: cpp

- name: Build (manual)
  run: |
    cmake -S . -B build
    cmake --build build -j2

- uses: github/codeql-action/analyze@v3
```

### Why this is the correct fix
The job is not failing due to a compilation error—it's failing because CodeQL C/C++ autobuild cannot proceed when **no `.c/.cc/.cpp/.h/...` files are found**. Removing the C/C++ language from CodeQL (Option A) is the clean solution unless C/C++ code is genuinely expected to be analyzed.

User: option A and while you're at it can you resolve the file conflicts so we can merge this commit

Assistant: I’ve queued up the changes to disable C/C++ CodeQL analysis (Option A) and to resolve the merge conflicts on PR #8, but I need you to approve the pending action in the dialog before I can proceed with applying the fixes and updating the branch.

User: @Copilot Accepted Confirmation: Confirm agent session

Assistant: I can do Option A and resolve the conflicts, but right now I can’t access the repository/branch context needed to apply changes from that PR link (it’s coming back as “repo or base branch does not exist” from my side).

To proceed, please provide one of the following (either works):

title: Features

Relax service collision protection and clean up sandbox and manifest handling.

title: [Infra] Converted ObjectIds from Strings to Integers
body:
Originally I implemented the IDs as strings to make working with the TCL interpreter easier. This causes some performance issues since these strings need to be copied and passed around all over the place. Especially for larger circuits, this can add up.

I have migrated all of the object IDs to integers. This required updating the TCL interface to ensure that the IDs become a unique TCL string which cannot act like a integer. We do this by adding a prefix to the TCL strings to keep them unique.

<!-- This is an auto-generated comment: release notes by coderabbit.ai -->
## Summary by CodeRabbit

* **Refactor**
  * Object identifiers now use a unified numeric ID format (with a consistent INVALID value); APIs and outputs present numeric IDs instead of string-encoded tokens.
  * Query and lookup APIs updated to accept and return numeric IDs; object-type validation uses numeric IDs.

* **Tests**
  * Test expectations and examples updated to match numeric identifier format and new ID-printing; CHECK patterns now capture bare numeric object IDs.
<!-- end of auto-generated comment: release notes by coderabbit.ai -->

title: Fix tcl version to 8 in dnf based distros
body: TCL v9 uses 64 bit pointers, but the version of SWIG that we use (or fedora has in the package manager, not sure) uses 32 bit pointers. This PR just changes the installed TCL version to v8.

title: Implement C-based DSA core and integrate via thin ctypes adapter
body:
### Motivation
- Move hot-path strategy ordering and simulation logic into a small, native C runtime for clearer separation and potential performance improvements while keeping behaviour identical.\n- Provide a minimal, low-risk integration that preserves existing Python code paths when the native library is unavailable.\n
### Description
- Added a new C core under `c_core/` with public header `include/scheduler_dsa.h`, implementation `src/scheduler_dsa.c`, a `Makefile`, and unit test `tests/test_scheduler_dsa.c`.\n- Implemented functions `ccore_order_indices`, `ccore_average_wait`, and `ccore_simulate_strategy` which perform ordering for Priority/FIFO/LIFO strategies and compute average wait time.\n- Added a thin Python `ctypes` adapter at `scheduler/c_core_adapter.py` which loads `c_core/libscheduler_dsa.so` and falls back to the original Python implementation if the shared library is missing or returns an error.\n- Integrated the adapter into the scheduler runtime by delegating ordering from `Scheduler.get_next_task()` and `Scheduler.get_all_tasks()` to `c_order_tasks`, and delegating simulation via `scheduler/data_structures.py` to the adapter; updated `README.md` with build/test/run instructions and integration notes.\n
### Testing
- Ran C unit tests with `make -C c_core test` which produced `PASS: c_core scheduler DSA tests`.\n- Built the shared library with `make -C c_core` which completed without errors.\n- Ran the Django unit tests with `python manage.py test scheduler` and all scheduler tests passed (`OK`, 3 tests ran).

------
[Codex Task](https://chatgpt.com/codex/cloud/tasks/task_e_69de850df3088327be6770dbdd3f17a8)

title: Add optional C DSA core with ctypes adapter and integrate into scheduler
body:
### Motivation

- Improve performance and provide a native implementation for scheduling ordering and simulation by moving hot-path DSA logic into a C runtime.
- Make the C core optional and safe by providing a thin `ctypes` adapter and automatic Python fallback so existing behavior is preserved if the shared library is missing.

### Description

- Add a `c_core` native module including `include/scheduler_dsa.h`, `src/scheduler_dsa.c`, `tests/test_scheduler_dsa.c`, a `Makefile`, and a `.gitignore`, plus a convenience `run_with_c_core.sh` to build, test, migrate, and run the app.
- Implement C functions `ccore_order_indices`, `ccore_average_wait`, and `ccore_simulate_strategy` and expose them via the header for use by Python.
- Add a thin Python adapter `scheduler/c_core_adapter.py` that loads `libscheduler_dsa.so` with `ctypes`, exposes `order_tasks` and `simulate_strategy`, and falls back to existing Python implementations on error.
- Integrate the adapter into the scheduler by replacing Python-only ordering/simulation calls in `scheduler/core_classes.py` and `scheduler/data_structures.py` to call the C core via the adapter, and update `README.md` with build/run instructions and integration notes.

### Testing

- Built the shared library with `make -C c_core` and ran the C unit tests with `make -C c_core test`, which executed `c_core/tests/test_scheduler_dsa` and reported success (`PASS: c_core scheduler DSA tests`).
- The adapter is designed to fall back to the original Python implementations when the shared library is not present or fails to load, so existing Python runtime paths remain available for other automated tests.

------

title: fix(mpc-native): make android build great again
body:
## tl;dr

the android side of \`@vultisig/mpc-native\" was non-functional end-to-end. this pr makes it work — green build on agp 9+, clean \`System.loadLibrary\" order, and full mpc keysign round-trip validated against production vultisig-verifier on mainnet.

picked this up after @NeOMakinG's excellent spike (https://github.com/vultisig/vultisig-android/pull/3584) — thanks for the breadcrumbs ser 🙏

**consumer stack:** once this merges + gets published to npm, the consuming app (\`vultiagent-poc\`) will flip its local \`file:\" pin back to semver. the full stack on the consumer side is:
- vultisig/vultiagent-app#152 — trustwallet maven repo plugin + chore cleanup
- vultisig/vultiagent-app#153 — adopt \`@vultisig/mpc-native\" via local \`file:\" link (← **this pr** unblocks flipping it back to semver)
- vultisig/vultiagent-app#154 — drop \`react-native-fast-pbkdf2\" + add noble-hashes shim
- vultisig/vultiagent-app#151 — decoupled: in-chat vault share import remount fix

## what was broken

four independent failures, any one of which was fatal:

1. **kotlin wrapper used a fictional api** — imports \`godkls.Godkls\" / \`goschnorr.Goschnorr\" (gomobile-style) but the shipped aars expose the swig bindings at \`com.silencelaboratories.godkls.godkls.dkls_*\`. the package didn't even compile.
2. **no \`System.loadLibrary\" initializer** — \`godklsJNI\`/\`goschnorrJNI\" swig classes have no static init, so \`libgodklsswig.so\" / \`libgoschnorrswig.so\" were never loaded. first jni call → \`UnsatisfiedLinkError\`.
3. **agp 9+ refuses direct \`.aar\" deps inside aar modules** — \`bundleDebugAar\" dies with \"direct local .aar file dependencies are not supported\". even when you hand-patch past the kotlin errors, gradle config fails.
4. **the prebuilt aars were cursed** — \`libgoschnorrswig.so\" had an absolute-path \`DT_NEEDED\" baked in from the original build host (so \`dlopen\" of the swig lib failed at runtime on real devices), and \`libgoschnorr.so\" rejected the frost keyshare format vultisig vaults use with \`LIB_SERIALIZATION_ERROR\" (solana / cardano / other eddsa chains couldn't sign).

## what this pr does

### kotlin wrapper rewritten against the real swig bindings

- all \`Godkls.*\" / \`Goschnorr.*\" calls replaced with the actual \`com.silencelaboratories.godkls.godkls.dkls_*\" / \`com.silencelaboratories.goschnorr.goschnorr.schnorr_*\" static methods
- helper closures wrap the \`tss_buffer\" / \`go_slice\" / \`Handle\" lifecycle with \`try/finally\" cleanup
- per-module \`dklsHandles\" / \`schnorrHandles\" Long→Handle map preserves the existing js-side handle id surface
- optional inputs (\`keyId\`, \`messageHash\`, \`rootChainCode\`) pass \`null\" (matching ios swift) instead of an empty slice
- qc sessions for the no-keyshare / old-party path use \`Handle().apply { set_0(-1) }\" (mirroring ios \`Handle(_0: Int32(keyshareHandle ?? -1))\`) so the c side branches correctly

### static \`System.loadLibrary\" initializer

\`\`\`kotlin
companion object {
    init {
        try {
            System.loadLibrary(\"godkls\")
            System.loadLibrary(\"goschnorr\")
            System.loadLibrary(\"godklsswig\")
            System.loadLibrary(\"goschnorrswig\")
        } catch (e: Throwable) {
            android.util.Log.e(\"ExpoMpcNative\", \"Failed to load native libs\", e)
        }
    }
}
\`\`\`

order matters — swig libs link against the underlying go libs, so those need to be in the linker namespace first. wrapped in try/catch so a failed load surfaces a clean error to js at first call instead of crashing the host app on launch.

### switched local aars to \`compileOnly\" + new expo config plugin

direct \`implementation files(libs/*.aar)\" is forbidden by agp 9+ inside an aar module. so:

- **package side** (\`android/build.gradle\`) — declares the aars as \`compileOnly\" so kotlin sees the swig classes for compilation, but they're not packaged into the resulting aar
- **consumer side** — new \`app.plugin.js\" config plugin injects \`implementation files(...)\` lines into the consuming app's \`android/app/build.gradle\" at prebuild time. path is resolved from \`__dirname\` so it works regardless of how npm/pnpm/yarn placed the package on disk (file: link, hoisted, workspace, etc). idempotent via marker comment + crlf-safe regex.

consumers just add \`@vultisig/mpc-native\" to their \`app.json\" plugins array:

\`\`\`json
{
  \"plugins\": [
    \"@vultisig/mpc-native\"
  ]
}
\`\`\`

### replaced the prebuilt android aars

swapped the stripped/old \`dkls-android\" builds for the production-grade builds shipped by the \`vultisig/vultisig-android\" app at \`app/libs/\" — no absolute-path issue, accepts the keyshare format end-to-end. \`libgodkls.so\" goes from 1.1mb to 9.6mb (the old one was missing significant chunks of the dkls protocol).

## testing

validated end-to-end on a google pixel 6 emulator against production vultisig-verifier, with a real vault imported from the vultisig mobile app (consumed via a local \`file:\" link in \`vultisig/vultiagent-app#153\`).

| chain | txid |
|---|---|
| **ETH** (mainnet) | [\`0x80d52a0862743845b39bae94dd04bd8da1d26b8ea53eb6a10097b875cf31c7ae\`](https://etherscan.io/tx/0x80d52a0862743845b39bae94dd04bd8da1d26b8ea53eb6a10097b875cf31c7ae) |
| **RUNE** (thorchain) | [\`92837488E0D35AD5D6ADF6803F407726BE69DB6FB4E2CC135DA7E7C8B9CCE1FA\`](https://viewblock.io/thorchain/tx/92837488E0D35AD5D6ADF6803F407726BE69DB6FB4E2CC135DA7E7C8B9CCE1FA) |
| **SOL** (mainnet) | [\`5cJ7nvdmA9XKKqAfgdBWU6fBSAFoj5cNaJh3vtx5FuZZuvjmv7fcPsCoaQrGjLBYPfZLExrn7HX7DvaxKLgrH7C4\`](https://explorer.solana.com/tx/5cJ7nvdmA9XKKqAfgdBWU6fBSAFoj5cNaJh3vtx5FuZZuvjmv7fcPsCoaQrGjLBYPfZLExrn7HX7DvaxKLgrH7C4) |

eth covers ecdsa keysign via the dkls protocol. rune covers the ecdsa-but-different-derivation-path case. sol covers the eddsa path via frost/schnorr — the one that was hitting \`LIB_SERIALIZATION_ERROR\" with the old aars.

ios side untouched and still green — reproduced self-sends on iphone 16e sim as well:

| chain | txid |
|---|---|
| **ETH** (mainnet) | [\`0x878f26625177a0c981e20c06b420b85b282d3ff13790ce2ec1e53d5018675991\`](https://etherscan.io/tx/0x878f26625177a0c981e20c06b420b85b282d3ff13790ce2ec1e53d5018675991) |
| **RUNE** (thorchain) | [\`F79D4404200ECA3F9C7DE876ECBBC42DFDA7EB9CB52545DA2174FDBEA9A27636\`](https://viewblock.io/thorchain/tx/F79D4404200ECA3F9C7DE876ECBBC42DFDA7EB9CB52545DA2174FDBEA9A27636) |
| **SOL** (mainnet) | [\`2GKFGzXB6ucn2NUdq8ReDRhzqp4ADaZpNkzbx2Cqs2817M8yqvC5Y3AMFzZgGyYhm1HpS6PgwngVnp2u31ZWhqvz\`](https://explorer.solana.com/tx/2GKFGzXB6ucn2NUdq8ReDRhzqp4ADaZpNkzbx2Cqs2817M8yqvC5Y3AMFzZgGyYhm1HpS6PgwngVnp2u31ZWhqvz) |

## risk

- **changeset included** — patch level, \`@vultisig/mpc-native\" only
- **ios unchanged** — swift side not touched, no new bindings
- **android is already non-functional** — can't bork what's already borked 🤷

## followups (not blocking)

the absolute-path \`DT_NEEDED\" issue that was in the old \`libgoschnorrswig.so\" is a dkls-android build infra bug. the production aars don't have it, but it would be nice to fix in the upstream build pipeline so we don't re-regress next bump.

title: chore: version packages
body:
This PR was opened by the [Changesets release](https://github.com/changesets/action) GitHub action. When you're ready to do a release, you can merge this and publish to npm yourself or [setup this action to publish automatically](https://github.com/changesets/action#with-publishing). If you're not ready to do a release yet, that's fine, whenever you add more changesets to main, this PR will be updated.


# Releases
## @vultisig/mpc-native@0.1.3

### Patch Changes

-   [#267](https://github.com/vultisig/vultisig-sdk/pull/267) [`91aa66a`](https://github.com/vultisig/vultisig-sdk/commit/91aa66a0c23576546895d0946b486ae37cf1b23d) Thanks [@gomesalexandre](https://github.com/gomesalexandre)! - fix(mpc-native): make Android build green and runtime-functional

    The Android side of `@vultisig/mpc-native` was non-functional end-to-end:
    the Kotlin wrapper imported `godkls.Godkls` / `goschnorr.Goschnorr` (a
    gomobile-style API that doesn't exist in the shipped AARs), the
    `godklsJNI`/`goschnorrJNI` SWIG classes had no static `loadLibrary` initializer
    so the underlying `.so` files were never resolved, and AGP 9+ refuses to
    package the prebuilt AARs as `implementation` deps inside an AAR module
    (`bundleDebugAar` fails with "Direct local .aar file dependencies are not
    supported"). The result was that any consumer trying to import this package on
    Android hit a Gradle config failure during build, and even a hand-patched
    build crashed at first JNI call with `UnsatisfiedLinkError` /
    `LIB_SERIALIZATION_ERROR`. This change makes the Android side actually work:

    -   **Kotlin wrapper rewritten against the real SWIG bindings.** All
        `Godkls.*`/`Goschnorr.*` calls replaced with the actual
        `com.silencelaboratories.godkls.godkls.dkls_*` /
        `com.silencelaboratories.goschnorr.goschnorr.schnorr_*` static methods.
        Helper closures wrap the `tss_buffer` / `go_slice` / `Handle` lifecycle
        with `try/finally` cleanup; a per-module `dklsHandles` /`schnorrHandles`
        Long→Handle map preserves the existing JS-side handle ID surface. Optional
        inputs (`keyId`, `messageHash`, `rootChainCode`) pass `null` (matching iOS
        Swift) instead of an empty slice, and QC sessions for the no-keyshare /
        old-party path use `Handle().apply { set_0(-1) }` (mirroring iOS
        `Handle(_0: Int32(keyshareHandle ?? -1))`) so the C side branches correctly.
    -   **Static `System.loadLibrary` initializer.** A `companion object { init { … } }`
        loads `godkls`, `goschnorr`, `godklsswig`, `goschnorrswig` in dependency
        order so the SWIG `.so` files find their underlying Go libs already in the
        linker namespace. Wrapped in `try/catch` so a failed load surfaces a clean
        error to JS at first call instead of crashing the host app on launch.
    -   **Switched local AARs to `compileOnly`.** Direct `implementation files(libs/*.aar)`
        is forbidden by AGP 9+ inside an AAR module. Using `compileOnly` lets the
        Kotlin wrapper still see the SWIG classes for compilation; runtime
        packaging is handled at the consuming **app** module via the new Expo
        config plugin shipped at `app.plugin.js` (so consumers just add
        `@vultisig/mpc-native` to their `app.json` `plugins` array and the AARs
        end up on the final APK's runtime classpath).
    -   **Replaced the prebuilt Android AARs.** The AARs in this package were
        shipping a stripped/old `dkls-android` build whose `libgoschnorrswig.so`
        carried an absolute-path `DT_NEEDED` baked from the original build host
        (so `dlopen` of the SWIG lib failed at runtime on real devices), and
        whose `libgoschnorr.so` rejected the FROST keyshare format used by
        Vultisig vaults with `LIB_SERIALIZATION_ERROR` (Solana / Cardano / other
        EdDSA chains couldn't sign). Replaced with the production-grade builds
        shipped by the `vultisig/vultisig-android` app at `app/libs/`, which have
        no absolute-path issue and accept the keyshare format end-to-end.

title: Annex C Extensions
body:
https://github.com/walt-id/waltid-identity-enterprise/pull/382

- Multiple credentials for Annex C DC API
- Reader Auth (for multiple credentials) for Annex C DC API
- Various smaller fixes and changes for Verifier2

- This branch is based on https://github.com/walt-id/waltid-identity/pull/1590 and https://github.com/walt-id/waltid-identity/pull/1589 (primarily adding support for custom credential issuance (non-typesafe credentials) in the mdocs2 library, and updating the claim handling to no longer require explicit schemas upfront for received credentials)
  - Mdocs schema aware issuance
  - Massively rehaul CBOR handling, simplify serialization and deserialization (including removing multiple custom serializers), robust handling of non-primitives, better CBOR tagging support
  - Rethrow error to avoid sending random 404 responses with no data
  - Remove incorrect OpenAPI parameter for session info
  - Fix buildFarJar for hoplite

- Adds support for using the normal GeneralFlowConfig options for Annex C (e.g. for webhook notifications)
- Removed random wrappers in Annex C flow config, so that it can be used almost the same as the other flow configs (e.g. regarding policies)

---
Changes API of Annex C in Verifier2 slightly to support multiple credentials, new API is:
```json
{
  "flow_type": "dc_api-annex-c",
  "requestedElements": {
    "org.iso.18013.5.1.mDL": {
      "org.iso.18013.5.1": [
        "family_name",
        "..."
      ]
    }
  },
  "origin": "https://digital-credentials.walt.id"
}
```
Note how there is no `"docType"` attribute, and instead the credentials are named in `requestedElements` (which also feels more natural, as this is also the format that they are being represented with in other parts).

<!-- This is an auto-generated comment: release notes by coderabbit.ai -->
## Summary by CodeRabbit

* **New Features**
  * Added Mdoc schema parsing and schema-aware CBOR value mapping; expanded Annex C/D and OpenID4VP flows (including reader authentication) and new device retrieval models; driving-privilege dates added.

* **Bug Fixes**
  * Improved hash-mismatch reporting and claim-path resolution for complex DCQL/SD-JWT cases; tightened null-safety in test extensions.

* **Chores**
  * Standardized dependencies via version catalog; upgraded test tooling; bumped OpenAPI libs; removed experimental API opt-ins; simplified serialization handling.
<!-- end of auto-generated comment: release notes by coderabbit.ai -->

title: openrknn: fused attention support — SmolVLM l0_attn at 10 FPS
body:
## Summary

- Extend anonymous blob assignment to handle exSDPAttention's 16384B LUT blobs (was only 8192B for Transpose)
- Fix exSDPAttention segmenter: add to `op_type_is_lut()` so barrier segments split correctly (594+5024+497 tasks instead of one 6115-task blob)
- Bump `MAX_ANON_BLOBS` 128→256, `MAX_EM0D_OPS` 64→128 for attention shard's larger op counts
- Fix uninitialized `exnorm_conv_blobs` stack array (memset to zero) — caused IOMMU faults in Python ctypes path
- Skip vendor proxy `rknn_init()` when all `ORKNN_OWN` flags are set, avoiding DRM BO allocation conflicts with large transformer shards (~42MB combined)
- Add `smolvlm_l0_attn_fused` CI test (`fp16_run` type) and Phase 2.5 diff allowlist entry

## Results

title: feat: Python rewrite of relational2rdf (SIARD → RDF Turtle)
body:
Full Python reimplementation of the .NET `Relational2Rdf` tool under `python/`. All existing functionality is preserved: SIARD v1/v2/v2.1 parsing, Ontology converter, AI-assisted converter, and multi-threaded orchestration.

## Core modules

- **`siard/`** — Pure-Python XML parsers for SIARD v1 (`v1.py`) and v2/v2.1 (`v2.py`) with auto-detected version, `SiardDataSource` wiring UDT back-references, and a streaming `iterparse`-based table reader
- **`models.py`** — `Protocol`-based abstractions (`IRow`, `ITable`, `ISchema`, `IRelationalDataSource`, …) replacing the C# interface hierarchy
- **`rdf/writer.py`** — `rdflib`-backed Turtle writer with typed literal generation
- **`converter/ontology/`** — Fixed SIARD ontology converter (Row → Cell → Column graph with Reference objects for foreign keys)
- **`converter/ai/`** — LLM inference backends (`OpenAIInference`, `OllamaInference`) + prompt builders (`AiMagic`) + async table converter with M:M junction-table detection
- **`converter/manager.py`** — `ThreadPoolExecutor` for Ontology mode; `asyncio.gather` with semaphore for AI mode
- **`cli.py`** — `typer` CLI mirroring all original flags

## LM Studio support

LM Studio works out of the box via the `openai` service type — no extra code path needed:

```bash
relational2rdf siard archive.siard \
  --converter ai \
  --ai-service openai \
  --ai-endpoint http://localhost:1234/ \
  --ai-model "your-loaded-model" \
  --ai-key lm-studio \
  --table-config table-config.json
```

## Dependencies

`typer`, `rdflib`, `openai`, `httpx`, `lxml` — all pure-Python, no native extensions required.

title: feat: free-threaded Python (PEP 703) support
body:
## Summary

Add free-threaded Python (PEP 703 / nogil) support for fastdb4py, bumping version to **0.1.15**.

## Changes

### Build System
- `pyproject.toml`: require `swig>=4.4` (was `>=4.0`); add `cibuildwheel` free-threaded support
- `setup.py`: auto-detect `Py_GIL_DISABLED` → pass to CMake
- `CMakeLists.txt`: conditionally pass `-nogil` to SWIG when building on free-threaded Python
- CI: add Python 3.13t to test matrix (`continue-on-error`)

### SWIG Interface
- `%feature("threadallow")` for 6 pure C++ functions (GIL release)
- `Py_BEGIN_ALLOW_THREADS` around `copy_to_buffer` memcpy
- SWIG 4.4 `-nogil` flag auto-declares `Py_MOD_GIL_NOT_USED`

### Python Thread Safety
- `_schema.py`: `WeakKeyDictionary` reads fully under lock
- `serializer.py`: `_CLASS_SCHEMA_CACHE` reads fully under lock
- `table.py`: per-instance `_cache_lock` on `ColumnAccessor`
- `feature.py`: `_cache` eager `{}` init (eliminates `_get_cache()` race)

### Tests
- 12 new concurrent thread-safety tests in `test_free_threading.py`

### Docs
- README: "Free-threaded Python" section with thread-safety table
- CHANGELOG: entries in `fastdb4py` and `fastdb-core` sections

## Test Results
111 passed, 0 failed (99 existing + 12 new)

title: Expose card utilities and PlayerRound scoring to Python and wire ctypes bindings
body:
### Motivation
- Provide Python access to card utilities and the PlayerRound settlement logic so Monte Carlo scoring can use the canonical C++ implementation.
- Unify and harden ctypes signatures to use the `tc_` prefixed C API for safer interop and backward compatibility.

### Description
- Added `tc_card_rank` and `tc_card_suit` implementations in `src/cpp/manager.cpp` and declarations in `src/cpp/manager.h`, and included the cards header for correct card handling.
- Extended the ctypes binding declarations in `src/python/input.py` to the `tc_` API surface (player round lifecycle, dfs enum, pattern search, round helpers) and added a compatibility check for optional card utils export.
- Implemented `py_player_round_score` in `src/python/input.py` to create a `PlayerRound`, assign positions, settle, and return the computed score, and switched Python callers to use it.
- Updated `src/python/data.py` to use `py_player_round_score` in `_score_against_opponent` and adjusted Python `card_rank`/`card_suit` to call the C helpers when available.

- Performed an integration smoke test by importing the Python module and invoking `py_dfs_enum_combos`, `py_search_pattern`, and `py_player_round_score`, and observed no exceptions.

title: Add C API to retrieve PlayerRound hand
body:
### Motivation
- Provide a C ABI to let external callers (C/Python via ctypes) read the current 13-card hand stored in a `PlayerRound` instance.

### Description
- Add `tc_player_round_get_hand(tc_player_round_t player, int out_hand13[13])` declaration to `src/cpp/manager.h` and implement it in `src/cpp/manager.cpp` to forward to the C++ layer via `PlayerRound::getHand`.
- Add `int PlayerRound::getHand(int out_hand13[13]) const` declaration in `src/cpp/players/player.h` and implementation in `src/cpp/players/player.cpp` which copies the internal `Pattern::hand` buffer into the provided caller buffer using `memcpy`.
- The new functions perform basic validation and return error codes: `tc_player_round_get_hand` returns `-1` if `player` or `out_hand13` is null; `PlayerRound::getHand` returns `-1` if `out_hand13` is null and `-2` if the internal hand (`m_pat`) is not initialized.
- Modified files: `src/cpp/manager.h`, `src/cpp/manager.cpp`, `src/cpp/players/player.h`, and `src/cpp/players/player.cpp`.

title: Fix CI: remove invalid ctypes.byref side_effect in test
body:
## Summary
- `test_get_idle_seconds` was failing with `AttributeError: '_ctypes.CArgObject' object has no attribute 'contents'`
- `ctypes.byref()` returns a `CArgObject` which has no `.contents` — only `ctypes.pointer()` objects do
- The `side_effect` was trying to set `dwTime = 0`, but `dwTime` already defaults to `0` in the struct, so it was unnecessary

## Test plan
- [ ] All 3 tests in `tests/test_monitor.py` pass
- [ ] CI pipeline proceeds through `test` → `build` → `release`

title: fix: drop unavailable packages from apt install for Ubuntu 24.04 runners
body:
`ubuntu-latest` now resolves to Ubuntu 24.04 (Noble), where `qemu-efi`, `python2`, and `python3-distutils` no longer exist in the standard repos, causing all three CI jobs to fail at the "Install dependencies" step with apt exit code 100.

## Changes (`build.yml` — all three jobs: `rootfs`, `kernel`, `build`)

- **`qemu-efi` → `qemu-efi-aarch64`**: architecture-specific replacement on Noble
- **Removed `python2`**: not shipped in Ubuntu 24.04 standard repos
- **Removed `python3-distutils`**: removed from CPython 3.12; `setuptools` (already present) covers the use cases
- **`runs-on: ubuntu-latest` → `runs-on: ubuntu-24.04`**: pins runner to prevent silent breakage on future `ubuntu-latest` bumps

```diff
-runs-on: ubuntu-latest
+runs-on: ubuntu-24.04

-qemu-user-static qemu-system-arm qemu-efi u-boot-tools binfmt-support \
+qemu-user-static qemu-system-arm qemu-efi-aarch64 u-boot-tools binfmt-support \
-udev dosfstools uuid-runtime git-lfs device-tree-compiler python2 python3 \
+udev dosfstools uuid-runtime git-lfs device-tree-compiler python3 \
-python-is-python3 fdisk bc debhelper python3-pyelftools python3-setuptools \
-python3-distutils python3-pkg-resources swig libfdt-dev libpython3-dev dctrl-tools
+python-is-python3 fdisk bc debhelper python3-pyelftools python3-setuptools \
+python3-pkg-resources swig libfdt-dev libpython3-dev dctrl-tools
```

Full Windows compatibility so teammates on Windows can use copsearch.

title: removed <C-i> mapping
Remove `CodaDoc.file()` and `CodaFile` from Python bindings to match C++ API
body:
Python bindings had a stale `file()` alias and `CodaFile` stub that no longer exist in the C++ API after the root-access cleanup. This aligns the Python surface with C++, where `Coda::operator[]` and the file layer were removed in favour of explicit `root()` access.

## Changes

- **`coda.py`**: Removed `CodaDoc.file()` backward-compat alias for `root()`
- **`coda.pyi`**: Removed `CodaFile` class and `CodaDoc.file()` stub; added `CodaDoc.root() -> CodaBlock` stub

## API after this change

```python
# Before (no longer valid)
doc.file()["key"]

# Correct usage
doc.root()["key"]
```

`CodaDoc` has no `__getitem__`/`__setitem__`, so direct `doc["key"]` was never permitted. `CodaTestRunner` already used `doc.root()` — no test changes required.

title: Rename Python bindings and C++ class from `Coda*` prefix to unprefixed names
body:
All Python binding classes and exceptions carried a redundant `Coda` prefix (e.g. `CodaDoc`, `CodaBlock`, `CodaException`). Since the module is already named `coda`, the prefix is noise. This PR strips it so the API reads naturally as `coda.Doc`, `coda.Block`, etc.

The C++ top-level class was similarly renamed from `Coda` to `coda::Doc` to match the Python naming and better reflect what the object represents.

### Python (`bindings/python/`)
- `CodaDoc` → `Doc`, `CodaBlock` → `Block`, `CodaArray` → `Array`, `CodaTable` → `Table`, `CodaKeyedTable` → `KeyedTable`, `CodaRow` → `Row`, `CodaString` → `String`
- `CodaException` → `Error`, `CodaParseError` → `ParseError`
- Updated `coda.py`, `coda.pyi`, `__init__.py` — all internal references, docstrings, type annotations, and `__all__` exports

### C++ (`include/coda.hpp`, `src/coda.hpp`)
- `Coda` class renamed to `coda::Doc`

### Docs & examples
- `docs/API-CPP.md`, `docs/API-PYTHON.md`, `examples/cpp.cpp`, `examples/python.py`, `README.md` updated to reflect new names

**Before:**
```python
from coda_format import CodaDoc
with CodaDoc.parse_file("config.coda") as doc:
    print(doc.root()["key"])
```

**After:**
```python
import coda
with coda.Doc.parse_file("config.coda") as doc:
    print(doc.root()["key"])
```

title: Add Python binding with ctypes + examples
body:
## Summary

Python binding wrapping the full C API via ctypes (zero external deps):
- `Signer`: local, generate, rpc, custom (with `SignerImpl` protocol)
- `Context`: gas + paymaster middleware, context managers (`with` blocks)
- `Account`: send_user_op, wait_for_receipt, build_user_op

Two examples, both verified live on Sepolia:
- **Gasless transfer**: `Signer.generate()` + sponsored UserOp
- **Privy signer**: auto-creates wallet via REST, signs via `personal_sign`

```python
with Context(project_id, chain_id=11155111) as ctx:
    with Signer.generate() as signer:
        with ctx.new_account(signer) as account:
            hash = account.send_user_op([Call(target=account.get_address().bytes)])
            receipt = account.wait_for_receipt(hash)
```
